title: White House proposal could add political litmus test to research grants
author: Jolynda Wang
text: A recent proposal by the White House could add a political litmus test to the federal government’s evaluation of research proposals and allocation of grants. On May 29, the White House’s Office of Management and Budget proposed a rule to federal grant regulations that would require political appointees to review grant awards and ensure they “demonstrably advance the President’s policy priorities.” The sprawling proposal, if implemented, would prohibit federal funding for projects deemed to promote “anti-American values,” as well as diversity, equity and inclusion initiatives and “gender ideology” that denies that there are two immutable sexes within humans. As written, “the proposed changes risk undoing the highly-effective, merit-based, peer-reviewed systems that have allowed researchers to develop countless breakthroughs,” University Vice Provost for Research Michael Crair wrote in an email to the News, adding that both Yale and the federal government share a commitment to transparency and efficiency in their relationship. “Yale is actively contributing to conversations and advocating for policies that strengthen the U.S. research funding model that is admired and emulated around the world and has, for many decades, produced discoveries that Americans benefit from every day,” Crair also wrote. The federal government is currently holding a 45-day comment period during which the public can review the proposed rules and submit feedback. As of June 8, the proposed rule has received 11,135 comments . Crair wrote that the Association of Public and Land-Grant Universities, the Council on Governmental Relations and the American Association of Universities are “already working to provide feedback.” Yale President Maurie McInnis sits on the board of the American Association of Universities and said in a March interview that she has collaborated with the organization to advocate for a more transparent federal research funding model in recent lobbying efforts . Three Yale professors criticized the proposal in interviews with the News, describing it as a threat to scientific independence and warning that it may slow life-changing scientific progress. “If science can only ask politically comfortable questions, it stops serving the public,” Frederick Altice, a professor of medicine and epidemiology at the Yale School of Medicine, wrote in an email to the News. He noted that requiring approval from presidential appointees could delay funding decisions and “create uncertainty for trainees, staff, and community partners.” Thomas Pollard, a now-retired Sterling professor of molecular, cellular and developmental biology, said in a Zoom interview that he expected these changes, citing President Donald Trump’s cancellation of several grants last year. “I've always said that getting an NIH grant was about as competitive as making a team in the National Football League. The money is very hard to get, and this is going to make it even more difficult to get funds to do the work,” Pollard said. Pollard said the proposal is “horrible” and “immoral,” noting that the changes could push back funding for research on developing cures and treatments. “This is a damn shame because these funds have created endless advances in technology and endless advances in clinical medicine,” Pollard said. Molecular, cellular and developmental biology professor Valerie Horsley also wrote in an email to the News that inserting political review into merit-based funding could “undermine scientific independence and slow research progress.” Horsley added that she has been funded by the NIH since she was a graduate student and that she thinks the proposal could complicate faculty recruitment and jeopardize support for graduate students. “These changes would make the process more unreliable and reduce confidence in the system,” Horsley wrote. “It would also make it harder for Universities to support research because the funding would be unpredictable.” Scott Delaney, a former research scientist at Harvard and co-creator of Grant Witness, a website that tracks federal research grant disruptions, described the proposal as an unprecedented expansion of executive control over the research funding process. He added that the changes could make it easier for future administrations to delay, deny or terminate grants to universities like Yale. “This is the biggest attack on the American scientific enterprise in my lifetime,” Delaney said in a Zoom interview. “We cannot underestimate the impact, the transformative impact that this regulation could have if it goes into effect as it’s proposed.” Continued hurdles, ‘worrisome’ delays Last month’s proposal follows a turbulent period for federal research funding at Yale under Trump’s second term. Approximately 120 grants tied to Yale researchers were affected by federal actions in 2025, including around 50 awards that were terminated and later reinstated. In March, some researchers still receiving federal funding told the News that they were facing complications due to recent policy changes, such as longer application and approval processes for grants. On April 9, the United States National Science Foundation’s Office of Award Management placed a “Future Awards to Organization on Hold” designation on Yale, Harvard, Princeton and Duke, impacting 33 research proposals associated with the universities, according to reporting by the scientific journal Nature . The day after the publication of Nature’s reporting, on May 28, the foundation removed the hold from its database for Yale, Harvard and Duke, with several grants released to Harvard and Duke following the change, Nature stated in an update to the original article. The University has not yet received formal notice from the federal government indicating that new NSF grants were placed on hold, according to Crair. However, he noted that Yale has continued to observe delays in grants being awarded. “What we have observed, and continue to monitor, is that some new grants recommended for funding through standard NSF processes, including peer review, have not been funded in the timeline we would typically expect,” he wrote in his June 4 email. “However, I am glad to report that Yale has received a number of new NSF grants in recent days.” “Allowing agencies to withhold funds, or to pick winners and losers outside of the process outlined in law, is a problematic erosion of the fundamental principle in our Constitution that Congress has the power of the purse,” Cerin Lindgrensavage ’06, a counsel for Protect Democracy, a nonpartisan nonprofit organization that advocates for stronger democracy in the United States, said in a Zoom interview. “The law is what should govern how these funds are used and not any single opinion of a bureaucrat at the OMB or NSF.” Altice wrote that the recent stalling of research proposals adds to his concern about political influence on research funding. The affected proposals have already passed independent peer review and agency-level reviews, he wrote, and they seem to have been delayed only at the award stage. “When funding is held at that stage, outside the normal scientific workflow, it raises serious questions about whether decisions are being influenced by factors other than scientific merit,” Altice wrote. “It is worrisome.” The OMB’s proposal will receive public comments on its website until July 13.

title: New Haven is the only city fighting Trump’s ‘slush’ fund in court. Why?
author: Adele Haeg
text: Since President Donald Trump moved back into the White House, New Haven’s new legal counsel has been busy. The city has repeatedly sued the federal government over the past year and a half, joining six lawsuits and 24 other amicus briefs against the Trump administration. New Haven has argued against funding freezes for environmental initiatives and SNAP benefits , and supported Minnesota in its lawsuit over immigration enforcement in the state. Mayor Justin Elicker has praised a “growing nationwide coalition of local governments” who have taken similar action — but with its latest suit, New Haven stands apart. Last month, the city was the only one to join a high-profile lawsuit seeking to block the president’s $1.8 billion “anti-weaponization fund” in a Virginia district court, and none of the other suits that have been filed in opposition to the fund have municipal plaintiffs. New Haven joined the lawsuit a month after the Trump administration sued the city for its immigration “sanctuary policies” — in that case, New Haven and Connecticut officials were the only defendants. Soon after filing the lawsuit opposing Trump’s fund, New Haven and its fellow plaintiffs — a fired Jan. 6 prosecutor, a California professor formerly charged with assaulting a federal officer, the National Abortion Federation and a government watchdog group — requested emergency relief to block the federal government from disbursing money through the program. The next day, Judge Leonie Brinkema granted a temporary injunction in the case until June 12, and last Friday, the Department of Justice told Brinkema that the fund was “not going forward.” As of Tuesday, the future of the slush fund remains unclear . Allie Jacobs, the corporation counsel for New Haven, wrote in an email to the News that the city thinks its strategy in the latest lawsuit has been successful so far. However, it is not clear how much of an impact New Haven’s involvement in the suit has had on whether the fund will ultimately move forward. The other lawsuits against the “slush" fund are ongoing, and the program has come under bipartisan fire in Congress. The mayor said he is not pursuing legal action for the purpose of political visibility. “If there were a case that was simply symbolic, I’m not sure we would join,” Elicker said in a phone interview with the News. “It’s about us making sure that we have impacts that help New Haven residents.” “The punchline for a lot of Democratic elected officials is that it may both be the right political move and the right move for ‘small-d democracy’” to sue the Trump administration, Dan Barrett, legal director of the Connecticut American Civil Liberties Union, said in a phone interview with the News. Maybe, he added, “people will look back and say, ‘well, at least somebody tried to fight back.’” How did New Haven get involved in the lawsuit? In May, lawyers from the nonprofit Democracy Forward — which is representing the plaintiffs in the suit — approached Elicker and asked if New Haven would join in suing the Trump administration for setting up their “Anti-Weaponization Fund.” The fund — a result of a settlement with the Trump administration in a legal dispute over his tax returns — was set up to compensate allies of the Republican Party who “suffered weaponization” by the DOJ under former President Joe Biden. Along with the three other plaintiffs, Democracy Forward planned to prosecute the Trump administration for misuse of taxpayer funds and “creating a politically discriminatory process ” in their disbursement, alleging that it would reward only Republicans who claimed weaponization. According to Jacobs, New Haven had “a preexisting relationship with Democracy Forward,” which previously represented plaintiffs suing the Trump administration for blocking SNAP benefits. New Haven was one of eight municipalities who participated in that lawsuit. Elicker said that after hearing from Democracy Forward, he considered his options in conversations with legal counsel, recognizing that there were “pros and cons” of suing. Democracy Forward would cover legal fees, and Elicker said that the suit aligned with “our values as a city.” “We’re defending our residents from what is in my view a real misuse of federal funds and taxpayer dollars,” the mayor added. Elicker said that one risk was that the lawsuit might “attract undue attention” from the federal government with New Haven as the only municipal plaintiff. But for Elicker, that did not mean “New Haven should not join these lawsuits but that more municipalities should join these lawsuits.” When lawyers are compiling lawsuits and “trying to get as many viable plaintiffs as you can find, you notice that each one checks a different box,” Barrett said, speculating that New Haven was brought into the suit because it had previously been sued for its “sanctuary policies.” He said it was not surprising New Haven has developed a relationship with Democracy Forward, adding that it is “common” for municipalities to collaborate with similar nonprofits. “There are certainly situations in which municipalities really have to be on the front lines litigating, particularly with some grant programs that municipalities will directly interact with the federal government over,” Barrett said. “In other cases, it’s less clear, but this probably serves a democratic purpose.” New Haven as an “anti-monarchist” city Apart from the city’s adherence to its “sanctuary policies” and other municipal priorities and systems that Elicker said the federal administration is “dismantling,” Jacobs suggested that it is in New Haven’s character as a city to take legal action in the way that it has been. It is “unusual” for cities to sue the federal government, Jacobs said — especially one like New Haven, with only 135,000 residents. “It takes courage for a smaller municipality to stand up to a much larger, much more well-resourced federal government,” she wrote. Jacobs said she recognized legal and historical imperatives for legal action against the Trump administration. “New Haven has a deep history of anti-monarchist principles, dating back to the 1600s and 1700s when the people of New Haven rebelled against the British Crown,” Jacobs wrote, “and we have been fighting against tyranny and injustice ever since.” City Historian Michael Morand ’87 DIV ’93 likened the city’s opposition to the chief executive to the actions of Roger Sherman, a founding father and the first mayor of New Haven. Sherman believed that a “person or persons ought to be appointed by and accountable to the Legislature only, which was the depository of the supreme will of the Society,” Morand wrote, quoting James Madison. “I’ve gotten a lot of positive feedback from residents about the stance that New Haven has taken at this moment,” Elicker said. The city’s position in the case reflects the discontent with Trump administration policies that have drawn thousands of New Haven residents to protests this year. Though there are “other communities that might not be as supportive to their leadership” taking a confrontational stance, Elicker said he feels he has New Haven’s support. “ We have a good team and we’ll continue to respond to opportunities that come in the future,” Elicker said. “Things are so volatile and unpredictable right now, it’s hard to come up with a specific plan.” Elicker was first sworn into office in January 2020.

title: Former Yale golfer James Nicholas ’19 returns to the U.S. Open
author: Brody Gilkison
text: PURCHASE, New York — Before last year, no Yale golfer had played in the U.S. Open since Bob Heintz ’92 in 1999. However, James Nicholas ’19 secured a spot in the 125th iteration of the tournament in 2025 — and this Monday, he qualified yet again for the 126th in Shinnecock Hills. Nicholas earned his spot during “Golf’s Longest Day,” the day when the United States Golf Association hosts 10 of its 13 U.S. Open final qualifying events. For those not already exempt into the U.S. Open, qualifying typically comes in the form of an 18-hole local qualifier in early May and a 36-hole final qualifying site a week before the championship begins. “It feels great after such a long day,” Nicholas said in an interview with the News on Monday. He added that he has experienced “a lot of ups and downs this season,” but hopes that having qualified for the tournament will help him “finish the season strong.” Nicholas, a one-time winner on the Korn Ferry Tour — the PGA’s developmental tour — sits at 15th place on the tour’s point list and inside the Top 20 mark needed to earn his PGA Tour card for next year. Just days after playing in the BMW Charity Pro-Am in South Carolina, Nicholas flew to New York to play 36 holes in the hopes of earning his way into his second consecutive U.S. Open. The New York site, which was the only site hosted at two separate clubs, was played at Century Country Club and the Golf Club of Purchase, both in Purchase, New York. Nicholas teed up first at the Golf Club of Purchase and took advantage of scorable conditions with a cool –4 round of 68 to put himself in second place after 18 holes. While he was in a good position halfway through the event, Nicholas needed to remain in the top four of a strong field in order to qualify. Among others competing at his site were four-time All-American Ben James of Virginia, as well as seasoned PGA Tour veterans Max Greyserman and Kevin Roy. The second round proved to be much more difficult, as the rock-hard greens and thick rough required accuracy and a strong mental game. Through 27 holes, Nicholas was still two shots clear of the cut line but had to finish strong in order to punch his ticket to the national championship. Standing on the 17th tee, Nicholas was a shot clear of the cutline at –3, leading amateur Christian Cavaliere by one stroke and two strokes ahead of Matt Jones, a two-time PGA Tour winner himself. However, Nicholas misjudged a greenside shot on the 17th, causing him to drop a shot before paring the final hole to post rounds of 68–72 and sit in a tie for the final spot at two-under. Luckily for Nicholas, Cavaliere made a costly mistake on his final hole of the day, a short, scorable par-4 hole. After Cavaliere double-bogeyed the hole, Nicholas qualified for the U.S. Open without needing to play any extra holes. Nicholas will be playing in this week’s Korn Ferry Tour event in Amarillo, Texas, before heading back to his home state to play at Shinnecock Hills next week.

title: Lincoln Center president Mariko Silver ’99 to join Yale Corporation
author: Aria Lynn-Skov
text: Yale graduates elected performing arts executive Mariko Silver ’99 to the Yale Corporation, alumni board of governors chair Jennifer Ebisemiju Madar ’88 announced in a Monday email to voters. Silver will begin her six-year term as trustee on July 1. Silver, who has been president and CEO of the Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts since 2024, prevailed over Michael Singer ’95 GRD ’99 ’00 MED ’02 to replace outgoing trustee Carlos Moreno ’70. According to a Yale alumni fellow election brochure , Silver raised the largest annual funding total in the Lincoln Center’s history during her first year as its president. She was previously president and CEO of the Henry Luce Foundation, a grantmaking nonprofit, and was also president of Bennington College in Vermont. “We are in a moment when people are asking questions about the value of higher education and the value of publicly funded research, but I believe they’ve never been more important,” Silver said in a video included in the official election materials . The Yale Corporation, or board of trustees, includes 10 successor trustees — appointed by the current board — and six alumni fellows. Alumni fellows serve staggered six-year terms, and one is elected each spring. Moreno was elected to the role in 2020 over Maurie McInnis, who was later appointed to serve as a successor trustee from 2022 to 2024 before assuming her current role as University president. This year, eligible voters — including Yale College alumni who graduated at least five years ago and all other University graduates and honorary degree recipients — were able to vote to elect Singer or Silver until May 17. According to the elections brochure , candidates do not traditionally campaign for the alumni fellow role, standing for election “solely based on the information included in the official election materials.” Silver could not be reached for comment as of Monday evening. As president of Bennington College from 2013 to 2019, she brought in the most diverse class in the school’s history, expanded financial aid and tripled the school’s endowment to $51 million, according to Bennington’s website. In her alumni fellow candidacy video, Silver said that her interactions with faculty were core to her experience as a student, adding that the curiosity and dedication of those she encountered on campus as an undergraduate have carried her through her career and her life. “That sense of curiosity that leads to innovation, that leads to new research, that leads to new ways of thinking, and also that sense of stewardship — gathering what we know, marshalling it for good purposes and sharing it with others — all of those things are part and parcel of the Yale mission and it would be an honor to help to bring them forward,” she said. After graduating, Silver worked under the president of Arizona State University and as policy adviser to Arizona Governor Janet Napolitano. From 2009 to 2011, she served the Department of Homeland Security’s Office of International Affairs under the Obama administration. Candidates for the alumni fellow position are currently chosen by the Alumni Fellow Nominating Committee, which reviews candidates nominated by Yale graduates. Up until the 2021 election, candidates could put themselves on the ballot by gathering signatures from 3 percent of eligible voters. The decision to eliminate the petitioning process was unpopular among alumni, and Victor Ashe ’67 and Donald Glascoff Jr. ’67 filed a lawsuit challenging the legality of the change in 2022. The Connecticut Supreme Court declined to hear the lawsuit in March, after it was unsuccessfully argued in the Hartford district court and Connecticut Appellate Court. Singer, who was Silver’s opponent in this year’s alumni fellow election, is a physician-scientist and entrepreneur who serves on the Yale For Humanity Campaign Committee . In his candidacy video , Singer said the campaign serves as a reminder “that we’re a unified body in New Haven but also a global university ultimately looking to serve the world.” The For Humanity fundraising campaign — which describes itself as “guided by our mission to improve the world now and for future generations — launched in 2021 with the goal of raising $7 billion. It is set to conclude this month. In March, McInnis announced Derek Chang ’89 and Philippe Costeletos ’87 as Yale’s newest successor trustees. The two will begin in their roles alongside Silver in July. The Yale Corporation will meet next on June 6 for the final of five meetings this academic year, according to a University webpage .

title: In faculty group survey, professors report self-censorship under Trump
author: Aria Lynn-Skov
text: Faculty members described an environment of decreasing academic freedom and instances of self-censorship under President Donald Trump’s second term in a recent report by Yale’s chapter of the American Association of University Professors. Yale AAUP released its 26-page report on Tuesday, three months after the group launched a campaign calling on Yale to add explicit guarantees of academic freedom to the faculty handbook. The Tuesday report’s recommendations additionally include increased protections for non-tenured faculty, the consideration of a University-wide faculty senate beyond advisory roles and a faculty-elected committee to hear and decide upon academic freedom complaints. “There’s been a lot of talk about the decline in public trust in higher education, but trust on campus is in jeopardy right now too,” Amy Kapczynski LAW ’03, a Yale Law School professor and member of the AAUP executive committee, said in a phone interview. “And without trust that we won’t be disciplined or harassed for our speech, we can’t offer the best education to our students or do our best research or best contribute to public debates.” The group’s survey received 177 faculty respondents from Yale College and “nearly every professional school.” According to the report, 68.4 percent of respondents indicated that their academic freedom has decreased either somewhat or a great deal since January 2025, when Trump began his second term. Yale has 5,842 faculty members, according to its website . When asked in a phone interview about the challenges of conducting the survey, Daniel HoSang, an American Studies professor and the president of Yale’s AAUP chapter, cited “apprehension among faculty” to share their experiences and a lack of access to a centralized list of faculty. 56.3 percent of respondents also reported being “extremely concerned” or “somewhat concerned” that “students might file anonymous complaints about them,” while 44.6 percent reported concerns about being “disciplined by the university for their public-facing speech and engagement.” The survey also asked respondents about actions — related to teaching, research and public engagement — they may have taken since January 2025 “in response to the Trump administration’s higher education directives and actions and in light of the general climate of attacks on higher education.” 32.2 percent of respondents said they “avoided controversial topics in class or lectures,” and 47.5 percent said they “stopped or avoided posting on social media about controversial topics,” per the report. “The conditions in higher ed have really shifted in the last couple of years, and this is important evidence about how faculty are reacting at one institution,” HoSang said of the report. Since Trump’s inauguration, Yale has avoided direct funding cuts from the federal government that peer institutions, including Harvard and Columbia, have faced. In meetings with professors last spring, Yale President Maurie McInnis indicated that she was refraining from public commentary to avoid the federal government’s targeting, according to professors who attended the meetings. McInnis never confirmed these discussions, citing the private nature of meetings. In October 2024, a month before Trump was elected to his second term, McInnis adopted the recommendations of the Committee on Institutional Voice that University leaders refrain from making public statements on significant issues, except in rare cases. Kapczynski said that the audience for the University’s “lay-low strategy” seems to be the Trump administration and those outside of Yale, adding that when universities neither comment publicly on what’s happening in their “broader environment” nor take action to clarify their “commitment to academic freedom,” it “speaks volumes.” “The reality is that action is important in this context because it’s what conveys the sincerity of our commitment to academic freedom,” she said. Tuesday’s report also reads that “While some reporting has taken the view that Yale has successfully defended the institution and its finances from the present administration, our data show that all is not well at Yale.” In March, the office of Provost Scott Strobel established the Committee on the Principles of Academic Freedom, which is set to release a statement that “articulates the principles that guide the academic freedom of Yale faculty,” according to the Provost’s website . Strobel did not respond to questions about whether the committee’s formation was a reaction to the AAUP chapter’s advocacy when asked by the News in March. When asked if the University plans to address the concerns raised and changes recommended in the report, Yale spokesperson Tina Posterli pointed to the existing language about academic freedom in the faculty handbook, but did not directly address the AAUP chapter’s report. “Academic freedom is a crucial, central element of Yale’s purpose and mission. This tenet is explicitly recognized in Yale’s Faculty Handbook, which states that ‘academic freedom and the spirit of free inquiry are fundamental to Yale’s mission,’ and includes procedural safeguards for faculty who believe their academic freedom has been improperly restrained,” Posterli wrote in an email to the News. She also emphasized the work of the Provost’s Committee on the Principles of Academic Freedom, adding that the “committee has begun its work, including distribution of a survey on academic freedom to all members of the faculty and others in teaching and research,” and that it will produce a report by the end of the fall semester. HoSang said that the faculty handbook is updated annually “at the Provost’s discretion” and that introducing additional language into the faculty handbook is costless, can be “easily done” over the summer by the Strobel and McInnis and would not override the authority of the Provost’s committee on academic freedom “because that’s focused on what the statement should be.” He also pointed to the speed at which Yale narrowed its mission statement following a recommendation by the Committee on Trust in Higher Education — two weeks after the committee released its report. “When President McInnis wanted to change the mission statement, we took note of how quickly it was done,” HoSang said. “She felt there was urgency, there wasn’t a big campus-wide review, there was a recommendation from one committee, but no other campus constituents were engaged, and it was done.” “We would hope that they could act on this with the same urgency that President McInnis acted on changing the mission statement at her discretion,” HoSang said. “We assume that they’ll take seriously that many dozens of their colleagues, their faculties as well, have reported that they don’t feel like they can teach and research freely, and that they’ll want to address that problem.” The AAUP sent McInnis and Strobel the report once it was released but has not yet met with members of the administration since the report’s release, according to HoSang. “We’re looking forward to their response, given the widespread endorsement of the campus,” HoSang said, referring to the student support the AAUP’s recent campaign for academic freedom has received. Last month, the Yale College Council Senate and the Graduate and Professional Student Senate passed a joint resolution backing Yale AAUP’s academic freedom demands. Following the Tuesday release of Yale AAUP’s report, leaders from both student organizations emphasized the importance of protecting academic freedom. “Academic freedom is fragile,” Sam Haddad LAW ’26, last year’s president of the Graduate and Professional School Student Senate, wrote in an email to the News. “The Federal Administration has proved that over the past few months. If our university’s focus is truly on the production and dissemination of knowledge, then Administrators must act on this report’s concerning findings.” YCC President Alex William Chen ’28 wrote in an email to the News that the report “emphasizes the unmistakable need for students and faculty to jointly advocate for our shared interests: students can’t learn if teachers don’t feel free to teach.” “The Yale education students deserve is achieved through, and only through, critically engaging with an unfettered range of teaching and thought --- academic self-censorship robs students of exactly that,” he wrote. Yale’s chapter of the AAUP was established in November 2024.

title: Yale fills four out of six open residential college dean positions
author: Jolynda Wang
text: Yale College announced new appointments to four out of six open residential college dean positions. Yale College Dean Pericles Lewis announced on Tuesday the appointments of Ksenia Sidorenko GRD ’14 ’23 as the dean of Berkeley College, Katherine Leach as the dean of Davenport College, Matthew Newman ’08 as the dean of Silliman College and David Gardner as the dean of Timothy Dwight College. The new deans are set to begin their tenures in July. The dean positions for Branford and Grace Hopper colleges still remain open. The recent appointments follow a string of departure announcements from six residential college deans, four of whom said they are taking on administrative roles within the University. Lewis told the News in April that the quick succession of departures was “just a coincidence.” “We have interviewed a lot of great candidates and the people who are joining as deans are experienced administrators and caring people who will make great contributions to their residential colleges and to Yale,” Lewis wrote in an email to the News. The appointments were announced in emails to each college community from Lewis following consultations with heads of college and advisory committees within each college. When asked about the selection process, Lewis wrote in an email to the News that “I can say that I’ve appreciated the input of students, staff, faculty, fellows, and heads.” Newman, the next dean of Silliman College, graduated from Yale as a member of Ezra Stiles College in 2008 and later earned his doctoral degree in Classical Studies from the University of Michigan. He currently serves at Princeton University as assistant dean for studies in Whitman College, one of the university’s seven residential colleges. Newman wrote in an email to the News that the “challenge of a new place” and the “opportunity to shepherd Yale students from matriculation to graduation” drew him to the role, adding that he is excited to teach “at some point” and that his transition to his new position has been as “smooth as such a transition can be.” “I hope students will see my ‘office,’ literal and conceptual, as a space for unabashedly asking whatever questions they have, for finding support in moments of difficulty, and for learning to show themselves (and others) grace as they face the trials of the undergraduate experience,” Newman wrote. Newman will move into Silliman with his partner, Nicole Rodriguez, and two pets, Bernard, their black cat, and Charlie, their dog. Julien Amsellem ’27, who is in Silliman, said in a phone interview with the News that he hopes Newman will be an active figure in the college community by attending student events and interacting with students outside formal office hours. “Deans really do play a large role for students,” Amsellem said. “One of the things we really emphasize is that much like the head of college, the dean is someone who is part of this family within the residential college system.” Sidorenko, the incoming dean of Berkeley College, received two master’s degrees in 2014 and a doctoral degree in 2023, all from Yale. She currently serves as assistant dean for access and engagement at Yale’s Graduate School of Arts and Sciences, where she oversees recruitment and retention initiatives and supports graduate students. Before joining the Graduate School administration, Sidorenko taught courses at Yale and also served for six years as a first-year adviser in Pierson and Grace Hopper colleges. Joining her in the Berkeley dean’s residence will be her Ragdoll cat, Salammbô. Sidorenko’s appointment follows the abrupt departure of former Berkeley dean Bárbara Cruvinel Santiago ’17, who was in her second year on the job. Lewis announced Cruvinel Santiago’s leave over spring break, which was met with student disappointment. Leach, the incoming dean of Davenport College, currently serves as assistant dean of undergraduate students at Dartmouth, where she offers academic and personal advising to undergraduates. Leach previously taught at Harvard, where she also worked as a resident tutor, academic advisor and interim dean of a house. According to an announcement sent to the Davenport community, Leach will be joined in the Davenport dean’s residence by her wife, Jordan, as well as their newborn son, Ellis, and their cat, Giles. “She brings to Yale a deep enthusiasm for residential college life and for supporting students both inside and outside the classroom,” Lewis wrote in his announcement of Leach’s appointment. Gardner joins Timothy Dwight College from Phillips Academy in Andover, Massachusetts, where he currently serves as assistant dean of students and instructor in English. Alexander Medel ’27, a Yale College Council senator for Timothy Dwight and a staff writer for the News, said he hopes his college’s next dean will understand the close-knit culture that defines its community and be prepared to embrace its traditions. “I would love to see somebody who understands what makes TD’s community so special, somebody who’s ready to embrace all the customs, traditions and all the special little things that make TD,” Medel said in a phone interview with the News. The upcoming school year is the last year of Lewis’ five-year term.

title: After Harvard passes grade cap, Yale faculty say ‘bravo’ for first step
author: Jolynda Wang
text: Conversations around grade inflation in the Ivy League turned into action on Wednesday when Harvard announced that 70 percent of its faculty voted in favor of a policy to cap A grades at 20 percent for each course, allowing for up to four additional A’s per class, beginning in fall 2027. Wednesday’s vote marked a major shift by Harvard to confront grade inflation that coincides with similar efforts at Yale, which are in earlier stages. Yale professors, while decrying that grade inflation contributes to student stress, perfectionism and difficulty distinguishing academic performance, have described the issue as a collective action problem, in which stricter grading by individual faculty — and universities — comparatively disadvantages their own students. In a statement after Wednesday’s vote, Amanda Claybaugh ’93, Harvard’s dean of undergraduate education who has spearheaded the school’s effort to curb grade inflation, expressed hope that the vote drives Harvard’s peers to follow its lead. “This is a consequential vote,” Claybaugh wrote. “It will, I believe, strengthen the academic culture of Harvard; it will also, I hope, encourage other institutions to confront similar questions with the same level of rigor and courage.” In interviews with the News, Yale professors expressed support for Harvard’s new grade cap as a meaningful step by a leading university to combat grade inflation. Many still pointed out what they described as imperfections in the system, including a lack of nuance between seminars and lectures, the possibility of a proliferation of A-minuses and a focus away from intellectual growth. Yale Law School professor Sarath Sanga LAW ’14 said “bravo to Harvard” in a Zoom interview, praising its faculty for taking concrete action on grade inflation. Sanga said that although he thinks Harvard’s solution is imperfect, it is preferable to maintaining the status quo of grade distribution, which he described as “beyond terrible and illogical.” “This is Harvard’s attempt to make a baby step toward putting together the best possible pedagogical environment for the students,” Sanga said. “By virtue of making the right step in the right direction and being the first one to do it publicly, I mean, that takes guts, right?” Grade inflation has reached similar heights at Yale and Harvard. At Harvard, after its administration urged faculty to combat grade inflation in October, the share of A’s fell from 60.2 percent in the 2024–25 school year to 53.4 percent last fall. At Yale, 58.3 percent of grades were A’s in the 2022–23 school year — part of a distribution that Yale College Dean Pericles Lewis described as “pretty consistent” over the past four years. Yale College’s class of 2026 set a record-high cutoff for Latin honors this month, with summa cum laude requiring a 3.99 GPA. Harvard’s cutoff for summa cum laude last year was 3.989. In April, Yale’s Committee on Trust in Higher Education released a report that includes remedies for grade inflation. The report recommends that the University registrar “immediately” start including grade percentiles per course in transcripts to reflect the “context for each grade.” It also recommended a “3.0 mean, or some other college-wide standard,” which it acknowledged will require “further conversation among the faculty” and urged that the conversation “begin now.” Yale College’s Standing Committee on Teaching, Learning and Advising — convened by Lewis in conjunction with University President Maurie McInnis — is currently reviewing grade inflation at Yale. In February, Lewis told the News that the standing committee’s members were closely monitoring the grading policies at Harvard, noting that peer universities are “competitors” and that he doesn’t want “an A at Yale to be seen as a lesser A.” When asked about the Harvard vote on Wednesday, Lewis wrote to the News that grading practices “will be a topic of discussion” for Yale faculty in the upcoming school year. Economics professor Ray Fair speculated in a phone interview that if Harvard significantly reduced the number of A grades while Yale continued awarding them at much higher rates, a Harvard A could come to carry more weight than a Yale A. That discrepancy could potentially affect Yale’s reputation and ultimately incentivize the University to adopt similar reforms, Fair said. Fair chaired the University’s ad hoc committee on grading policy, which convened in the 2012–13 and 2013–14 academic years. That committee’s proposal recommended a set of grading guidelines as well as that Yale College switch from a letter-grade system to a 100-point scale, which faced fierce student backlash and was ultimately dropped by faculty. “Yale tried in 2014 to be a leader in this, and the faculty rejected that. However, now Harvard accepted something similar, and so somebody has started this process,” Fair said, referring to efforts to combat grade inflation. “One would hope that — if you think grade inflation is a problem — the Harvard move would trigger other peer institutions to do the same thing or do something similar.” Sanga, who helped write the Committee on Trust in Higher Education’s report, described the committee’s recommendations as a “better-designed rule” than Harvard’s grade cap. Including course percentiles and aiming for an overall 3.0 average GPA would provide students with clearer, standardized feedback about their academic strengths while also making transcripts easier for outside readers to interpret, he said. But Sanga recognized that universities often hesitate to act until peer institutions move first, and he noted that Harvard’s prominence could pressure schools like Yale to reconsider their own grading practices. “There’s no denying that Harvard is the American flagship. This is the American global leader in higher education, whether you like it or not,” Sanga said, making note of Harvard’s resources. “It has this kind of leadership status even when it’s not perfect, maybe especially when it’s not perfect. And so I’m looking at what they just accomplished there.” Political science professor Dan Mattingly ’04 GRD ’08 referred to Harvard’s policy as “the least terrible option in a sea of terrible options” to combat grade inflation. He said the policy would reduce professors’ ability to independently assess student work and expressed hope for more “nuanced” reforms. Still, he compared Harvard’s move to “very strong medicine” that will “force people into a new equilibrium.” If more elite universities adopt measures against grade inflation, those reforms will become more “durable,” Mattingly added. Philosophy professor Thomas Pogge also described grade inflation as a “nationwide phenomenon” fueled by competition among universities, noting that prominent institutions like Yale and Harvard have a particular responsibility to lead reform efforts rather than continue escalating grades upward. “Highly visible institutions are much more able to take the first step,” Pogge said in a Zoom interview. Some Yale professors, however, pointed out that Harvard’s new system does not account for nuances between course styles and leaves room for the proliferation of A-minuses. Global affairs professor Ted Wittenstein ’04 LAW ’12 wrote in the email to the News that he was of “two minds” about the new Harvard policy. While he supported the Harvard grade cap for its immediacy and simplicity, Wittenstein raised concerns that a universal cap would fail to account for differences between large introductory lecture courses and smaller advanced seminars. Pogge also noted that Harvard’s policy places no cap on A-minus grades, potentially creating what he described as a “loophole.” Physics professor A. Douglas Stone echoed this concern. “They allow unlimited A-minuses it seems and this seems a huge loophole in that instructors not wanting to upset students can have only two grades, A and A-, and still not really differentiate different levels of achievement,” Stone wrote in an email to the News. “I hope we make changes which require a larger range of grades.” Astronomy professor Charles Bailyn ’81 wrote in an email to the News that he thinks Harvard’s proposal does not go “nearly far enough” to address the underlying problems with modern grading systems, arguing that the traditional scale of A through F remains fundamentally flawed. But Bailyn added that he would vote in favor of any Yale proposal to combat grade inflation — “Anything is better than the current ‘system,’” he wrote. Five professors said in interviews with the News that the broader goal of grading reform should be to return attention to intellectual growth and academic challenge rather than perfecting numbers on a transcript. English senior lecturer Heather Klemann GRD ’08 ’13 said in a Zoom interview that determining a percentage cap on grades can feel arbitrary and emphasized the importance of restoring students’ “love of learning.” Film and Media Studies professor Charles Musser ’73 questioned the fundamental concern about grade inflation and opposed Harvard’s proposed cap A grades, stating that the policy would undermine “academic freedom.” He said in a phone interview with the News that his goal is to inspire his students so that all of them produce strong enough work to receive an A. Musser added that Yale’s highly selective admissions process naturally results in a student body capable of earning excellent grades. Rather than viewing high grades as evidence of inflation, he said, they reflect the academic strength of admitted students. Grading systems should focus on encouraging intellectual growth rather than manufacturing an “artificial” distribution of grades, he noted. According to a 2023 report by Fair, 79 percent of grades in Yale College in the 2022–23 school year were A’s or A-minuses.

title: IN PHOTOS: Commencement 2026
text: VOL. CXLIX New Haven, Connecticut Tuesday, June 16, 2026

title: At Law School Commencement, Boasberg ’85 LAW ’90 stresses moral fiber
author: Sasha Cabral
text: Judge James Boasberg ’85 LAW ’90 spoke at Yale Law School’s 2026 Commencement ceremony on Monday to approximately 240 graduates and their guests. The event, which was held in Payne Whitney Gymnasium’s Lanman Center, followed the University-wide Commencement on Old Campus. Boasberg, the chief judge of the United States District Court for the District of Columbia, has been the subject of a firestorm of criticism from the right for his rulings against the Trump administration — particularly his order halting deportation flights of Venezuelan nationals in March 2025. In keeping with recent years, the Law School did not publicly announce Boasberg as the Commencement speaker in its announcement about the ceremony. In addition to the Commencement speech, the ceremony traditionally features remarks by the dean of the Law School and a speech from a faculty member. The faculty speaker this year was Nicholas R. Parrillo GRD ’01 ’05 ’12 LAW ’04. Former Dean Heather Gerken spoke about her departure from the Law School to lead the Ford Foundation, and current Dean Cristina Rodríguez ’95 LAW ’00 also addressed attendees. After Parrillo’s speech, Rodríguez introduced Boasberg, remarking on his participation in theater and basketball during his time at Yale. She added that the judge has made rulings on topics “at the heart of our democracy.” Last spring, Boasberg made headlines when he pursued a contempt investigation into the second Trump administration over its refusal to comply with his order to cease deportation flights to El Salvador — an order that prompted Trump, along with Republican lawmakers, to call for the judge’s impeachment. Last month , a court of appeals ordered the termination of the contempt investigation, citing the lack of a “clear and specific” order from Boasberg mandating that the Venezuelan nationals not be transferred into Salvadoran custody. Last July, former United States Attorney General Pam Bondi’s office filed a misconduct complaint against Boasberg, alleging that he had attempted to inappropriately influence other judges to raise concerns about the Trump administration’s actions. The complaint was ultimately dismissed by a federal appeals court. Two months ago, Trump characterized Boasberg as “wacky, nasty, crooked and totally out of control” after the judge quashed subpoenas in the Justice Department’s investigation into Federal Reserve chair Jerome Powell. Days later, Supreme Court Chief Justice John Roberts rebuked personal criticism of federal judges, saying, “it’s got to stop.” Roberts also criticized Trump’s calls for Boasberg’s impeachment last year. Boasberg did not address political matters in his speech, instead offering advice to graduating students. He advised graduates to seek out occupational opportunities with a “mission” that corresponds with their personal values and reminded them, “your life is larger than your career.” He recounted a pivotal moment from his own career where he realized he did not want to continue in a private practice role that involved denying some individuals’ insurance coverage. Boasberg said he found more fulfillment working for the government. In his speech, Boasberg also praised the instruction he received at the Law School from professors including Harold Koh and Guido Calabresi ’53 LAW ’58, who taught about “heroes in the law” that demonstrated “what committed lawyers can do to improve society.” He cited the work of judges like Thurgood Marshall and Frank Johnson, who championed civil rights and racial equality. Koh said that Boasberg’s speech reflected the judge’s own legacy in upholding the rule of law. “Chief Judge Boasberg is one of our finest graduates and judges,” Koh wrote to the News. “His deeply humane and principled speech both inspired our students and challenged them to stand up for the rule of law, as he himself has so courageously done.” Boasberg acknowledged that students might embark on a career in a large corporate firm for financial stability, but advised them to question constantly whether the job is the right one for them. He warned against the practice of choosing a career for the sake of keeping one’s options open. “But when you are representing a public entity, for example, a municipality, a state, or a federal government, there’s a meaningful difference between isolated disagreements with your client and helping to defend policies that are wholly antithetical to your core values,” Boasberg said. “I encourage all of you to have your own red line. Each of you will put it in a different place.” “If you were asked to cross that red line,” he said, “you must decide whether you can do so and still look at yourself in the mirror, or whether it’s time to start searching for a new job.” Lastly, Boasberg encouraged students to pursue unconventional legal careers, despite the uncertainty that might come with such paths. “You will find Yale Law graduates in an amazing array of positions, and mind-bogglingly varied millieus. But you won’t be one of them if you just sit in your cubicle, hoping that one day, someone will come by and tap you on the shoulder with something interesting,” Boasberg said. “Be imaginative, be creative and don’t be bound by the most traditional options.” During his time at the Law School, Boasberg said he lived at 61 Lake Place with seven other students, including current Supreme Court Justice Brett Kavanaugh ’87 LAW ’90. In his speech on Monday, Boasberg said the group still gathers annually. Last year, the Law School’s commencement speaker was Elizabeth Prelogar, the solicitor general under the Biden administration.

title: Yalies for mental health
author: Paul Johansen
text: Congratulations to the class of 2026! It has been a long and difficult road to graduation, and it’s a uniquely challenging moment in history to begin post-college life. How do I know it’s been a long and difficult road? In 2021, a group of students, alumni and family members impacted by Yale’s mental health policies organized Elis for Rachael. I serve as president of this group, and over the past four years, your fellow Yalies have communicated their trials and tribulations with us regarding mental health services at Yale. We are grateful to those of you who shared painful stories of struggling through the labyrinth of mental health services offered at Yale. We used your feedback to create a 10-point petition, which some of you may recall signing at Yale-Harvard games. And we used this petition as the basis of a landmark class-action lawsuit, which we settled with Yale in August 2023, just as you began your sophomore years. In the settlement, Yale agreed to many of the conditions that student groups had requested of the administration over the previous decade. Those student groups included the Yale College Council twice, Mental Health Justice at Yale, Students Unite Now and the Yale Law School Mental Health Alliance. Yale had previously made no credible changes in response to the requests of these student groups. In response to the lawsuit, however, Yale agreed to the majority of your 10 petition requests: Yale stopped terminating health insurance for students taking a medical leave; Yale eliminated costly, non-medical roadblocks to reinstatement following a medical withdrawal; Yale restructured the refund schedule for tuition, room and board fees; Yale allowed students reasonable access to campus while on medical withdrawal, no longer requiring written permission to set foot on campus; Yale agreed to reduce the minimum duration for medical withdrawals; Yale designated a part-time, non-evaluative administrator to advocate for students considering, currently on or returning from a medical leave of absence; and Yale thus far has ended their practice of rejecting reinstatement applications without any actionable explanations. In addition, Yale now allows part-time study as a reasonable accommodation in “rare” instances. Yale also agreed to establish a process for appealing accommodation denials from Student Accessibility Services. We are not convinced this process is independent from SAS, however. On the other hand, many students still wait unacceptably long to see a therapist. For instance, upon returning from summer vacation, students do not automatically continue seeing their therapist from the previous school year. Instead, they must undergo the placement process all over again, unnecessarily lengthening the time it takes to be matched with a therapist. Yale has not met student requests for a more diverse range of therapists. Yale still does not offer an affordable Preferred Provider Organization option for health insurance. And Yale did not agree to implement annual mental health first-aid training for students, faculty, staff and administrators. Furthermore, none of the settlement’s changes apply to graduate students. Under our August 2023 settlement with Yale, the University agreed to a three-year monitoring period during which alleged violations of the new policies could be brought quickly before a judge. That period ends Aug. 28, just two months from now. Although we have generally been encouraged by Yale’s implementation of their new policies, any future erosion will become significantly harder to challenge once judicial oversight expires. We note that the class of 2026 is the last set of students who can remember suffering through Yale previous punitive policies. One of the largest unresolved issues remains federal law. Under current regulations, universities that provide medical care may classify student medical records — ordinarily protected under HIPAA — as “educational records” governed instead by FERPA, which provides substantially weaker privacy protections. We have spoken with Massachusetts’ Senator Ed Markey, a longtime mental health advocate and one of HIPAA’s original authors, about closing that loophole. We were very encouraged to see the Princeton Review include three Ivy League schools — Columbia, Dartmouth and Princeton — on its “2026 Mental Health Services Honor Roll.” Yale was notably absent. The schools recognized this year have “shown a strong commitment to their students’ mental health and wellbeing.” We look forward to the day when Yale earns similar recognition through sustained, rigorous and genuinely student-centered mental health support for both undergraduate and graduate students. Only then will Yale’s policies truly help future classes get to their own finish lines. PAUL JOHANSEN is president of Elis for Rachael and an adjunct professor of mathematics at Berkshire Community College in Pittsfield, Massachusetts. Paul graduated from Yale in 1988 as a member of Ezra Stiles College. He can be reached at findus@elisforrachael.org .

title: On rights and responsibilities
author: Michael Ndubisi
text: Today, on Old Campus, the University’s president will stand before the graduating class for the 325th time and admit them, by tradition, to “all the rights and responsibilities of their degrees.” Some version of those words will be spoken at thousands of commencements across the country, but Yale’s carries particular weight. Where 133 miles north, Harvard will welcome its graduates into “the fellowship of educated men and women,” Yale’s conferral reflects a distinction once made to elevate the obligations of learning and has since diffused into the shared ceremonial language of American commencements. It is a small but telling example of how Yale has long set the terms for American higher education. This April, in another attempt to shape the landscape of higher learning, Yale released a 58-page report on why public trust in universities has collapsed. It identifies real failures in costs that have made elite education a luxury and admissions processes that perform meritocracy while, in reality, rewarding wealth . To its credit, the committee that wrote the report confronts the crisis of higher education with unusual candor. But what it cannot bring itself to examine is the role Yale played in spreading those failures everywhere else. Nor can it reckon with what has become of the graduates it sends into the world. Yale's career infrastructure delivers 46% of its work-bound graduates into finance, consulting and technology. That pipeline concentrates them in five cities across four coastal states. They work in industries that have done more than almost any other to narrow the geography of American opportunity, to hollow out the places they came from and to make the kind of upward mobility Yale claims to represent harder for everyone else. For the rural or first-generation student whose family sacrificed for the degree, that pipeline is not a promise kept. For the country that subsidizes Yale's tax exemption, it is a negative return on its investment. For the faculty who came to contribute to something larger than credential production, it is a quiet embarrassment. And the trust report does not mention any of this. Yale’s report was still circulating when emails obtained from San Francisco’s mayor’s office revealed that the University’s engineering dean had been developing a business plan for a satellite campus in the Bay Area. Yale’s spokesperson says there are no current plans. The emails suggest the conversation is further along than that. The move would follow Vanderbilt’s recent acquisition of the financially insolvent California College of the Arts, in what one Atlantic writer called the national-chain model of undergraduate education. The schools with the resources to expand are doing so toward the cities where talent and capital have already concentrated. While Yale measures its national impact on a state-by-state map , counting clinical trials and patents, its expansion strategy points in the same direction as its graduates . Just days after the trust report’s release, Yale quietly revised its mission statement, excising its commitment to improving the world and educating aspiring leaders. What remains is the preservation and dissemination of “knowledge,” in a narrowed ambition that happens to position the University perfectly to become a national leader in artificial intelligence. Researchers writing in this year’s Business and Society journal warned that generative AI threatens to degrade our collective knowledge ecosystem through what they call “ epistemic pollution ” or the dissemination of low-quality, misleading and fabricated information at scale. And the University that just committed itself to preserving knowledge is racing toward the industry most likely to corrupt it. The report identifies Yale’s predicament as a trust problem, but it is more precisely a resentment problem. Americans increasingly see elite universities and their graduates as participants in a system designed to concentrate wealth and opportunity for a small class of people who already possess most of both at their expense . A university that has revised its mission to withdraw its vision from the world beyond its walls, tracks its impact in patents while half its graduates funnel into extractive industries and is considering expanding toward the same coastal cities those graduates already cluster in is not answering that resentment; it is confirming it. Most of us will leave Old Campus for the last time after commencement with some sadness. There will be tears for the end of our shortest, gladdest years of life. But the conferral of our rights and responsibilities is not a farewell. It is a beginning. Gone are the days of the University for God and for country. But a University that exists only for Yale is not much use to the country that sustains it. So if the institution no longer takes the obligations embedded in the words of our commencement ceremony seriously, then we must. We know the rights: Yale clubs, library access, reunions, The Game and others. The responsibilities are real, too, and they are ours now as well. MICHAEL NDUBISI is a former editor of the Yale Daily News’ Opinion desk and writer of the fortnightly column “A More Perfect Union.” He is a graduating senior in Saybrook College majoring in History and Political Science. He can be reached at michael.ndubisi@yale.edu .

title: Consider our legacy
author: Emily Hettinger
text: We made it. After four years of essays, problem sets and a million choices to make about extracurriculars and internships and social groups, all that’s left is to design quirky hats and enjoy the momentous occasion of our commencement with our friends and family. But in the midst of all this joy, I think we should make one final commitment. We need to grapple with the fact that we are the beneficiaries of a deeply broken and unequal system. And as the class of 2026, we must use the privileges of our education to do something about it. It needs to be said: Yale has a wealth problem, uncomfortable as it may be to acknowledge. According to the New York Times , Yale accepts more students from the top one percent of the United States’ income distribution than the entire bottom sixty percent combined. Daniel Markovits ’91 LAW ’00 says it well in his 2019 book, The Meritocracy Trap: The wealth-based admissions system we have concentrates wealth and power in the hands of a small few, at the expense of political unity, upward social mobility for most Americans and the life satisfaction of even the most successful among us. But, to me, the deeper risk is that the disproportionate wealth at Yale denigrates the very idea of the liberal arts education. There are many reasons to choose a liberal arts education, but probably the main value of the liberal arts, especially in this moment, is that they can, allegedly, prepare citizens to be critical thinkers. In her 1963 book, Eichmann in Jerusalem , philosopher Hannah Arendt describes evil as banal, that most people who do evil things are not evil people but just people following orders. Critical thinkers, in my view, are the ones who say no to banal orders to do evil things. Or at the very least, stand up for what is right, even when it is easy to stay silent. For a school that claims a devotion to the liberal arts, I am concerned by our acceptance of the banal orders we are confronted with. Yale sends a disproportionate number of its students into fields that bring wealth and prestige to their workers, often at the expense of the common good. Places where it is someone’s job to cut costs in United States detention centers, turbocharge opioid sales and invest in fossil fuels. Is this how we want to be remembered? I think things would be different if we reimagined who a Yale education was for. For middle and working class students, a college education, much less an elite college education, is not inevitable. Paying for a school like Yale as a middle class family usually means pinching every penny for years, often to end up in serious debt . And coming to Yale from an underfunded public school often means being the only person from your high school at Yale, which can be an incredibly lonely and daunting experience. Taken together, the opportunity cost to attend Yale for non-wealthy students is quite high. As such, the very choice to attend a fancy school where life is put on hold for four years, just to think and grow, is grounded in the critical thought that the liberal arts are meant to cultivate. For these students, a Yale education is a service to something much larger than themselves, an undertaking on behalf of the community that sacrificed much to make the dream possible. If we had more students for whom this education carried a strong sense of community responsibility, maybe more people would dedicate themselves to the many problems in our world that need solving. We, as a generation of Yalies, have the power to change the definition of a Yale student, to utterly transform our admissions system into one that prioritizes the public good over wealth. But in order to do that, we need to acknowledge that regardless of how we all arrived at Yale, we leave with far more to personally gain from the status quo than we ever will from a reformed system. All of us will have access to opportunities that very few are afforded, simply because of our Yale diploma. All of us who choose to have children will be the parents of Yale legacies who will have an explicit advantage in the Yale admissions process. And even if legacy preference goes away, our kids will have disproportionate access to fancy private schools, music and sports lessons, test prep and all sorts of other things that will look good on college applications. If we change the system, we directly jeopardize our own children’s chances of following in our footsteps at Yale. So, to my peers in the class of 2026, what do we want our legacy to be? Though it will involve personal sacrifice, I think we ought to lead a transformation of Yale’s culture to prioritize the public over the self. We can do better than agnostically educating a disproportionately wealthy student body to develop skills used for personal benefit instead of the public good. In a country with increasing distrust in elites , it is our duty to do better. As my parting contribution to the News, I ask the class of 2026 to do one thing: Pull the ladder up behind you. Reject the structures that we will soon benefit from and call for something better, even though it may cost your future children the opportunity to study at Yale. Commit to organizing against legacy admissions at our university, reinvest the thousands of dollars you might have spent on private school tuition into your local community, surround yourself with people who did not receive an Ivy League education and demand that Yale serve the public good over its own interests. If we succeed, we will be remembered for something much more than clever hats on Class Day. EMILY HETTINGER is a senior in Pierson College studying Psychology and Education and a staff columnist for the News. Contact her at emily.hettinger@yale.edu .

title: The death of college exploration
author: Ryan Wang
text: If you read my application to Yale, you would probably guess that I spent my bright college years flitting between economic research seminars, physics labs and a political campaign office. Instead, I spent my first week of classes grinding through applications for the Yale Student Investment Group or the Yale Undergraduate Consulting Group. By the end of sophomore year, I had climbed the ranks of the consulting group and was running its client acquisition team. Academically, I dropped my physics major and rarely strayed outside of economics and mathematics classes. Sometimes, I mourn for the kid who eagerly submitted his Yale application. I suspect I am not alone. In his 1852 treatise “ The Idea of a University ,” the English theologian John Henry Newman argued that a college education should cultivate the intellect broadly, not provide vocational training. Newman was not alone in his position. Yale’s own 1828 report on academic instruction declared: “Our object is not to teach that which is peculiar to any one of the professions; but to lay the foundation which is common to them all.” Yet for many of us Yalies, this vision has not materialized. Somewhere between 1828 and today, the dream of spending our college years exploring our passions and career interests remains only that — a dream. Exploration is dead. The question is: who killed it? My first suspect is one familiar to most Yalies: our future employers and graduate schools. In the past two decades, the college internship has devolved from an act of exploration into a prerequisite for entry. During my time at Yale, top consultancies have steadily moved recruiting timelines earlier. The proliferation of sophomore summer internships and pre-professional clubs pressures new students to dedicate their underclassmen years to breaking into the industry. My friends in other fields tell the same story. The aspiring investment banker needs a boutique stint before junior year, the software engineer needs a project portfolio to capture the attention of tech companies, the mechanical engineer needs lab experience before finishing their core requirements and the future policymaker needs a D.C. internship before their first political theory seminar. Yalies interested in further academic study face the same curse. Matriculant GPAs at top medical and law schools have crept up consistently, while aspiring doctors now spend thousands of undergraduate hours on clinical experience and research to remain competitive. Students drawn to doctoral studies face National Science Foundation and National Institutes of Health fellowship applications demanding research experience dating to freshman year. Career preparation has superseded exploration across campus. Yet, can our future employers and schools be fully to blame? I have a second suspect for the murder of exploration: us. When younger Yalies seek career advice over dining hall meals, I first ask if they have found their guiding purpose. The conversation usually plays out like this: “What is your north star?” “Right now, I’m locked in on securing that McKinsey internship.” “Yes, but what’s your end goal? Your own guiding mission?” “...I’m not sure yet. Maybe law school?” The ambition that carried us to New Haven is easily hijacked by arbitrary measures of success established by the student community. Within weeks of arriving, first-years are exposed to what “success” looks like in each realm of campus: “You should apply to YSIG if you’re interested in finance. They have a ton of alumni at Goldman Sachs.” “If you want a shot at a T14 school, you should have a 4.0 and a law journal publication.” Rather than charting their own paths, Yalies often co-opt a communal definition of success and work tirelessly to achieve it. First-semester me — the kid that raved about particle physics and economic policy in his Yale application — sleepwalked my way up the ranks of a consulting club without ever asking if that was what I had really wanted. So, who killed college exploration? Both suspects are culpable. Employers and schools impose aggressive recruitment timelines and stringent requirements, and ambitious students eagerly rise to the challenge. This hyper-competitive applicant pool encourages firms to raise their standards again, fueling an arms race. Consequently, higher education produces a generation of over-prepared, under-explored graduates. Students determined to succeed within a single field never discover what they truly want, arriving at 22 in a job they chose at 19 for reasons that were never really their own. Is there a way to break the cycle? Employers and graduate schools should ask which of their requirements still measure genuine ability, and reckon with what they lose by screening out candidates who spent college actually exploring. Absent institutional change, I find it naive to tell Yalies they should simply ignore post-graduate pressures. Instead, I recommend “strategic exploration:” invest seriously in at least two fields you actually care about, fulfill your distributional requirements early to explore the whole range of classes Yale has to offer, and ask whether your ambitions are genuinely yours or inherited from the students around you. I broke out of autopilot at the end of junior year. I rediscovered my love for economics, reneging on a full-time consulting offer to pursue academic research. The world today rewards the straightest path, yet there is a quiet tragedy in arriving at a destination you never chose for yourself. The kid you described in your Yale application is still in there — go find them. RYAN WANG is a senior in Jonathan Edwards College studying Economics and Mathematics. After Yale, Ryan will be working at the MIT Economics Department as a research fellow. Contact him at ryan.wang@yale.edu .

title: What is Yale for?
author: Matias Guevara Ruales
text: Why does one come to Yale? One reasonable answer is money. Just four years out of college, Yale alums earn almost double what the typical college graduate does, and the median lifetime return of coming here was recently estimated at 3.4 million dollars. Still, one could be forgiven for thinking that a Yale education ought to offer more than the material benefits that might accrue from the degree. You may have heard Dean Lewis make some such distinction during a public appearance at some point, and I echo it here because my concern is precisely one of Yale’s main selling points to prospective students: the idea that when you decide to enroll here, you’re signing up to get a “liberal education.” The phrase is familiar to all of us at American higher-learning institutions, but what has it come to mean, amid the onerous demands of our results-oriented culture? Can we still recognize in it the suggestion that there are at least two parts to the value of a college education that matter, only one of which produces “results” in the traditional sense? The first of those is plainly the accreditation of technical skills embodied by the diploma, which paves the way for the superior earnings elite schools are famous for. The second part, often called the liberal arts, is harder to discern. It has to do with the development of students as people — their personal flourishing, we might have said once, though that word now carries a sentimental ring that may itself be a symptom of the diminishing status of the liberal arts in our culture. But the liberal arts have a use, too. They are, in the words of the recent report by the Committee on Trust in Higher Education, “the foundational wisdom that will serve [students] throughout their lives.” No virtue has ever advertised a payoff in strict cost-benefit terms, yet “wisdom” does properly characterize the part of our education that makes us critical thinkers, generous friends and conscientious citizens. These attributes are crucial to any individual life, and to any free society, but in recent years we have begun to surrender them. Ultra-competitive job markets have meant that a growing number of students are allocating their time so as to maximize early-career outcomes at the expense of pursuits once considered worthwhile, like taking challenging courses outside of their majors or joining non-professional clubs. Even within the areas directly related to their post-graduate goals, students must often turn to artificial intelligence, dig up class materials from previous semesters or employ other workarounds that free up time for professional development but defeat the most basic point of academic work: to learn. Underlying this trend is the belief that wasting time on pursuits not easily translatable into attractive resume entries can be fatal for long-term success. But recent research says otherwise. A literature review about the highest levels of human achievement, published last year, analyzed data from top performers across different fields — from Nobel laureates to Olympic champions to the the world’s best chess players — and found that what distinguished them from their sub-elite peers was that the former showed a more gradual progression toward excellence and a larger amount of multidisciplinary practice early on. That is, they took the time to explore many different interests before they specialized and became best-in-class. The world is not organized to facilitate this kind of exploration. Universities emphasize early displays of merit over lifelong achievement, while corporations use their deep pockets to lure young graduates into highly specialized jobs. And on a cultural level, too, we tend to think of doing things simply because we’re passionate about them as somehow amateur in the pejorative sense. But doing things for their own sake should not demote our pursuits to the status of hobbies. On the contrary, when we treat a hobby with enough seriousness we elevate it to the category of a passion, and the power to do this lies, in no small part, with each of us. What would happen if we approached our education as an experience in itself valuable? For one, our efforts might begin to seem worthwhile even without anybody’s stamp of approval, and we, in turn, might become more resistant to failure. When we looked in the mirror and saw that we had played our cards wrong, squandered our time, disappointed the expectations of parents and friends, we would not give up, because our work would not be just a means, but an end in itself. Why does one come to Yale? Yes, to be successful. Yes, to have a good income. But we also come here to be students, become better persons, and find the thing, or things, worthy of our lives’ work. We come here, in short, to flourish. The role of administrators in protecting that sacred soil of our personal flourishing, the university, is paramount. Yale could do more to fight the utilitarian trends that endanger it presently. But we, too, have a part to play in reclaiming the privilege of a truly liberal education — a phrase whose roots lie in the idea that to be educated is to become more free.
