What could be more profitable than that? MATIAS GUEVARA RUALES is a senior in Jonathan Edwards College studying Humanities and Economics. He can be reached at matias.guevararuales@yale.edu .

title: Unilever research facility set to harness New Haven biotech scene
author: Evelyn Ronan
text: The consumer goods company Unilever will invest $270 million in a new research and development center in downtown New Haven, the company announced in a press conference last Thursday. The center is set to open at 2 Church St. in 2029. At the conference, which was livestreamed , Mayor Justin Elicker and Gov. Ned Lamont SOM ’80 heralded the investment as a sign of growth for New Haven and the state. Elicker lauded the city’s “booming” bioscience sector, which Unilever hopes to harness in studying “skin biology, the microbiome and the skin-brain axis,” according to a press release. In a statement emailed to the News, Leo Aquino, the company’s senior vice president of research and development, cited New Haven’s “ecosystem” of universities and quantum computing investment as a reason for the move. The center will apply “AI-powered, digitally integrated science” to product development and will house “formulation, fragrance, packaging, consumer insights, clinical testing” all “under one roof,” Aquino wrote. The Church Street location is set to employ around 300 workers. The Unilever development news arrives amid a steady flow of biotech developments in New Haven, funded by both Yale and the state. In September, Connecticut announced a $50 million grant to facilitate biotech growth in the city, alongside separate grants for startups QuantumCT and BioCT, which received $10 million and $3 million from the state, respectively. The building at 2 Church St. is set to be constructed by Winstanley Enterprises, a commercial real estate developer that has acquired properties across New Haven, CT Insider reported . In 2013, Winstanley Enterprises began construction on a tower at 100 College St. which now houses Yale’s psychology and neuroscience departments and Alexion Pharmaceuticals, a biopharmaceutical company. In 2024, the group finished construction on a building at 101 College St. which also houses Alexion, as well as biotech lab facilities. Both are located about a quarter of a mile from 2 Church St., where the building housing Unilever is set to stand. “New Haven places us at the center of some of the world’s leading medical schools and research institutes — including Yale — and we fully intend to build on those relationships as the center develops,” Aquino wrote. Becca Schelling GRD ’29, the director of content for the Yale Biotech Club, suggested that the Unilever investment could make the city a more attractive destination for recent graduates. The club supports student engagement with the local biotech sector by partnering with companies through externship and informational programs, Schelling said in a phone interview. Having more biotech companies in downtown New Haven “makes it easier” for the club to “facilitate those interactions with students,” she said. Although Schelling said that she had “not seen” members of the club staying in the city after graduating, she guessed that “if the opportunities presented themselves in New Haven, people would take them.” The Connecticut Department of Economic and Community Development will contribute a $10 million loan to support the construction of the 2 Church St. building, part of an effort to retain jobs for Connecticut residents, CT Insider reported. The new lab will replace Unilever’s longtime research and development lab in nearby Trumbull, Connecticut. David Schleicher, a Yale Law School professor who focuses on local government and property law, wrote in an email to the News that Unilever could have relocated out-of-state, like General Electric did in 2016 when it left Fairfield, Connecticut. A News article from the time described criticism that painted General Electric’s departure as an example of “the state’s chronic inability to grow its high-tech industry to full potential.” Ward 7 Alder Christine Kim ’99 wrote in a Monday email to the News that an “anchor tenant” of Unilever’s scale would mean “daytime population, foot traffic for our small businesses, and energy on blocks that have had vacancies.” Kim’s ward includes 2 Church St. as well as a cluster of nearby Winstanley bioscience developments. Kim added that she is keeping an eye on the “community impacts” of the center. “These are world-class scientists and engineers, many with families, who’ll need housing in an already-tight market. I don’t want our growth to price out the residents who built this city,” she wrote. Kim’s conversations with constituents about the future Unilever center have yielded both “genuine excitement about jobs and a livelier downtown” and “real questions about housing costs, parking, who gets hired, and what the public puts in versus gets back,” she wrote, noting that many are still “taking it in.” Aquino did not comment on which future roles would be filled by Unilever employees currently based in Trumbull and which would be filled through local hiring, but he noted that “pinpointing an area that worked for talent retention and attracting future talent” was one of the “key elements” in choosing the New Haven location. When asked about affordability concerns, Elicker wrote that New Haven is a “growing city” and is “committed to growing inclusively.” Elicker also pointed to the city’s goal of constructing 10,000 new housing units by 2035, a goal which New Haven is “well on our way” to achieving, he wrote. The 2 Church St. building will contribute millions of dollars in tax revenue to help support municipal services, Elicker wrote, adding that constructions at 100 College St. and 101 College St. contributed “more than the annual budget for the Parks Department or New Haven Free Public Library” in taxes to the city last year. Schleicher wrote that he did not think Unilever’s plans would substantially alter the city’s housing market, noting that Yale University and Yale New Haven Hospital each employ more than 10,000 employees, much more than Unilever’s expected staff of 300. Unilever will share the 2 Church St. space with biotech startup QuantumCT, according to the New Haven Independent .

title: Witnessing ICE arrests and nonprofit cuts, locals decry Trump policies
author: Nellie Kenney
text: A group of New Haveners, clad in bright colors and carrying paper cranes, sang out advice for resisting Immigration and Customs Enforcement officers at a recent May Day rally on the New Haven Green: “Don’t say anything, don’t sign anything. Don’t let them trick you into opening the door.” Local activists — like these members of Singing Resistance New Haven, a chapter of the national organization — have mobilized in response to sweeping federal policy changes and their local effects since the beginning of Donald Trump’s second presidential term. New Haven advocates described an atmosphere of anxiety amid both the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown and federal cuts to social services, which have jeopardized local access to food. The “biggest thing, both for attorneys and for non-citizens living in the U.S.” is the “uncertainty and how quickly things are changing,” Maureen Abell, an immigration attorney at the New Haven Legal Assistance Association, said in a phone interview last Friday. Federal immigration officers have arrested at least five people in New Haven this year as part of the president’s ongoing mass deportation operation . ICE arrests increased by around 300 percent in New England in the first seven months of Trump’s presidency, according to data from the Immigration Research Initiative. Abell pointed to cases of federal immigration officers arresting people while leaving court or at green-card interviews . Although these arrests comprised “a small percentage of cases,” she said, the practice is “still having a huge effect through how afraid it is making people to engage with the system.” ICE detained a woman from the Hill neighborhood last June and a Wilbur Cross High School student the following month. The student, Esdrás Zabaleta-Ramirez, was later released following a ruling by a federal immigration judge. In January 2026, ICE arrested three people in downtown New Haven and another person in a New Haven courthouse . In March, federal agents arrested a nursing student at Southern Connecticut State University, Keyla Vasquez-Zuniga, who was granted bond last week. Those arrests have sparked an array of local protests. In July 2025, over a hundred people gathered at Wilbur Cross to protest Zabaleta-Ramirez’ detention. Teachers at the school led a letter-writing campaign attesting to the student’s schoolwork and character. Last month, students and activists rallied at Southern Connecticut State University, calling for Vasquez-Zuniga’s release. New Haven has also been the site of protests against ICE activity at the national level. In response to the deaths of Renée Good and Alex Pretti this January during the Trump administration’s crackdown in Minneapolis, locals formed the New Haven chapter of Singing Resistance and organized four demonstrations , each with at least a hundred attendees, in January and February. As organizers gather to protest immigration enforcement actions, nearby city organizations continue to grapple with federal funding cuts and locals describe increased food insecurity due to Trump-era policy. Integrated Refugee and Immigrant Services, a local nonprofit that aids refugee resettlement, closed its New Haven office in April 2025 after its funding was slashed by the federal government. The organization was also forced to lay off 30 percent of its staff, Maggie Mitchell Salem, the nonprofit’s executive director, told the News in February. In 2024, Integrated Refugee and Immigrant Services had a budget of over $20 million, most of which was provided by the federal government, Mitchell Salem said in a phone interview last Friday. In 2025, following federal funding cuts, its budget dropped to around $11 million, and this year, it hovers around $7 million, Mitchell Salem said — a number which will continue to decrease as funding to support Afghan arrivals “sunsets” in September. Mitchell Salem called the fallout of federal cuts “painful,” adding that for many immigrants, the organization’s New Haven location was one of the “first places they went to receive support and services” in the United States. “We’re having to make some very tough choices, and we have to figure out what the people we serve need most,” she said. “What, reasonably, can IRIS do through our food pantry, through our array of program services? And then what do others do, perhaps better than us? And how can we all work together to de-duplicate, to make sure that the most essential services remain available while having to cut back on others?” The Trump administration’s policy has altered the nonprofit’s partnerships as well as its funding, Mitchell Salem said. In October, the nonprofit announced that it would not receive refugees from the United States Refugee Admissions Program after the Trump administration moved to prioritize the resettlement of Afrikaners, a white minority group in South Africa. “We have never really questioned who the federal government was sending. And now we are,” Mitchell Salem said. Other local nonprofits have felt the impacts of federal actions through their effects on the populations that these organizations seek to help. Steve Werlin, the executive director of New Haven’s Downtown Evening Soup Kitchen, wrote in an email to the News that the greatest effects of federal policy “on the food assistance side” have been changes brought on by the One Big Beautiful Bill Act. The act, which Trump signed last July, tightened the work requirements around eligibility for the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program, restricted eligibility for non-citizens, made changes to benefit calculations and shifted payment responsibilities onto states, according to the National Conference of State Legislatures. “Many of the people we serve have lost eligibility completely, or have seen their monthly allocations through SNAP decreased,” Werlin wrote. He also noted blocked funding to the Emergency Food and Shelter Program, which had been “pumping millions of dollars of food assistance funding directly to community providers across the country.” Werlin hypothesized that the “atmosphere of fear” created by immigration crackdowns has “made it more difficult for people to access food.” Kimberly Hart, a member of the board of directors for the Loaves and Fishes Food Pantry, has seen a “drop in migrants coming to the pantry,” she said. Hart said that changes to the SNAP program “disrupt” the lives of “have-nots.” SNAP was “working very well” before the changes, she said. 6,626 New Haveners — about 17.4 percent of the city’s total voters — voted for Trump in 2024, according to the News’ coverage at the time.

title: New Haven fights back against an antagonistic Trump administration
author: Evelyn Ronan
text: Since the beginning of President Donald Trump’s second term, New Haven has been targeted by the federal government as part of its crackdown against progressive and sanctuary cities. The city has responded with a series of coalition lawsuits and amicus briefs opposing the federal government, with local and state officials seeking to distance their political priorities with those of the Trump administration. In March, the federal government sued New Haven back, alleging that the city’s policies impede cooperation between local authorities and federal immigration enforcement. New Haven has joined five lawsuits and 24 amicus briefs since Trump took office, challenging policies related to immigration enforcement, federal funding, municipal authority and cuts to social welfare programs. In January 2025, the federal government released a memo which froze roughly $3 trillion in nonprofit and local government funds that provide the essential short-term cash flow allowing cities like New Haven to maintain regular operations. The motion was rescinded just as Mayor Justin Elicker was condemning the action at a Hartford press conference. A month later, the city signed an amicus brief alleging that the National Institutes of Health’s research funding cuts would destabilize the scientific research ecosystem and pose threats to early-career researchers, lamenting the “slash-and-burn philosophy” of the Trump administration regarding crucial funding, according to previous interviews with the News. Mayor Justin Elicker offered his first major commentary on the federal blows last February at his annual State of the City address, where he briefly alluded to the federal targeting of sanctuary cities like New Haven and disapproved of the Trump administration’s aims to defund diversity, equity and inclusion initiatives. Soon after, New Haven joined several Democrat-led municipalities in a lawsuit arguing that Trump’s crackdown on illegal immigration violated several clauses that protect municipal autonomy and unjustly targeted cities like New Haven for their “sanctuary” status. Last March, when announcing his proposed budget for fiscal year 2026, Elicker emphasized the difference between New Haven’s political posture and that of the Trump administration. Elicker also criticized the unpredictability of the Trump administration’s financial cutbacks, which he said makes it difficult for municipalities to draw up spending plans. He noted that New Haven is exercising “fiscal caution,” and that public entities like New Haven Public Schools rely on funding from the federal government. A month later, New Haven signed on to another lawsuit, which alleged that recent funding freezes to environment-related initiatives — including the termination of $20 billion in grant funding, $30 million of which was designated for New Haven — were illegal. That March, Elicker lamented the federal government “turning its back on cities” despite binding agreements in an interview with the News. Later that year, in October, New Haven joined another lawsuit which challenged the Department of Homeland Security’s updated terms and conditions for grant recipients. New regulations required recipients to agree not to “operate any programs that advance or promote DEI, DEIA, or discriminatory equity ideology .” The lawsuit coincided with last year’s New Haven mayoral race. Facing a Republican challenger , Elicker continued to emphasize New Haven’s “progressive values.” In a debate , he called the city the “most welcoming” in the world, alluding again to its sanctuary status. Allie Jacobs, the city’s primary legal counsel, said that New Haven weighs the benefits of joining the legal fight against the federal government against the fact that doing so could invite more scrutiny from Trump. “We assess whether the city, its interest, or its residents would be significantly adversely impacted by a particular law, policy, or judicial decision,” Jacobs wrote in an email to the News. “Sometimes that takes the form of joining a lawsuit if we have standing, sometimes that takes the form of an amicus brief, and sometimes that takes the form of other advocacy approaches.” When announcing New Haven’s participation in the lawsuit against the DHS, Elicker said that he was not concerned with drawing extra attention to New Haven. “I hear from some of my mayoral colleagues out there that they don’t want to make their cities a target,” Elicker said at a press conference at the time. “Now is a time that we should not be hiding. Now is a time that we should be standing up and fighting for the values of our community, both because at some point we’re all going to be a target anyway, but more importantly, because this is the right thing to do.” Days after New Haven joined the lawsuit against the DHS, Elicker contrasted the city’s balanced budget with the financial gridlock at the federal level that had resulted in a shutdown. “We in New Haven are continuing to do things differently by providing transparent, reliable, and stable budgeting,” he said at a press conference on the city’s fiscal outlook. “It’s what our residents deserve.” Two weeks later, New Haven joined a lawsuit filed by a coalition of municipalities, charities, religious organizations, business and unions against the Trump administration’s decision not to fund Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program, or SNAP, benefits in the wake of the government shutdown. In response to the federal government’s cuts to SNAP funding — which Elicker condemned as “cruelty” that “blocked food from the table of 30,000 New Haveners” in a November interview with the News — Connecticut set aside $500 million for an emergency relief fund that subsidized food stamps during the government shutdown. In December 2025, New Haven joined a fifth lawsuit against the federal government, led by the Public Rights Project, over the federal government’s attempt to claw back over $12 billion in local funding designated for transit and public healthcare programs. The federal government declared that states would need to change policies on DEI initiatives, immigration, and abortion to obtain frozen funds. Aside from the slew of lawsuits, New Haven has joined in the national effort against the federal deputization of municipal governments and a national crackdown on immigration. This January, New Haven signed onto an amicus brief backing Minnesota in its lawsuit against the officials who authorized the federal government’s mass deportations. Elicker doubled down on his effort to distance New Haven ideologically from the Trump administration at the swearing-in ceremony for city officials this year, emphasizing New Haven’s values of “tolerance,” “respect for diversity” and “compassion for our most vulnerable.” He said that the Trump administration’s haphazard funding cuts force city officials to remain vigilant. Now, a year and a half since his first major action against the federal government, Elicker has continued to define his own ideological posture, as well as New Haven’s, as antithetical to Trump’s. In his State of the City address this February, the mayor doubled down on his previous criticism of the Trump administration, calling it an “endless dumpster fire of chaos and cruelty.” Following the dispatch of federal agents to carry out Operation Broken Trust, an Immigration and Customs Enforcement roundup that resulted in the arrest of 65 immigrants across Connecticut, the Trump administration last month filed its own lawsuit against New Haven. The Department of Justice complaint argued that New Haven’s policies illegally restrict local authorities’ collaboration with federal immigration enforcement, disregard federal law and threaten public safety. The suit, which also named Elicker, Connecticut Gov. Ned Lamont SOM ’80 and Attorney General William Tong as defendants, criticized New Haven’s enforcement of the Trust Act , which prevents the “deputization of local and state law enforcement for immigration enforcement.” A 2025 “Year in Review” message from Tong criticized the Trump administration’s “cruel, dangerous, and hurtful agenda,” adding that his office would not relent in its protection of Connecticut families. Rep. Rosa DeLauro, who represents New Haven in Congress, blasted Trump for “pursuing politically motivated lawsuits targeting our state for not carrying out ICE’s extreme enforcement agenda.” “President Trump’s actions are hurting working families, making it harder for them to afford basic necessities,” DeLauro said in a statement sent to the News by a spokesperson. “We must fight back, and New Haven and Connecticut are leading the way by challenging the Trump administration’s actions in court. Many of these cases have already been successful and restored funding to our communities.” In a phone interview with the News, Elicker said the city had been “prepared all along for a possible lawsuit” but emphasized New Haven’s compliance with federal law. “We can see that similar Trump lawsuits across the nation have already been thrown out,” the mayor said. “Trump is trying to force us to settle and carry out immigration policy, but that is not our job,” Elicker added. Several of the city’s legal challenges have already produced major rulings. One federal judge blocked the Trump administration from withholding federal funding from sanctuary jurisdictions, siding with New Haven, and another ordered the restoration of roughly $31 million in frozen environmental grants owed to the city, though the Trump administration has appealed portions of that ruling. Some legal disputes remain unresolved, including the litigation over DHS grant conditions and broader fights surrounding immigration enforcement. Elicker seems to have no intentions of backing down. He said that his approach differs from those of his colleagues across Connecticut. “Some have spoken out, but some have remained under the radar, and I don’t think that’s the right thing to do at this moment,” Elicker said. “We have to fight for what we believe in.” The Department of Justice does not currently have a permanent attorney general. Elijah Hurewitz-Ravitch contributed reporting.

title: Wooster Square and walkability encourage seniors to stay in New Haven
author: Tajrian Khan
text: As seniors prepare to leave Yale’s campus and enter the workforce, a handful of them are choosing to remain in the Elm City next year. In a survey sent out to the class of 2026 by the News, 13 out of 115 respondents who said they will remain in the United States — or 11.3 percent — said they plan to live in Connecticut. According to data published by Yale’s Office of Career Strategy, around 12 percent of the class of 2025 stayed in Connecticut, compared to an average of 14 percent in the years since 2016. Rory Bricca ’26 says he hopes to stay in New Haven next year to teach music lessons at the Neighborhood Music School, as well as audition for choirs at the Yale Institute of Sacred Music and work on his own compositions. Bricca was born at the Yale New Haven Hospital and lived in Hamden until he was 10 years old, when his family moved to Tucson, Arizona. But he has “fond memories” of New Haven, he said, “especially East Rock and Wooster Square.” About a month ago, Bricca said he had a “gut feeling” that he wanted to stay in New Haven. “I’m hoping to just have a year where I can stay in this area and stay connected to the relationships I’ve built,” he said. Bricca is also excited by New Haven’s performing arts scene. “Nowhere else in the world could I be going to a friend’s musical or theater performance every week,” he said. “That’s just super fun.” Victor Nguyen ’26 plans to stay in New Haven next year to continue his research at a Yale School of Medicine neuroscience lab. “My current lab aligns with my research goals, and I didn’t want to have to start back at square one in a new location,” Nguyen said. At the same time, he thinks New Haven offers a “nice contrast” to his hometown of Fort Smith, Arkansas. When applying to colleges, he said he wanted to move to a city with a “fast living pace” he is excited to spend at least one more year in New Haven after four years at Yale. Jae-Hee Bae ’26 grew up in Dayton, Ohio, and first came to the east coast as a Yale student four years ago. But now that New Haven feels “comfortable and familiar,” she said, she is not ready to leave. “I think there’s a consensus that people want to leave New Haven after graduation,” Bae said. “But when you’re graduating, there’s a lot of change that happens and having some constant really relieves the pressure of all that change.” She loves that New Haven is “more pedestrian-friendly” than suburban Ohio and that residents are engaged in their communities. “I like that there are a lot of protests that happen on the Green, and there are a lot of events like the 5Ks and the Cherry Blossom Festival,” Bae said. “It shows that organizers really care about making it feel like home.” Like Nguyen, Bae is staying next year to work on mRNA research at the Yale School of Medicine’s Neugebauer Lab and apply to medical schools in the meantime. Many Yale students take a “gap year” before applying to medical school, and for those interested, research opportunities at Yale are “straightforward,” Bae said. “I feel comfortable knowing a handful of my friends are staying here,” Bae added. “I’m super excited to stay involved with a community that I’ve developed a relationship with.” Gia Cook ’26 will be working as a Woodbridge Fellow at Yale’s Office of Public Affairs and Communications. “I’m super-duper excited to be in New Haven,” Cook said. Originally from a suburb of Detroit, Cook said she lived in the “same little yellow house in the same street” her entire life until she came to Yale. “It’s been incredible living here, I don’t really have any dislikes,” Cook said, adding that she appreciated being “somewhere walkable” and having “access to such vibrant nightlife.” Yale University has been the largest employer of graduating students every year since 2016, when the Office of Career Strategy started publishing data on postgraduate plans.

title: After increased pledge, seniors split on whether Yale pays city enough
author: Chantal Eulenstein
text: Beginning next fiscal year, Yale will boost its annual payment to New Haven. Graduating seniors differ on whether they think the increased contribution is appropriate. Two months ago, University President Maurie McInnis and New Haven Mayor Justin Elicker announced an updated town-gown agreement that will see the University give its home city more than $230 million over the next seven years. A survey conducted by the News found that a plurality of graduating seniors — just under 50 percent of 147 respondents — feels that Yale’s new voluntary contribution is either “too little” or “somewhat little.” Elicker said in March that the deal “represents the largest voluntary payment” from any American university to a municipality. Yale will pay New Haven $29.9 million in the next fiscal year, and contributions will increase to $33.6 million by 2033. These sums represent a substantial increase from the previous agreement, under which Yale paid some $23 or $24 million annually. The new payment schedule followed months of negotiations between Yale and New Haven, and the agreement was announced as the class of 2026 looked ahead to graduation. Just over 44 percent of senior respondents said that the new commitment is “appropriate,” and nearly 11 percent said it was “too much” or “somewhat much.” “I think it’s important for Yale to give back to the city it is built in,” Estelle Balsirow ’26 wrote in an email to the News, adding that the survey results suggest that “a lot of seniors agree with that sentiment.” Other seniors were taken aback by the proportion of their classmates who felt that Yale’s contribution to New Haven was excessive. “I found it surprising that nearly 11 percent of people thought that Yale was giving too much money to New Haven,” Colin Kim ’26 wrote in a text to the News. “Yale as an academic institution siphons so many resources like land from the surrounding area, so I personally feel like the school should give more to rehabilitate the structural harms that it has caused to the city.” Yale owns over 850 acres in New Haven, according to CT Insider . Thanks to its status as a nonprofit, the University does not pay property taxes on any buildings that are used for academic purposes. That exemption is protected by an 1834 agreement between Yale and Connecticut. Efforts to push Yale to increase its payment ramped up in the months before the new deal was announced. In January, leaders of Yale’s Local 33, 34 and 35 unions joined city leaders and activists to demand that Yale up its annual contribution to $110 million. And in early March, nearly 500 people attended an online “solidarity summit” organized by New Haven Rising in which speakers denounced Yale for not contributing more. The University is New Haven’s second-largest taxpayer. The new town-gown agreement preserves an existing arrangement in which Yale makes payments equivalent to full property taxes for three years after purchasing a property for academic use, before reducing those payments by 10 percent each year. New initiatives announced alongside the updated agreement in March include a fund to support local nonprofits and a commitment to exploring the possibility of using vacant University land to address Connecticut’s affordable housing crisis . Some seniors believe that Yale’s volunteer contribution to New Haven could increase further. “I’m wondering what determines whether Yale is giving ‘too much,’” Kim wrote, “since I personally think that the quality of education that Yale provides would not suffer at all from the school giving more money to New Haven. In the end, isn’t that all that matters for answering the question of whether Yale pays New Haven ‘too much’ or ‘too little’?” For some seniors, attending Yale meant becoming a New Haven resident — which means that their alma mater needs to give back. “I hope that a stronger relationship from this agreement will form for both Yalies who call New Haven a temporary home and residents who will live here with their families for years to come,” Balsirow wrote. In the University’s first voluntary contribution agreement, signed in 1990, Yale pledged to give around $1.5 million to the city each year.

title: Even after monthslong scandal, ex-chief remembered for community ties
author: Evelyn Ronan
text: It hasn’t been a career-high year for Karl Jacobson. In January, Jacobson, then the chief of the New Haven Police Department, abruptly resigned from his post as chief and later turned himself in on an arrest warrant for two charges of larceny. He allegedly embezzled a combined $85,500 from two city funds, one intended for paying confidential informants in the narcotics unit and the other for the city’s Police Activity League. According to an affidavit, Jacobson bet more than $4 million on online gambling platforms during the time he was allegedly embezzling the funds. In April, the chief pleaded not guilty to the two larceny charges against him. But even as his department moves on from him — on May 5, the city swore in David Zannelli, who was among the three assistant chiefs who confronted Jacobson with allegations of embezzlement — New Haveners remembered his tenure as an era of progressive strides on community engagement. “He had the closest relationship to affected communities of any chief I’ve seen,” Paul Bass ’82, the founder of the New Haven Independent and longtime reporter on local policing, said in a phone interview. “I’ve been shocked at how little I’ve seen of that personal scandal reverberating at a larger scale.” Bass elaborated that the grievances about Jacobson allegedly stealing funds were limited to him and did not taint the department at large. In a phone interview with the News, Mayor Justin Elicker said he didn’t doubt that Jacobson “cared about New Haven.” But he struck a colder tone, emphasizing that the transparency-focused approach that Jacobson championed also extended to his removal. “It’s important to understand the fact that holding Jacobson accountable builds more trust in the community,” Elicker said. “When we talk about accountability, this is what we mean.” According to Bass, it wasn’t policy shifts that marked Jacobson’s term, but his overall “de-emphasization of arrest” and a focus on community ties. “A good policy doesn’t work if you don’t have trust of the community, if you haven’t been in people’s kitchens and living rooms during tough times and helped them navigate crisis,” Bass said. Zannelli, an 18-year veteran of the NHPD, has suggested he wants to continue Jacobson’s legacy of strengthening collaboration between the police and local community. Jacobson’s people-first approach took center stage in the wake of Randy Cox’s arrest . New Haven police arrested Cox on June 19, 2022. He was handcuffed and placed in a police car without a seatbelt . The officer driving the car, Oscar Diaz , made a hard stop, which caused Cox to slam into the front of the vehicle. In body cam footage released promptly after the incident, Cox could be heard saying that his neck felt broken. Diaz called for medical assistance but continued driving instead of waiting for paramedics to arrive and attend to Cox, who was then dragged face down into a holding cell, according to the police report . He could not move and repeatedly tried to get the officers to stop moving him. Officers in the footage can be heard saying Cox was “faking it” and doing “extra s—” before they dragged him out of the vehicle. The officers’ “reckless” treatment of Cox, a New Haven Police Department Internal Affairs report found, led to his ultimate paralysis. Cox is now in a long-term care facility . Jacobson moved to release bodycam footage of the incident and address the officers’ violation of conventional policy immediately after the incident. In March 2023, after the release of the Internal Affairs report, Jacobson called for the dismissal of the involved officers, who were fired in August of that year. “I think the community has asked us to be transparent and swift,” Jacobson said while announcing his choice to call for the officers’ dismissal, according to News coverage from the time. “We have put a lot of thought and investigation into this decision. We want to make sure that everything is done right, so that through this hearing, justice can be done.” Bass speculated that if Jacobson had handled the incident differently, “we would have the potential for riots.” “He had such a great connection with the people who were understandably upset,” Bass said. “He and the mayor got so much good information right away, rather than being defensive, which another chief would’ve been.” Michael Lawlor, a former state representative and a professor at the University of New Haven’s Henry C. Lee College of Criminal Justice and Forensic Sciences, said that Jacobson reached out to Cox’s family immediately after the incident. “That instinct to do something very uncomfortable immediately is a significant attribute of Karl,” Lawlor said. Jacobson later instituted a new policy in reaction to the Cox incident dubbed “ medical Miranda ,” where officers transporting prisoners are required to ask if they need medical attention. Jacobson also co-signed a judge’s decision not to expunge the records of the officers involved in Cox’s arrest. Diaz, the officer driving the police car during the arrest, was later reinstated under Jacobson’s leadership. “He was big on procedural justice,” Lawlor said, describing it as a central aspect of Jacobson’s policing philosophy. “If you’re a cop and you’re dealing with a citizen, it’s extremely important to explain what you’re doing. He really instilled among the rank-and-file cops the importance of this.” Lawlor said Jacobson also emphasized policing strategies that acquainted officers with particular neighborhoods — such as walking beats, in which officers patrol neighborhoods on foot rather than in a car. “When I came to New Haven, I became a better cop and a better person because I walked a beat,” Jacobson said in a WNHH Community Radio interview six months into his term as chief. 15 years on, he still maintained community ties from that time, Jacobson said in the interview. Lawlor said he considers Jacobson’s community-focused approach to be the crux of his term as police chief. “If you’re gonna be a police chief anywhere, you have to be sort of a politician,” Lawlor said.“A lot of cops have a tunnel vision approach to their job, but as a chief, you have to deal with the community, you have to explain things, and you have to be comforting. Karl really had that skillset.” Bass, the longtime reporter on local policing, contrasted Jacobson’s controversy to a 2007 NHPD scandal in which a group of top cops stole thousands of dollars of reserved police department funds , prompting the departure of then-chief Francisco Ortiz. The NHPD appointed James Lewis soon after in 2008, a chief who had never before worked in New Haven and took an “aggressive” approach to policing reminiscent of NHPD’s militaristic crackdown in the 1980s. Lewis was responsible for 20 arrests in his first week as chief. Bass said the fact that the city hired Zannelli “from within,” instead of hiring an outsider, “means this is so different from 2008,” adding that Zannelli maintained his predecessor’s community-focused approach to policing. “Zannelli has really good relationships with people from working in different neighborhoods,” Bass said. “He’s built up an extraordinary level of goodwill and trust in the community.” Karl Jacobson was sworn in as police chief on July 7, 2022.

title: Yale reinstates SAT, ACT requirement after six years of flexible policy
author: Leo Nyberg
text: First-year and transfer applicants to Yale College will be required to submit either their SAT or ACT scores beginning this fall. The change, which Yale announced in a Wednesday evening press release after updating its webpage on standardized testing, reinstates a pre-pandemic mandate after six admissions cycles in which SAT and ACT score submissions were not required. Yale axed the requirement for undergraduate applicants to submit standardized test scores in 2020, then required the submission of either SAT, ACT, International Baccalaureate or Advanced Placement scores under a test-flexible policy beginning in 2024. “Academic excellence is the foundation of the Yale College experience, and, likewise, is the core component of our admissions process,” Yale College Dean Pericles Lewis said in the press release. “SAT and ACT scores are strong predictors of a student’s future Yale academic performance, and, when considered thoughtfully as part of a whole person review, they can help identify well-prepared candidates, especially those from socio-economically disadvantaged backgrounds.” The return to a test-mandatory policy follows a recommendation from the Presidential Council on Yale College Admissions, a nine-member group formed by University President Maurie McInnis in fall 2025 whose membership includes Lewis, other Yale administrators and former New York governor George Pataki ’67. The council’s work follows previous efforts by Yale College to review its admissions processes, including after the Supreme Court ruled in 2023 that affirmative action is unlawful. The group also “considered recent executive orders and guidelines from federal agencies” in its work, according to a Yale webpage . Brown, Cornell, the University of Pennsylvania, Dartmouth and Harvard all require SAT or ACT scores for undergraduate applicants. Princeton will require that applicants report scores from one of the two tests beginning with those seeking enrollment in fall 2028, while Columbia does not require standardized test score submission. According to the undergraduate admissions website , 90 percent of Yale first years who enrolled last fall submitted either SAT or ACT scores. Around two-thirds of those students submitted an AP or IB score, and 61 percent submitted scores from multiple exams. “Academic strength is the admissions committee’s first and most important consideration when evaluating candidates,” Jeremiah Quinlan ’03, the dean of undergraduate admissions, said in the press release . “This central pillar of our work has remained constant, even as our policy on tests has changed in recent years.” Quinlan, through a spokesperson, declined to comment beyond his quotes in the release. The council’s review of admissions policies and procedures concluded at the end of the 2025-26 academic year. According to its website , the group issued guidance “to prevent misrepresentation on applications and increase transparency across the admissions process—a focus shared by the Committee on Trust in Higher Education.” The decision to reinstate mandatory SAT or ACT score submission also followed the Committee on Trust in Higher Education’s recommendation in its April report that the University clarify admissions standards. The report floated the requirement of minimum SAT scores for admission or a Yale-specific entrance exam. The Trust Committee identified hyper-selective admissions as one of three “immediate” factors causing declining public trust in Yale and higher education. “When selective admissions seem so inexplicable — or, worse, tilted in ways that benefit the already advantaged — it should come as no surprise that many Americans do not trust the process,” the committee’s report reads. “We recommend that the university embrace a standard of candor: It should only use criteria for admission that it is willing to describe publicly and defend openly.” Following the report’s release, University President Maurie McInnis wrote in a message to the Yale community that she had accepted many of the committee’s findings and referred its recommendations on admissions to the Presidential Council on Yale College Admissions. In a May interview with the News, McInnis said she did not give the council a deadline for when the group should make recommendations. Yale College accepted 4.2 percent of applicants in its most recent admissions cycle.

title: Introducing the Yale Daily News mobile app
author: The Yale Daily News
text: The Yale Daily News is pleased to announce that our new mobile app is now available to download on the iOS App Store. On the app, you can read our latest news articles and opinion columns, listen to our podcasts, play our games and browse our archives. Readers can enjoy our coverage in dark mode, view their reading history and save articles to read later. App users will also be the first to learn about breaking news and top stories, through push notifications. The app was developed by the News’ technology directors Midhun Sadanand ’27 and Raymond Hou ’27, with Seth Goldin ’26 and Yassin Mudawi ’28, in the “Advanced Software Engineering” course during the spring 2026 semester. Sadanand, Hou and Goldin previously led the redesign of the News’ website , which launched in January. With gratitude for their effort, we’re excited to invite you to read and listen to our coverage on the app. You can download it here.

title: Dramat makes ‘fetch’ happen in riotous rendition of ‘Mean Girls’
author: Cameron Nye
text: Ah, Commencement! The time when graduating Yalies gaze upon the sea of white folding chairs on Old Campus and look back on four prosperous academic years. Friends and family fly in from across the globe to celebrate the students who are ready to break out of their blue and white chrysalides and spread their beautiful, corporate wings. It’s a time of rejoicing and reflection for most, but for the Yale University Dramatic Association, Commencement means one thing and one thing only — a fast, frantic and full-throttle musical production. In the downtime between finals and Commencement weekend, an ambitious group of thespians work for ten days to produce a show in celebration of the year’s theater season and the seniors that will go on to bigger, brighter stages. This year, director Will Sussbauer ’27, producer Jack Dozier ’27, music producer Lyndon Chen ’28 and stage manager Rena Kawasaki ’27 teamed up to present a musical based on one of this generation’s cultural pillars — “Mean Girls.” If you haven’t seen Tina Fey’s original 2004 satiric film, close out of this tab and go watch it. It’s 97 minutes of unadulterated Y2K drama and witty dialogue that remains quotable and iconic even 22 years later. After receiving critical acclaim, “Mean Girls” was turned into a musical, hitting Broadway in 2017 and bringing Fey’s beloved characters into the age of social media. The musical includes all the quips and digs that made the original iconic, and stays true to the film’s plot: 16-year-old Cady Heron moves from Kenya to the United States and, after years of being homeschooled, realizes the lions of her youth did not prepare her for the beastly monsters of high school. She’s initially befriended by two social outcasts but is quickly noticed by the Plastics, a gaggle of popular girls who are North Shore High’s apex predators. Cady is thrown into the maws of popularity, and quickly turns from naive newcomer to manipulative mean girl. The musical gained mostly positive reviews and then gained a frighteningly dull film adaptation in 2024. The production, which I saw Friday night, opened to a friendly audience of supportive suitemates, weepy parents and reminiscent peers. The next day, Dozier informed me via text message that the production had become the “highest grossing show in recent history” staged by the Dramat. A very fetch development. Kudos to them! Staying true to the 2017 production, the University Theatre has been transformed into a minimalistic high school — desks come and go as classes change and cafeteria tables are wheeled off once the lunch bell has rung. The back of the stage remains mostly illuminated a fittingly vibrant shade of pink, backlighting slits of drywall decoupaged in a postered collage of song lyrics, inspirational quotes and Uno reverse cards. The rendition incorporated some changes to the show’s book. Lyrics that contained homophobic slurs and problematic language were tweaked, creating a more inclusive environment without taking away from the show’s intended cruelty. Sussbauer did incorporate some more campy choices that leaned into the melodramatic undertones of the comedic musical. Damian Leigh, the show’s resident flamboyant homosexual, cheekily directs his “sexually active band geeks” line to the pit orchestra, most of whose members give toothy grins and wave. Meanwhile, during Regina George’s fiery revenge tune “World Burn,” her notorious hot pink burn book — which contains The Plastics’ nasty notes about their peers — is lowered down like a clunky weapon of mass destruction. Papers then begin cascading down from the rafters onto the stage, picked up by an anxious and enraged ensemble who has learned that their business has been aired to the entire school. It’s choices like this that make “Mean Girls” a gleeful riot, leaning into the absurdity of stringent social hierarchies in which gossip literally rains from the sky while remaining grounded in the lockered halls of a midwestern high school. That balance is further maintained by the performances of the shows’ cast. The Plastics are brought to life by Hadley Murphey ’29 as the tyrannical Regina George, Sasha Fedderly ’27 as the anxious, antsy Gretchen Weiners and Nneka Moweta ’27 as the delightfully dimwitted Karen Smith. It’s clear Murphey studied at the “Reneé Rapp School of Regina George Portrayals,” because her mix of conceited “cursive” singing — heard in “Geeoorggggeeeeehhhh” — and piercing vibrato is a ferocious ode to Rapp’s performance both in the 2024 movie and on Broadway. Meanwhile, Moweta’s impeccable comedic timing elevates Karen from just another “dumb blonde” to a girl who understands the unspoken workings of high school social hierarchies. The social rejects-turned-narrators — comprised of the jaded Janis Sarkisian, played by Olivia Ridley ’26, and the showy “too gay to function” Damian brought by Sebastian Gervase ’27 — drive the show with grungey “F YOU” anthems and electric numbers reminiscent of classic Broadway showtunes. Gervase’s numbers, in particular, feature showstopping choreography — fluttering jazz hands and showy kicks that infuse an intoxicating intricacy to the production, all courtesy of choreographer Sakura Grashow ’28. Caught in the middle of these two worlds is Cady Heron, brought to life by Amelia Gallegos ’29. She captures Cady excellently, portraying the bright eyed, naive math nerd with remarkable earnestness before embodying her character’s curdle into a conniving, calculated bully. Her bright soprano lends itself well to festive, ingenuous numbers like “It Roars” and “Stupid With Love,” in which her developing crush on hunk Aaron Samuels, played by Abram Knott ’27, fills her with “calcu-lust.” With only ten days to become a fully fledged musical, “Mean Girls” accomplished a great deal. Many of the characters were fully realized with tics and quirks that captured the itchy awkwardness of teenage angst. The production, however, was hampered by its technical inconsistency. Even from a seat relatively close to the stage, lines and runs were lost as microphones crunched and sputtered. The lighting wavered, too, with spotlights often scrambling to catch up to actors already speaking in shadow. With more time, undoubtedly, these wrinkles could be ironed out, but for the rehearsal period it was afforded, “Mean Girls” is practically a triumph. What this cast and crew was able to pull off in a short amount of time celebrates the commitment thespians give to their craft — which in turn honors the seniors who have given their all to the University Theatre they’ve called home for the past four years. “Mean Girls” makes sense as a Commencement musical because its story has already cemented itself in the cultural imagination as funny and sharply quotable. But beneath the pink plastic surface is a tale about insecurity and the exhausting performance of trying to be liked. Those questions do not disappear after high school — or even college. We still navigate cliques, self-comparison, friendship fallout and the pressure to package ourselves into something more impressive than we feel. At a moment meant to celebrate moving forward, “Mean Girls” offers a reminder that growing up is about learning how to be kinder and more honest — a fitting sendoff for Yale’s seniors before they contemplate acquiescing to the Regina Georges of the world. “Mean Girls” will run until May 17.

title: Seniors reflect on their experience within Yale’s music scene
author: Gemard Guery
text: Every fall, many first years at Yale weigh how they’ll take part in one of the University’s largest selling points, the music scene, through everything from a capella rush to Yale Symphony Orchestra auditions. Now on their way out, four seniors spoke to the News about their participation in Yale’s music scene, its impact on their Yale experience and what’s next for them. The singer-songwriter Zaida Rio Polanco ’26 has been passionate about music for as long as she can remember. An environmental studies major from White Plains, New York, Polanco grew up with a dad and brother who were both singer-songwriters, contributing to Polanco’s decision to do the same. Polanco grew up watching her dad play for weddings and her brother, who is 11 years older than her, play drums for her dad’s band. “There was always music playing in the house, and they let me find my own genre that I liked,” Polanco said in an interview with the News. Polanco was writing songs when she was as young as three years old. Her dad ran the songwriters club at her high school, and Polanco would perform at open mics as early as third grade alongside high school aged students. By the time she entered Yale, Polanco had already released an album with 10 songs. Polanco was drawn to Yale’s theater and acting scene when she first arrived at Yale but knew she wanted to continue singing and songwriting. “I just knew I wanted to keep going, no matter where I ended up,” said Polanco. “I just got lucky I was able to go to a school that had a student record label that had a bunch of people willing to help bring this music to life.” Polanco was a member of the Shades of Yale for her first three years at Yale and a frequent participant in theater on campus . She was also an active part of 17o1 Records, the student record label that helped Polanco release many of her own projects. For a while, Polanco was reluctant to pursue a career as an artist, but her time at Yale showed her that it is possible. “I’ve met people that turned this pipe dream of going into this career as an artist into something that could actually happen,” Polanco said. “I love the fact that I can hear my best friends performing at Chi Psi from my bedroom window. There are not many places you can do that, be able to have that experience where you’re literally living in a small little bubble of performing artists. I’ll miss that a lot.” While she enjoyed her experience as an environmental studies major, it is not what she hopes to pursue professionally. Following commencement, Zaida plans to pursue music in Los Angeles while working in marketing with a company that has a creative entry point. She also hopes to continue auditioning for theater and film. The bassist For Thara Joseph ’26, music has always been an important part of her identity. A bassist from Jacksonville, Florida, Joseph began playing the piano regularly in elementary school. “Every day, I knew I had at last ninety minutes of playing per day,” said Joseph in an interview. “That really started in sixth grade, and it stayed consistently until I graduated, so it was very intensive in that way.” Courtesy of Thara Joseph Joseph attended a magnet arts program for both middle school and high school, where her passion for music intensified. She was a part of her city symphony orchestra and youth symphony orchestra, where she played the French horn, and in her senior year of high school, she was accepted into a jazz combo through the Jazz Education Network, which connects young musicians with professionals in the field. But as Joseph was preparing to come to Yale, she anticipated putting music on the back burner, focusing on academics instead and seeing where Yale would take her. “I didn’t know if I was going to be able to play and practice at the same frequencies that I had been for many, many years,” Joseph said. But when Joseph found herself unable to practice playing music every day during the First-Year Scholars program, she said she realized how little she enjoyed that experience. In fact, during the program, Joseph went to a local pawn shop and bought a bass bundled with an amp so she could practice. “Looking back, even that moment should have clued me into how important music was as a core part of my daily routine and how hard it would have been to just drop it,” Joseph said. Joseph has been a bassist for the Yale Jazz Ensemble during the entirety of her undergraduate experience, playing in musical productions like “The Wiz” and “For Colored Girls.” She has regularly gigged professionally with graduate students at the Yale School of Music, and has performed for events held through Yale’s Schwarzman Center and opened for celebrities such as Cécile McLorin Salvant and John Legend. Originally, Joseph came to Yale planning to double-major in both global affairs and music. Instead, she tested out classes across many different fields and departments until she landed on Ethics, Politics and Economics. She said her interdisciplinary major, alongside her music experience, opened her eyes to the nature of Yale’s liberal arts education. “I realized I don’t have to choose one path here. I feel like I’m the type of person where I want to have my hands in many different pockets,” Joseph said. “Because of all that time I was able to still invest in my craft, it really had me making that shift into considering pursuing music in a more serious and intentional way.” Joseph says she will miss Yale’s vibrant community the most from her music experience as she leaves Yale. “You get to meet so many different people, and a lot of people, I don’t think I would have ever crossed paths otherwise,” said Joseph. “There’s such an emphasis on collaboration here when it comes to the arts, where it’s music that feels good, it sounds good, and it’s enjoyable. I’ll miss that a lot.” Joseph hopes to someday work in the more executive side of the music industry. “There’s a need for more artists as industry leaders, so that we can increase artist advocacy and bridge what can sometimes be misunderstandings between creatives and executives,” Joseph said. Following graduation, Joseph will be staying in New Haven as a Chauncey Fellow, where she will be developing a service-based arts project. She plans to continue gigging throughout her time as a fellow. The Whiffenpoof For John Colbert ’26, the Yale journey was unlike what he originally anticipated. A humanities major, he originally matriculated in fall 2020, as a member of the class of 2024, but took a gap year due to the pandemic to get more out of his Yale experience. Colbert played the drums often in his hometown of Montclair, New Jersey, gigging with jazz bands through his time in middle school and high school. He also participated in an after school and summer program where local musicians help youth create their own ensembles. Colbert’s older sister attended Yale and recommended that he try out for Yale’s Tangled Up in Blue — or TUIB — a folk music group. While Colbert’s sister was not a part of the group, he said she always enjoyed watching them perform. “It was a big event for her and her friends to go to every year. She was like, ‘You should do this.’ Colbert said in an interview with the News. “So then I auditioned, and that’s been a big part of my time at Yale. That’s really what made me take singing more seriously as something that I did here.” Colbert eventually served as the musical director of TUIB during his junior year, in addition to participating in bands and theater on campus. He also became a member of the Whiffenpoof class of 2025, where Yale seniors take gap years to perform worldwide, something he said he had no intention of being a part of originally. It wasn’t until a friend from TUIB encouraged Colbert to audition after sharing her own experience that Colbert considered becoming a Whiffenpoof. “The idea of being a professional amateur musician or an amateur professional was kind of appealing to me,” Colbert said. “I spent so much time doing music stuff here that I was like, I really wouldn’t mind doing essentially just that for a whole year.” Colbert enjoyed his time as a Whiffenpoof, which allowed him not only to have a fun experience but also gave him the opportunity to reflect on what he wanted his life after Yale to look like. For Colbert, music was also a means of finding his community at Yale. “Being involved in music here was a wonderful way to be engaged with campus life,” he said. “I think playing music together is a really interesting and unique way to form bonds with people and it means a lot to me.” As Colbert prepares to leave Yale, he said he already feels nostalgic about his time in the music scene and how it became a serious endeavor. “In some ways, the things that I’ll miss about playing music here are the things that I’ll miss about not being as young as I am now,” he said. “I’ll miss the strange, infinite possibility of college that is so, so alluring when you’re here.” Following commencement, Colbert hopes to move to New York and continue playing with his band, Dog Boy, which he began during his time at Yale. There, he hopes to engage in the city’s art scene while he continues searching for a job separate from his creative endeavors that he finds fulfilling on a personal level. The concert pianist Paul Ji ’26 has been a well known and distinguished musician for years. A philosophy and music double major from France, Ji has been playing the piano since he was 4 years old. Ji participated in many international competitions when he was younger, winning almost all of them, which further fueled his passion for music, specifically the piano. “I did the Steinway competition for seven years, consecutively, and fortunately, I won all of them and that motivated me to continue,” he said in an interview. “But at one point, I decided that music was much more than competitions, and there was so much joy to be found in connecting with people through music.” Courtesy of Paul Ji When Ji was 15, he recorded his first album after winning a national competition in France. Ji’s second album, the one he recorded while at Yale, will be coming out in early 2027. Much of Ji’s Yale experience was finding the perfect balance between his professional piano career and his academic interests. “I very much appreciate the liberal arts, and I really wanted to delve deep into philosophy,” he said. “I also felt that it would help my music, that it would give me more insights and more intellectual experience.” The majority of his sophomore year was devoted to learning the work of Polish composer Frédéric Chopin. Ji’s next album, recorded in Sprague Hall, consists of the full 24 preludes by Chopin, accompanied by other pieces. This year, Ji had the opportunity to perform with the Yale Symphony Orchestra as the winner of the William Waite competition , where he played Chopin’s second concerto, a romantic piece he performed on Valentine’s Day. Ji’s talents have brought him all over the world throughout his time at Yale. During his sophomore year, he performed his debut recital in Paris at the Théâtre des Champs-Élysées. Since then, he’s played in Vienna in spaces where famous musicians such as Beethoven have performed. While Ji wasn’t a consistent part of any music group, his experience continuously demonstrated to him how strong the musical community at Yale is. He often participated in graduate piano classes in Sprague Hall and would play alongside experts in the field. “Meeting musicians who are also very intellectually driven and invested in many, many different fields has been a gift,” Ji said. Ji hopes to continue to build his career as a concert pianist while simultaneously finding solutions and strategies to make classical music more accessible. “It’s really such a great art that can help people transcend, and I think more people deserve to know it,” he said. Following graduation, Ji will be pursuing his master’s of music in piano at the Juilliard School.

title: Under Bundy, drama school went tuition-free and saw a cultural shift
author: Kiva Bank
text: The office of James Bundy DRA ’95, who is retiring from his posts at the David Geffen School of Drama and the Yale Repertory Theatre, is set to be cleared out by the end of June. But for now, the room’s blue walls are still covered with framed posters from student thesis productions Bundy has seen throughout his 24-year-long tenure — the longest of any Drama School dean to date. “The theater is filled with mementos,” Bundy, who has served in his role as dean and as the Yale Rep’s artistic director since 2002, said in an interview with the News. His office is laden with keepsakes, each object hinting at a piece of his life and work at Yale. Beneath his coffee table sits a “Shakespeare Lexicon and Quotation Dictionary,” a nod to the “Acting Shakespeare” undergraduate course he will continue to teach after stepping down this summer. Nearby is a gold-painted leg signed by an Estonian clown who performed at the Yale Rep this past season, and on Bundy’s desk sits a Funko Pop gifted to him by Nancy Yao, the assistant dean of student life at the drama school. The figurine is styled in his likeness — bald, wearing clear-framed glasses — and the diploma it holds echoes Bundy himself, who earned his MFA in directing from the School of Drama in 1995. “The school completely changed the direction of my career, and opened up a world of opportunities,” he said. Prior to his appointment to the Drama School, Bundy served as the artistic director of Great Lakes Theater Festival, as well as the associate producing director of the Acting Company in New York City and as managing director of Cornerstone Theater Company. Posters for two different student productions of “Hedda Gabler” — the last play that Bundy himself directed at the Yale Rep while serving as its artistic director — hang in his office. During his tenure at the Rep, he also directed productions of Shakespeare’s “Hamlet” and “All’s Well That Ends Well.” Liz Diamond, the chair of the directing program at the David Geffen School of Drama, told the News she is “a little bit in denial” about the change in leadership at the school. She said was glad to see Bundy “pour his heart and soul” into the Yale Rep’s production of “Hedda Gabler.” “I was really moved by the work,” she said in a phone interview with the News. “I’m sure that was partly because I knew it was his last production as dean and artistic director.” Diamond said she first met Bundy in fall 1992, when he was one of four students in her directing seminar. She described him as “surpassingly conscientious,” an “indefatigable researcher” and a “wonderful thinker.” Catherine Sheehy DRA ’92 ’99, who is currently the chair of dramaturgy and dramatic criticism at the Drama School and a dramaturg at the Yale Rep, was a first-year faculty member when Bundy was in his final year as a student. She said she can still recall his 1995 thesis production of “Three Sisters.” “He created these images that were really strong,” Sheehy said. “I can still see Protopopov in his white suit, sitting and smoking.” She said that Bundy’s training as an actor enabled him to understand players better as a director, and that in her mind, “he was always sort of an old soul.” Sheehy recalled that Bundy balanced his studies at the drama school with his responsibilities as a husband and a young father. “I remember him wheeling around his kids at the time,” she said. Bundy said that his time as a student at the Drama School aided him when he took his place at its helm, helping inform his understanding of the school’s potential. When he stepped into the deanship, Bundy said that both the search committee that had appointed him and then-University president Richard Levin ’72 ’74 were clear in communicating their priorities for the school. He said there was a general agreement to rank financial aid as the top priority, followed by faculty compensation and new facilities. “24 years later, we still haven’t broken ground on that building, but we're close,” Bundy joked, referring to the Dramatic Arts building set to be completed in 2029. The seven-floor structure will house the Yale Repertory Theater, the David Geffen School of Drama and the undergraduate Theater, Dance and Performance Studies department in one space. “James willed this building into being,” Sheehy said. “James has had to have extraordinary persistence. The administration has changed. The president has changed, the provost has changed more than once.” At the same time, Bundy has been driven by his desire to decrease student indebtedness, which he said was a major problem when he first began his tenure. According to Bundy, debt for School of Drama students in 1982 averaged $10,000, or roughly $34,000 in today’s dollars, and the average debt for the class of 2005 was around $45,000, or $75,000 today. The average student today graduates from the school with about $16,000 in debt — around a 50 percent net reduction from 1982 and an 80 percent net reduction from 2005. Sheehy said that when she graduated from the drama school in 1992, her student loan payment was $575 per month, which she characterized as “enormous.” She said that the financial aid program did not offer much grant aid at the time, but that when Bundy arrived as dean, he immediately started working to increase financial aid. Diamond said that the first full faculty meeting under Bundy’s leadership marked a change from the previous dean’s style. “We really didn’t have very lively faculty meetings under the previous dean,” Diamond said, but under Bundy, “we began with a physical warm-up led by the acting teacher who taught movement.” “For the first time in history, faculty and staff of the drama school were jumping up and down together and sweating,” Diamond said. She said that when Bundy led a discussion to determine the school’s priorities during the first five years of his tenure, the desire to increase financial aid for students was unanimous amongst faculty. “He pursued that goal assiduously, and it took the better part of nearly 20 years,” Diamond said. She described Bundy’s skills as those of “remarkable systems analyst,” which enabled him to establish a plan to go tuition-free. In 2021, nearly twenty years into Bundy’s deanship, entertainment mogul David Geffen donated $150 million to the Drama School. The gift allowed the school to become tuition-free for all full-time students in perpetuity, and the school was renamed in Geffen’s honor. Before the donation, Bundy oversaw a need-based student aid program, according to Sheehy, and also played a key role in establishing 70 of the school’s 91 named scholarships. In 2008, Bundy helped facilitate funding for living stipends and summer support for playwriting students through a bequest from playwright Eugene O’Neill and Carlotta Monterey, which he said made Yale’s playwriting program competitive with other fully-funded MFA programs. As a result, all students in the playwriting program — of which there are three per year — receive stipends to cover their living expenses during both the academic year and summer months, in addition to full-tuition scholarships. Chantal Rodriguez, associate dean of the Drama School, said in a phone interview with the News that Bundy’s dedication “to decreasing the overall indebtedness of alumni” has formed part of his legacy. During the first year of the pandemic, the Drama School saved roughly $3 million in production costs because it did not put on any plays. The school then used the excess funds toward tuition and living stipends to offer a fourth year for students in the classes of 2021, 2022 and 2023 free of charge, Bundy said. Diamond also praised Bundy for his dedication to establishing a shared leadership model among the associate and assistant deans, which promotes collective decision-making. “You cannot make theater if you are a person who insists on flying solo,” Diamond said. “It’s a form of practicing for civilization, and I think that the fact that James, in his own practice as a leader of our school and theater, strove to lead collaboratively, was remarkable.” Bundy also brought about a cultural shift at the school, according to Sheehy. For years, the acting program followed a longstanding tradition of admitting 10 men and six women each year, based on the belief that classical repertory, particularly Shakespeare, required more male actors, Sheehy said. But students argued that the policy was both discriminatory and no longer beneficial to their training, and Bundy removed the quota. Evan Yionoulis ’82 DRA ’85 was recently announced as Bundy’s successor. The outgoing dean characterized Yionoulis as his former mentor, as he worked as her teaching assistant during the summers of 1993 and 1995 when she taught acting for a Yale program. Yionoulis, who previously served as the chair of acting at the drama school, also served on the search committee when Bundy was hired, he said. “She asked me, to the effect of, ‘How would you want people to describe your leadership style?’” he said, recalling the time when Yionoulis was involved in his selection. “I said that I would want to lead with my heart.” Diamond said Bundy is known to read the application of every single incoming student as well as memorize their names and proper pronunciation before orientation. As of last fall, the school enrolled 240 students, according to the Office of Institutional Research. “That is just an example of his deep commitment to this community, and it makes everybody step up,” Diamond said. Bundy also hosts an annual barbecue in the backyard of his house in Westville, a tradition he said started when he moved into the home with his family around the time his deanship began. He said he recalled thinking, “Oh, the whole school could fit in this backyard.” “It’s a nice way for people to be together at the outset,” he said. “We’ve had 10,000 guests at our house over the course of the decades, and that’s felt like a really meaningful experience.” In April, Diamond said, the school was able to return the favor and host a farewell party in celebration of Bundy with all students, faculty and staff. “There were a gazillion balloons that were shaped like hearts, which has extra meaning for a dean who had at least two open heart surgeries during his tenure,” she said. Sheehy said that Bundy’s “open-heartedness” and “open door” policy as a dean will be hardest to replace. “I always think that it’s a sign of good character if you’re willing to be a fool for love,” Sheehy said. “James is willing to be a fool for his love of this institution, and he’s willing to be humble in that way and enjoy a joke and see the funny side.” Bundy graduated from Harvard College in 1981.

title: Bulldogs return to NCAA tournament after second-place Ivy finish
author: Eli Ratner
text: Yale women’s crew will return to the national stage after showing strength at last weekend’s Ivy League championship and earning an at-large bid at Tuesday’s NCAA selection show. No. 7 nationally-ranked Yale will make its fifth straight appearance at the tournament with its fifth-seeded first varsity eight, sixth-seeded second varsity eight and seventh-seeded first varsity four crews. With a national title on the line in Gainesville, Georgia, 22 teams will take the water. The Bulldogs’ first varsity eight won the national title last year, and the team narrowly finished second to Stanford. This year, Yale’s first varsity eight boat, the defending national champion, will compete in Heat 4, facing off against the University of Massachusetts, Rutgers University, Princeton, the University of Miami and the University of Rhode Island. The second varsity eight boat will take on Boston University, the University of Miami, the University of Virginia, Oregon State University and Jacksonville University in Heat 3, and in the first varsity four, Yale will compete in Heat 2 against the University of Texas, the University of California, Berkeley, Boston University and the University Michigan. Tuesday’s at-large bid follows the Bulldogs’ strong showing at the Ivy League Championship last weekend. All Yale crews medaled, with the first varsity four and second varsity four taking home gold, the first varsity eight, second varsity eight and third varsity eight winning silver and the third varsity four making a bronze finish. With 82 points, Yale won the team points title for the second consecutive year, narrowly surpassing Princeton by two points. The Tigers, who won the conference championship, earned the Ivy League’s automatic bid. In addition to Yale, two other Ivies, Brown and Columbia, also earned at-large berths. Now, the Bulldog crews look to avenge the narrow loss to the Tigers at the conference championship and return to New Haven with new hardware, cementing their position as one of the nation’s top programs. The Elis will hit the water on Friday, May 29.

title: Yale heads to regionals after beating Brown, capturing Ivy Pennant
author: Justin Leahy
text: The Yale Bulldogs advanced to the NCAA regionals after defeating the Brown Bears 7-5 on Sunday afternoon in the final round of the Ivy League Tournament. With the win, Yale concluded a 3-0 sweep of the double-elimination tournament at George H.W. Bush ’48 Field. This year’s rise to regionals marks the Bulldogs’ first since 2017 and seventh in program history. The Bulldogs entered the tournament having won a dramatic second consecutive Ivy regular season title. Entering their last regular season series of the year against Dartmouth earlier this month, they needed a three-game sweep to take the title. They did just that, winning the regular-season Ivy title and earning the right to host the Ivy League Tournament. To reach the tournament championship game on their home turf, Yale knocked off last year’s champions, Columbia, 12-3 on Friday and this year’s runner-up, Brown, 5-2 on Saturday, before defeating Brown again on Sunday afternoon. “All the things this team has accomplished and worked through this year have all led to this,” captain Davis Hanson ’26 wrote in a text message to the News. “It’s even more special that we all get to go compete together for a championship.” Hanson emphasized the Bulldogs’ strong mindset heading into the tournament after Yale had dropped two of three to Brown and lost or tied every game versus Columbia in the regular season. “With Brown and Columbia, we really adapted the mentality that we owe those teams our best performance,” Hanson wrote in a text message to the News. “Those are two really good teams this year, and they got the better of us in the regular season, but everyone on our team wanted to play them again in the playoffs. We wanted to show them what we truly are.” The lean, light-throwing righty Daniel Cohen ’26 started for the Bulldogs on Sunday. Although Cohen had been dominant all season, compiling a sub-5 ERA, he struggled in his first two innings, giving up three runs and failing to record a sixth out. Though Yale was down 0-3 heading into the bottom of the second, Yale pieced together a rally. Garrett Larsen ’27, Kaiden Dossa ’27 and Chris DiPrima ’27 provided clutch back-to-back-to-back hits, eventually leveling the score at 3-3. After Cohen was pulled in the second inning, Teo Spadaccini ’27 came into the game. Spadaccini served up nearly six innings of one-run control, setting a near-impeccable tone for the afternoon. The Bulldogs went on to score four more unanswered runs, backed up by clutch doubles from Hanson, Dossa and Jack Dauer ’28. When Spadaccini faced a little trouble at the end of his performance, Ben Winslow ’27, a reliever who has been dominant all year, came in to mop up, instead walking two straight batters.
