Jack Ohman ’28, last season’s Ivy League Pitcher of the Year who had started on Friday, then came on in relief. He quickly forced three outs, walking only one batter, limiting the bases-loaded, no out damage to just two runs and helping Yale keep their lead. The next inning, Ohman closed out the game in dominant fashion, securing the Bulldogs the game and the Ivy League title. Winning the Ivy League meant especially more for Hanson, who tore his ACL in practice before the start of last season. “To me, personally, this game means everything,” Hanson wrote in a text message to the News before the championship contest. In Friday’s first game of the tournament, Yale faced No. 4 ranked Columbia. During the regular season, Yale struggled versus Columbia, going 0-2-1 in New York. However, Yale stood firm. Ohman tossed five innings of two-run ball on the mound. Ohman also stranded six runners and struck out five, often hitting speeds of 96 and 97 miles per hour, with many MLB scouts watching in the stands. After Ohman’s two runs, the Bulldog offense put up a concerted effort to back up their ace. With two outs, DiPrima hit a bases-clearing double, pushing the Bulldogs ahead 6-2. Spadaccini stood sharp on the mound in relief, retiring all nine batters he faced to carry Yale through the eighth inning. Yale then added four more runs in the bottom of the eighth to close out the 12-3 win. Dossa, an ALl-Ivy honoree, reached base four times, stole two bags and extended his on-base streak to 35 games. Dossa kept his perfect 28-for-28 stolen base record for the year. Another All-Ivy honoree, Larsen, who led the Ivy League in batting average this year (.389) added an RBI double in the eighth and also scored three times. In game two on Saturday, Tate Evans ’26 — who was named as the new Ivy League Pitcher of the Year on Thursday, succeeding Ohman — threw seven innings of one-run ball against the third-ranked Brown Bears. Evans tossed a career-high 123 pitches and struck out five Bears. During the regular season, Yale had lost two of three against Brown. But again, Yale was able to overcome the odds. The game was tied through the first five innings. However, the offense proved strong yet again. In the sixth inning, Larsen legged out an infield single, swiped second, then moved to third on a sacrifice bunt by DiPrima. Dauer then lined a double down the line, driving in Larsen and giving Yale the lead. To end the game, the workhorse reliever Winslow closed out the final two innings, securing the 5-2 win. The victory marked Evans’ seventh win of the season, placing him on top of the conference. Pablo Rodriguez ’27 summed up the Bulldogs’ no-quit mentality in a text message to the News. “Every pitch, the ball might not always fall our way, but when we stick with it and grind out every at-bat, eventually we’re going to get the results we want,” Rodriguez wrote. Yale will learn its NCAA Tournament regional assignment on May 25 after the bracket is revealed on ESPN2.

title: Seniors led first playoff run in history, four victories over Harvard
author: Brody Gilkison
text: The bar for making a splash in Yale’s football history is high. The Bulldogs have 18 college football national championships, the most in the history of the sport. Walter Camp, class of 1880, known colloquially as the father of football , headlines a deep list of accomplished Yale alumni in the sport’s chronology. But the seniors still managed to leave their mark on the program throughout a career that ended with Yale’s first playoff run in history. After joining a team that had gone 5–5 in its previous season and ended its year with a heartbreaking loss to Harvard, the now-seniors saw a stark contrast during their rookie season. Although Team 149 dropped its first game of the season to Holy Cross, it went on to win eight of its final nine games, including a 19-14 victory over Harvard, and became the Ivy League champions. Among the only members of the class of 2026 to get meaningful playing time on Team 149 were Josh Pitsenberger ’26 and Inumidun Ayo-Durojaiye ’26, both of whom would become foundational pieces on future Yale teams. In the now-seniors’ sophomore season, Yale’s 150th football team took the field looking to defend their conference title. The Bulldogs got off to a slow start, losing their first two games to Holy Cross and Cornell. But from there, the Bulldogs won six of their next seven games, entering the week of The Game with a 6–3 record. At The Game, Yale took down the rival Harvard Crimson to win the Ivy League Championship for the second year in a row. With the win, the class of 2026 were now two for two in conference championships — and against the team from the North. The 2024 season brought adversity, with two early conference losses handicapping Team 151’s chances of winning its third straight Ivy League championship. However, the Bulldogs persevered, fighting all sixty minutes of every game that season. This time around, Team 151 entered the week of The Game with one goal and one goal only. Yale, with a record of 6–3 overall and 3–3 in conference play, was mathematically eliminated from winning the conference title. The only thing left to do in the 2024 season was to take down the Crimson for the third year in a row. In an absolute barn burner that Yale led the entire game, the Bulldogs upset the Football Championship Subdivision No. 17 Harvard, winning 34-29. Among the top moments of the game was Pitsenberger’s unforgettable 39-yard touchdown catch and score , where he seemingly juked out half of Harvard’s defense en route to the end zone. It was the class of 2026’s third straight victory over the Crimson. The following offseason was unlike anything Ivy League football had ever seen before. In a shocking announcement , the Ivy League adopted a rule change allowing its teams to play in the postseason. This meant that, for the first time ever, the conference champion would receive an automatic berth in the Football Championships Subdivision playoffs following the end of the regular season. Yale, a program that has long held the record for most national championships, was given a chance to compete for their first title since 1944, when the Ivy Group Agreement was created. With the stakes now as high as they had ever been before, Team 152 entered the 2025 season led by team captain Josh Pitsenberger. The Bulldogs started strong, with two straight wins to kick off the season. Then, Yale fell 31-13 to Lehigh University, led by then-coach Kevin Cahill. The following week, Yale’s defense surrendered a last-second game-winning field goal to Dartmouth to fall to 2–2 for the season. The Bulldogs bounced back a week later with a 40-point win over Stonehill College to right the ship. From there on, it was smooth sailing for Team 152 as they rattled off four consecutive victories over Ivy League opponents to set the stage for the 141st playing of The Game. No. 8 Harvard entered the final week of the regular season with a perfect 9–0 record, the heavy favorite to be the inaugural Ivy League automatic qualifier. But Yale, with just one conference loss up to this point in the season, still had good reason to keep its Ivy title hopes alive. A win over Harvard would have put both teams at 6–1 in conference play, with the head-to-head tiebreaker then favoring Yale. Yale stripped Harvard on the first play from scrimmage, deep in Crimson territory, and never looked back. A quick score and another field goal put the Bulldogs up 10-0 nine minutes into the first quarter. A few drives later, Pitsenberger punched the ball in for the first of his three touchdowns on the day to put the Elis up 17-0. While Harvard finally managed to put points on the board, in the following drive, wideout Nico Brown ’26 hauled in a 64-yard touchdown pass, sending the Bowl into pandemonium. Yale maintained its lead throughout the game, and the Bulldogs secured their third Ivy title in four years. In addition to their fourth-straight win over the Crimson, Yale had also punched their ticket to their first-ever Football Championship Subdivision playoff appearance. The first-round playoff matchup saw Team 152 traveling to take on Youngstown State University. Down 42-14 halfway through the third quarter, the Bulldogs pulled off the unthinkable. Overcoming a 0.2 percent win probability, Yale came all the way back to knock off the Penguins 43-42 . In an instant classic, the Bulldogs won their first playoff game in school history. The following week, Yale’s Cinderella story came to a close with a close road loss to the eventual champions, Montana State University. Many of the Team 152 seniors have since found new homes. Pitsenberger recently signed as a priority undrafted free agent with the Houston Texans, while Ayo-Durojaiye signed with the Hamilton Tiger-Cats of the Canadian Football League. With eligibility still remaining, many other seniors found new homes with high-major FBS programs as well. Michael Bennett ’26 recently departed to join the Oregon Ducks, while Brown, Ezekiel Larry ’27 and Wilhelm Daal ’26 all left to join ACC programs in Stanford University, the University of Virginia and Duke University, respectively. Center Leo Bluhm ’26 is headed to Rice University, defensive lineman Ejiroghene Egodogbare ’26 committed to the University of Houston and safety Josh Tarver ’26 transferred to Tarleton State University to round out the Texas crew.

title: Seniors leave behind four years defined by resilience
author: Walter Royal
text: The five members of the class of 2026 made an immediate impact for Yale women’s soccer. In the now-seniors’ first season, the team jumped five spots in the standings to finish third in the Ivy League. The rejuvenated team boasted a 7–0–1 home record in 2022, with a season-opening 0-0 draw with Hofstra University being the only blemish to their undefeated defense of Reese Stadium. 2023 represented a step back. While their nonconference season showed promise, with the team’s offense displaying further improvement, the hot boots went cold in conference play, finishing the Ivy slate 0–5–2. However, the Bulldogs bounced back in the class’s junior year, posting a winning overall record and picking up a pair of conference victories. This season started out bright, with a 4-0 win over Hofstra University in the home opener. Things went south from there, but the Yale squad showed promise, rallying for a stunning 1-0 midseason upset over the eventual Ivy-champion Princeton Tigers on the road. Though the team missed the postseason once more, the Princeton win provided a crowning achievement for the team’s senior class that graduates after persevering through adversity.

title: As seniors cross finish line, a ‘proud’ record to look back on
author: Walter Royal
text: The class of 2026’s final season was one to remember for Yale’s cross country team. Last fall, the team finished fifth place in regionals, the highest in recent memory, for the second straight year. Owen Karas ’26 and Kenan Pala ’26 also qualified for NCAAs, becoming the first Bulldog men to do so since 2023. Captain Braden King ’26 recalled the team’s collective excitement after the regionals race in an interview with the News. “It’s such a weird mix of feelings and emotions when you just finished the hardest race ever in the 10K,” King said. “You find out your two best friends qualified for nationals, and it’s just super overwhelming. A lot of people hugging each other, a lot of tears.” Entering New Haven as a particularly highly recruited class, expectations for the class of 2026 were elevated. King said he is proud of what the class has achieved together. “When I look back at our time and how we’ve all developed, I’m super proud of what we built here and what we’ve continued,” he said. “We’ve really helped push this program further, and I couldn't have asked for a better group of fellow seniors to do that with.”

title: Seniors saw team through years of steady improvement
author: Eli Ratner
text: The four seniors of Yale field hockey — Captain Poppy Beales ’26, Lauren Venter ’26, CC Wolf ’26 and Maddy Zavalick ’26 — brought the program to new heights. The class of 2026 arrived in 2022 alongside a new head coach, Melissa Gonzalez, and played its way into a new era for Yale field hockey. Since their freshman season, the Elis have steadily progressed, moving up from a 7–9 record in 2023 to 10–6 in 2024, before their most successful 12–6 campaign in 2025. This fall, the Bulldogs advanced to the NCAA tournament for the first time ever. The Elis went 12–4 in the regular season, earning a 4–3 record in a competitive Ivy League before falling short against Princeton in the Ivy League tournament. The Bulldogs finished the season 12th in the NCAA Division I rankings. The excellence of the class of 2026 began before their senior season. Early in her career, Beales emerged as one of the team’s most accomplished players, starting every game she has played since her sophomore year and earning All-Mideast Region and All-Ivy First Team honors in each of the past three years. Following her successful senior campaign, she was dubbed a second-team All-American. Zavalick also played a key role on the team throughout her career, starting 12 of 15 games her sophomore year, before returning to start all games as a senior.

title: 91 percent of senior class has used AI for schoolwork, News survey finds
author: Hari Viswanathan
text: This year’s graduating seniors were first years when OpenAI released ChatGPT. As the first class with the opportunity to use large language models like ChatGPT during every year of their college career, most seniors have now incorporated artificial intelligence into their lives to assist them for various purposes, from aiding with problem sets to researching theses. According to an anonymous survey conducted by the News and filled out by 172 seniors, 91 percent of the class of 2026 have used AI for schoolwork. Only 9.1 percent of students surveyed said they had never used AI for schoolwork. A majority of students — 67.5 percent — reported using AI sometimes, often or very often. More than 75 percent of respondents said they have used AI in a problem set. About 64 percent of respondents said they have used AI to write a paper, while 48.5 percent of the surveyed seniors said they used AI to write their senior theses — slightly less than the 51.5 percent that said they did not. Joey Tan ’26, who is a computer science major, described AI as a “supplementary tool” that she utilizes alongside other forms of technology. “I’ve used it to look up things that I want, like a more in-depth answer compared to Google, for instance,” Tan said in a phone interview. “Or I’ll do a mix of Google searching and AI.” She said that she thinks one of the most evident impacts of AI is on office hours, adding that students can now use AI, instead of tutors and undergraduate learning assistants, to teach them content and help them through problems. “It’s kind of unfortunate, because I made so many of my computer science friends in office hours,” Tan said. “It was, like, a really important community for me. It was how I found people that take upper class electives with me later on.” Aviv Melamud ’26, another computer science major, said that using Claude — the large language model developed by Anthropic — to work through problems is like having a virtual teaching assistant. “It basically killed office hours,” Melamud said in a phone interview. Who is using AI? Male respondents were more likely to report that they used AI for schoolwork than female respondents. While 16.7 percent of male respondents said they used AI “very often” for schoolwork, only 1.6 percent of female respondents said they did. According to the survey results, students majoring in the sciences were most likely to report AI use for schoolwork, followed by those in the social sciences, those in interdisciplinary majors and those in the humanities. Brian Moore ’26 noted that in the humanities, AI can serve a similar function as writing partners in the Poorvu Center for Teaching and Learning, improving things like grammar, syntax and diction. “I tend to see this as polarizing. There’s a lot of folks who won’t touch AI with a 10-foot pole, and others who will readily adopt it,” Moore, who is majoring in humanities, said in a phone interview. Melamud said he thinks AI will not render STEM classes obsolete. He compared the experience of learning computer science with AI to that of learning math with the aid of a calculator. Professors will have to grapple seriously with the advancement of AI technology, Moore said, but he added that there are creative ways in which professors can weave AI into class assignments and pedagogy. “I guess, in some ways, AI is prompting us to re-examine what the philosophy of education is, right?” Moore said. ChatGPT was released on Nov. 30, 2022.

title: With high hopes, Yale continues to invest in Upper Science Hill
author: Sara Agrawal
text: One corner on campus has experienced a particularly expensive transformation over the past four years. The University had poured hundreds of millions into the development of Upper Science Hill, with the aim of setting Yale apart in the fields of physics, the applied sciences and engineering. University leaders have consistently emphasized their hopes to place Yale at the forefront of cutting-edge scientific fields like quantum computing. “The USHD reflects a long-term commitment to securing Yale’s leadership in scientific discovery and innovation in order to contribute meaningfully to addressing the world’s present and future challenges,” Michael Crair, Yale’s vice provost for research, wrote in an email to the News, referring to the Upper Science Hill Development project. In a February interview with the News, Yale President Maurie McInnis identified investing in the physical sciences and engineering as the “top priority” of the University, and in her end-of-year message to the Yale community, she singled out the University’s “strides in reimagining Science Hill.” When Linda McMahon, the United States’ secretary of education, toured Yale in April, she called the construction on Upper Science Hill Construction “incredible.” Yale’s 2023–24 financial report stated that out of the University’s $614 million in capital spending, which includes “renovations” and “acquisitions and new construction,” 12 percent — around $75 million — went into biological and physical sciences. That figure rose to over $190 million — 29 percent of $661 million — in the 2025 fiscal year . When asked why he thought Yale was investing so heavily in engineering and physical sciences, physics professor Karsten Heeger emphasized the importance of Yale’s frontier scholarship in those fields as well as the humanities. “Yale has always been leading in various fields, including the humanities, for example,” Heeger, who is also the director of the Yale Wright Laboratory for experimental nuclear physics, particle physics and astrophysics, said. “Science and engineering and technology is absolutely essential for society and our modern world. If we want to be a leading research university, we have to be cutting-edge in those fields.” The development of Upper Science Hill is a decade-long project that has been planned in two phases. According to Heeger, the first phase, which began in 2022 and broke ground in 2024, is set to be completed this fall. This phase involves the construction of a new Advanced Instrumentation Development Center and replacing an old part of the Wright Lab. Heeger said that the renovation will bolster the Wright Lab with “state-of-the-art laboratories that will give us the vibration isolation, the environmental control, all of the things that we need, the infrastructure for cutting-edge research.” That transformation will provide new research opportunities for both undergraduate and graduate students, Heeger added. The second phase of the Upper Science Hill development project will see the completion of a new Physical Sciences and Engineering Building, or PSEB, Heeger said. “The idea was to build a building complex that brings together research groups from physics, physical sciences, and the engineering departments and mainly with the focus on quantum science and material science,” he said. Crair wrote that the building complex will foster “cross-disciplinary collaboration.” In total, the building will house around 50 research groups, according to Heeger. Heeger also said that the physics department has made new appointments in quantum science and atomic, molecular and optical physics, but that exact final number “remains to be seen.” The development of Upper Science Hill follows a 2018 report of the University Science Strategy Committee, or USSC, which states that “tomorrow’s leaders need a solid grounding in STEM so they can understand the world and shape its future” and outlines investments the University might make to improve STEM at Yale. The report suggested that the University invest in graduate students, advance instrumentation, update core facilities and promote diversity in the STEM pipeline. The report also emphasized that the School of Engineering and Applied Sciences, or SEAS, would play a vital role in the future of science at Yale and “must be fully integrated into the fabric of the University.” In 2022, following another report from a SEAS strategic planning committee in 2021, the University announced major investments in applied sciences and engineering and the separation of SEAS faculty from the Faculty of Arts and Science. A focus on quantum Quantum science has been one of the focuses of Yale’s investments in engineering and applied science. The field, which studies the smallest units within atoms that act both as particles and as waves, is used both in physics and in computing and was one of the “five ideas of priority investment” highlighted in the 2018 USSC report. Speaking about the research in Wright Lab, Heeger said that “quantum science is intrinsic to all what we do.” Shaun Pexton ’26, the co-president of Yale Undergraduate Quantum Computing, called quantum the “shining beacon of Yale engineering” in an interview with the News. Pexton said that he’s noticed growing attention around quantum during his time at Yale as the “race” of quantum technology has made its way into “political conversations” and as Yale has gained recognition as a place where “a lot of cool stuff is happening.” In her end-of-year message, President McInnis said that the new facilities on Science Hill will “supercharge quantum science at Yale.” The Yale Quantum Institute will be relocated from Hillhouse Avenue to the PSEB, according to its website . In the message, President McInnis also mentioned professor Michel Devoret, who won a Nobel Prize in 2025 for his work in quantum physics , and QuantumCT , a nonprofit launched in 2022 by Crair and Pamir Alpay, the interim provost of the University of Connecticut. McInnis’ wrote in the end-of-year message that through quantum technology, “we will make our home state more attractive to start-ups” and “create a talented workforce in this area.” When asked why he thought the University was heavily investing in and promoting quantum, Pexton said that technological development, especially in AI and quantum, have become indicators of a university’s advancement. “Yale wants to be at the forefront, and they are at the forefront,” Pexton said. The construction on Upper Science Hill is located at 268 to 280 Whitney Ave.

title: After admin banner ban, YSE grads don anti-ICE t-shirts in class photo
author: Michelle So
text: Since the school’s founding in 1900, graduates of the Yale School of the Environment have taken an annual class photo behind Marsh Hall, where the original Yale Forest School was located . For over a century, these photos have captured the school through class size increases, the emergence of color photography and shifts in student body demographics. In recent years, the class photos have become a platform for School of the Environment students to make broader political statements. Members of the class of 2024 posed with pro-Palestinian protest materials, according to members of class of 2026, before the school updated its class photo protocol to prohibit signs and banners. This year, some members of the class of 2026 organized an unofficial class photo in which students donned t-shirts that depicted melting ice cubes. “The shirts were meant to be symbolic and hold two meanings: climate change is melting ice, and abolish ICE,” Riki Bertoldi ENV ’26, one of the photo’s organizers, wrote in a text to the News. On April 29, after the graduating students lined up for their official photo, nearly all of them stayed behind to change into the shirts and pose for a new photo, according to Bertoldi. She said that the idea for the unofficial photo had been conceived “months before.” Emily Kaufman ENV ’26 wrote in an email to the News that past students have presented more “explicit messages” in their class photos. “I really loved the class of 2024 photo when we saw a lot of protest signs, and I believe there was a big banner that said, ‘War is an environmental issue,’” Adrian Huq ENV ’26 said in a Zoom interview. “That really resonated with me as a peace advocate and anti-militarist sort of climate activist.” Huq said that administrators discouraged protest-related photoshoots after the 2024 display. Paige Stein, the school’s chief communications and marketing officer, wrote in an email to the News that in order to “emphasize the collective, inclusive nature of our school community,” the School of the Environment updated its “class photo protocol.” “Official photos are now taken without banners or signs,” Stein added. “The School of the Environment adheres to the university’s wider policy on freedom of expression.” According to the website of the office of the secretary and vice president for university life, the University prohibits large “banners held by multiple individuals” at outdoor events, but does not specify a policy prohibiting signs or banners in official photos. This year, as the annual class photo neared, Huq and Bertoldi said they worked to navigate around this updated policy change. “We decided to get creative and use textiles instead as a medium to express a collective statement in a way that still felt visible and meaningful to our community,” Bertoldi said. According to Bertoldi, Kaufman led much of the planning and coordination, while Brandon Hoak ENV ’26 created the initial design, produced the stencil and led the screen-printing process. “We made sure to communicate our plans with both international students and the broader class and asked whether people would feel comfortable with the shirts being worn during the official class photo,” Bertoldi wrote in an email. “Some students expressed discomfort, so we decided to take the shirt photo separately after the official class photo.” Bertoldi said she and other organizers invited other students to join the unofficial photoshoot through a WhatsApp group chat called “YSE Class of 2026,” which included every member of the graduating class. Students who wished to opt out were given the option to. According to Kaufman, the photo’s organizers “felt it was important to continue the tradition in a more subtle yet still powerful way” after they had elected not to wear the shirts during the official photo. “Through the drawing of melting ice, we wanted to show that environmentalism cannot be separated from the injustices currently facing immigrant communities and that YSE will continue our longstanding tradition of speaking out despite administrative pushback,” Kaufman said. Marsh Hall is located at 360 Prospect St.

title: Yale is building 263 new wells for geothermal energy
author: Michelle So
text: Natural gas provides roughly half of Connecticut’s energy, according to the U.S. Energy Information Administration. While burning the fuel produces half the emissions as does burning coal, natural gas is not as green as one might think. Any carbon-based form of energy produces carbon dioxide and other greenhouse gases when combusted for energy. Greenhouse gas emissions are responsible for trapping heat in Earth’s atmosphere, which contributes to global warming. Yale has committed itself to achieving zero emissions by 2050, a goal towards which the University has slowly been advancing. Alternative forms of energy, including geothermal technologies, may be the ticket there. “Yale is moving toward geothermal energy as part of the university’s commitment to achieve a zero‐emissions (zero‐carbon) campus by 2050,” Andy Bromage, the sustainability communications officer for the Yale Office of Facilities, wrote in an email to the News. In order to achieve that goal, Bromage said, drilling is underway to install 263 geothermal wells on Upper Science Hill. The new plant will support buildings including the Yale Science Building, the Sloane Physics Laboratory, the Sterling Chemistry Laboratory, the Kline Chemistry Laboratory, the Chemistry Research Building and the Bass Center. Electricity is generated when water is heated to turn turbines that produce steam. Yale currently employs several means of energy generation, including natural gas power plants on Tower Parkway and Congress Avenue, wind turbines on the roof of the Becton Engineering and Applied Sciences building and solar panels on Kroon Hall, Fisher Hall and West Campus. The core change in Yale’s energy-generating system involves the replacement of steam-based heating with low‐temperature water — heated to around 120 to 140 degrees Fahrenheit — generated by an electric heat pump that will draw heat from deep underground. “Any move away from using fossil resources (carbon) and toward using renewable resources (i.e., those that don’t move carbon from underground storage to the atmosphere) is a step in the right direction and is a good thing!” Hanno Erythropel, a researcher at the Yale University Center for Green Chemistry and Green Engineering, wrote in an email to the News. “That is certainly the case for the Yale geothermal energy project.” Erythropel added that more specificity regarding the amount of emitted carbon dioxide that could be offset by the project would be helpful. Yale’s move toward geothermal energy follows a trend of universities moving towards cleaner energy. In 2024, The New York Times reported that other universities and colleges, including Princeton, Carleton College and Ball State University, were also tapping into the technology. “I think it’s a good push for Yale as a university and as a whole to make good on their carbon capture initiative while being more sustainable,” Naomi Orcel ’27, the president of Club Geo, an Earth and planetary science student organization, said. Orcel said she was initially irked that the construction on Upper Science Hill took away parking spaces for faculty, but that she changed her mind on the project after hearing its mission. “I think it’s a really good thing that we’re using geothermal energy,” she added, “because it’s not an energy source people usually think to use; people usually think like solar or wind.” The shift toward geothermal energy extends beyond Yale. New Haven received around $9.5 million from the Environmental Protection Agency to build a networked geothermal system to provide clean heating and cooling to the New Haven Union Station. The funding would also support the construction of a mixed-income housing development adjacent to the station. Yale’s Upper Science Hill Development’s new facilities for quantum science, engineering and materials science are designed to hopefully support both new construction and existing Science Hill laboratory buildings by providing electrified thermal energy and reducing the need to add capacity to Yale’s existing power plants and steam distribution system. The Upper Science Hill Development is scheduled to be completed in 2030.

title: New student-built app aims to make dining hall menus more accessible
author: Anya Geist
text: When Yassin Mudawi ’28, a transfer student, arrived on campus last fall, he said he found Yale’s dining system confusing: There were so many dining facilities, all with varying closures, transfer restrictions and menu changes. Mudawi recently led a team at the Yale Computer Society to launch YaleMeals, an app that aims to offer an intuitive, easy-to-use menu guide to Yale’s dining options. The app was downloaded over 700 times by April 7, according to Mudawi. “For a new student coming here, it seemed to be a pain point for me personally, and how I approach software is I find a pain point in my life and then try to fix it,” Mudawi said in an interview, referring to Yale’s dining system. Mudawi wanted to create an easily navigable app. The Yale Hospitality website, which uses Nutrislice to display menus, has a lot of “nested pages,” Mudawi said. Nutrislice requires users to visit new pages to view a dining hall’s menu for each meal on a given day. Mudawi said he brought his idea to Yale Computer Society co-President Ryan Fernandes ’28 last fall, and the club decided to take on YaleMeals as a project. On YaleMeals, Mudawi tried to make it simple to switch between locations, meals and days. The app also allows students to customize its settings, including for their dietary preferences. The app features a “Friends” tab, where users can invite and schedule meals with their peers. “I noticed there’s a big meal culture here at Yale, where everyone wants to grab, like, lunch together or dinner together, and it’s really wholesome. So why not try to promote that and encourage that?” Mudawi said. Mudawi said he and his team hope students can use YaleMeals even in the case that Yale Hospitality’s database changes. He added that he built the YaleMeals code to make it easy to switch to a new interface for a changed database. Yale Hospitality spokesperson Alexa Gotthardt wrote in an email to the News: “We value student innovation and recognize the creativity students bring in developing new tools and platforms. At the same time, we encourage students to rely on Yale Hospitality’s official menus page for the most accurate and up-to-date menu information.” Other students have created similar apps to YaleMeals in the past, including Bulldog Menus, which was founded by Edmundo Chavez Ramirez ’28. Regarding similar apps created outside of the Yale Computer Society, Lily Lin ’27, the club’s co-president, said, “Our goal is not to monopolize the market.” YaleMeals builds on the work of Yale Menus, a previously popular app that also listed each dining hall’s menus. Yale Menus was originally launched by Eric Foster ’20 and his brother David Foster ’23 in 2017, and it was relaunched by David Foster and Erik Boesen ’24 in 2020, Boesen wrote to the News. After Boesen graduated, the app became challenging to maintain. “We negotiated a license to sell Menus to Yale Hospitality over a period of roughly 4-5 years,” Boesen wrote to the News. Ultimately, the sale fell through. Boesen, realizing the app was out of date due to changes to Yale Hospitality’s databases and programming interfaces, passed it on to the Yale Computer Society. While Yale Menus was not a project created through the club, Boesen wrote, he had been a club member as an undergraduate. “Yale Hospitality has previously explored partnership opportunities related to student-developed dining apps, including the ‘Yale Menus’ app,” Gotthardt wrote to the News. “At that time, we determined it was not the right fit, largely due to the operational complexity involved in maintaining a separate platform alongside our existing systems, which serve the same purpose.” Ruby Lytton-Hirsch ’29 frequently uses Yale’s Nutrislice app to look at lunch menus around campus. Looking at the menu in advance allows her to see whether she prefers the Greens and Grains option or the regular offering. However, Lytton-Hirsch said the Nutrislice app can be challenging to use, particularly because she has to scroll to the bottom of the page to see the main options for each meal. Lytton-Hirsch said she thought the YaleMeals app looked “prettier and easier to navigate” than Nutrislice. Similarly, Hrishita Shah ’28 said she found YaleMeals more “intuitive” to use than Nutrislice, though she added that she does not mind using Nutrislice because she has gotten used to it. Shah, however, noted that the YaleMeals app lacks reviews of the menu items. “The old Yale Menus app, it had a lot of reviews, like the Commons cookies had, like, 50 hearts,” Shah said, adding that “not a lot of people” use YaleMeals. “So, like, the s’mores brownies have zero ratings.” Rishi Sankhe ’28 sometimes uses YaleMeals, though he does not always feel the need to check dining hall menus before visiting. He finds the app easy to use, he said. Sankhe noted that he thought the YaleMeals team was continuing to make good changes to the app, including adding pictures of the dishes. Some students have never used YaleMeals. Zach Pan ’27 said he had not heard of YaleMeals or even the Nutrislice website. “I show up at the dining hall and eat what’s there,” Pan said. “I know I’m not going to spend additional funds on food from other sources. So I enter the dining hall, and I eat the food in the dining hall.” The dining halls serve chicken tenders for lunch every other Thursday.

title: Yale study finds link between cancer and living near factory farms
author: Michelle So
text: Earlier this year, Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. announced a revamp of the familiar food pyramid, with protein sources like red meat and full-fat dairy at the top of the list of recommended foods. Yet, certain studies have found consumption of red meat to be linked to cardiovascular diseases and cancers of the colon . Now, simply living near sites of red meat production may be linked to cancers as well. A Yale study published in the journal Environmental Research examined whether living in areas with higher densities of animal feeding operations and concentrated animal feeding operations is associated with cancer incidence. The feeding operations, commonly known as factory farms, often contain livestock in cramped conditions. Concentrated operations exceed a threshold population of 1,000 beef cattle, 700 dairy cows or 2,500 hogs. “These facilities are known sources of environmental pollution, including air pollutants (e.g., ammonia, particulate matter, hydrogen sulfide) and water contaminants such as nitrates, which may have implications for human health,” Jiyoung Son, a researcher at the School of the Environment and the paper’s first author, wrote in an email to the News. Using county-level data from California, Iowa and Texas between 2000 and 2021, the researchers found that counties with higher factory farm density had higher overall cancer incidence. The increased cancer incidence prevailed despite variations in factory farm makeup; for example, dairy operations are more common in California, swine in Iowa and beef cattle in Texas. Cancer isn’t the only risk associated with living near a factory farm. When animals live in close proximity with one another, disease transmission becomes nearly unavoidable. Farmers often add antibiotics to animal feed to prevent disease-related deaths and bolster livestock growth. Overuse of antibiotics can cause bacteria to develop antibiotic resistance. When runoff from these feedlots enters the environment, these superbugs may infect humans and other animals and remain unresponsive to treatment — a scenario that is dangerous to both livestock and human health. Cole Haynes ’27, the co-president of Plant Forward Yalies, a student group that promotes sustainable food systems, said he was not shocked by the study’s results. “While the findings of this Yale study are deeply concerning, they are unfortunately not surprising. The public health impacts of animal agriculture are widespread and well documented,” Haynes wrote in an email to the News. The study’s researchers still say more detailed research is needed. Son suggested implementing environmental monitoring, especially of air quality and drinking water contamination, and improving waste management and reducing emissions. Consuming red and processed meats is also associated with “pro-inflammatory mechanisms” that increase gastrointestinal and colon cancers and cardiovascular disease, John Dinkler, a cardiologist and the associate head of Timothy Dwight College, said. Instead, Dinkler recommends that people switch to a more plant-based diet. Changing one’s eating habits has marked benefits on cardiovascular disease and reduces cancer risk. A plant-based diet tends to lower blood pressure, improve cholesterol levels, maintain healthy blood sugar levels and reduce inflammation, he wrote in an email to the News. All of those changes improve the health of blood vessels throughout the cardiovascular system, reducing the risk of heart attacks and strokes. “I’ve actually had many patients lower doses of cholesterol, blood pressure, and diabetes medications (or come off medications completely) after changing to a plant-based diet,” Dinkler wrote. “It’s impressive to see!” According to Haynes, a plant-based diet has been shown to be one of the most effective ways to reduce an individual’s carbon footprint by 75 percent, while also decreasing reliance on systems that harm human health, animals and the environment. “Even small changes, like replacing meat with plant-based alternatives, opting for plant-based desserts, or swapping dairy milk for oat or soy, can have a significant impact,” Haynes said. An estimated 99 percent of the United States’ farmed animals live on factory farms, according to the Sentience Institute, a think tank.

title: Scrolling through the semester
author: Beatrice Barilla
text: We’ve all been there: your brain turned off at a 9 a.m. lecture, tired from a late night at Bass, the dining hall coffee suspiciously uncaffeinated. Across the room, someone accidentally blasts an Instagram reel with what can only be described as a brainrot audio, and everyone laughs. You groggily pull out your phone to check the time and — is that an email about your late paper? A text about dinner plans? A grade posted on Canvas for that quiz you know you flunked? You gradually get absorbed into the lunch menu at Berkeley, your long-distance best friend’s Instagram story and now Connections. When you look up, everything in lecture is foreign — since when did you start taking Astrophysics 8000?! Technology is bittersweet, and we all know it. Sure, now we can conveniently take notes on an iPad, record and photograph important concepts and subtly stalk our friends on Find My — but our attention spans have suffered as a consequence. In my own biology lectures, I look over the shoulders of those in front of me and see TikTok, not Goodnotes. And as far as notes go, ChatGPT seems to be putting plenty of legwork in. I notice that I can’t even make it through a full lecture without checking my email. History professor Carlos Eire ’79 attended Yale as a graduate student around 50 years ago when typewriters reigned, and he has watched Yale change along with its technology. Back when he first attended in the 70s, Ashley’s Ice Cream was Whitlock’s Typewriter Shop, which Eire says “was essential for everyone’s survival.” “Sometime around 1975 a fellow graduate student in computer science told me that someday ‘soon’ we would all be able to type text onto a TV monitor and produce error-free documents that could be endlessly edited and polished to perfection, and then — best of all — produce the final version on a printing machine,” Eire wrote in an email. “I said something like ‘sure, yeah, and we'll soon be driving flying cars too . . .’ A couple of years later, boom!” Computers were too expensive for common use at that time, but they were becoming more widespread in the collegiate sphere and would spread across Yale over the next few decades. Even in 1996, when Eire returned to Yale to teach, office work was still done on typewriters and paper, and books had to be located in the stacks manually and checked out using cardboard forms. “The printing on those forms was very elegant,” Eire wrote about the check-out forms. “And the libraries were kind enough to provide stubby little pencils to help us all complete this laborious task.” Eire wrote that Yale “fully embraced” computer technology and the Internet around the turn of the millennium. According to him, Yale’s computerized renaissance was fairly rapid: the library and its catalogue became digital, rendering the card catalog shelves in Sterling obsolete, a “reminder of days gone by.” The era of Microsoft Word had dawned, though paper copies of assignments were usually required until about 2010. “And Yale started to provide professors with personal computers,” Eire wrote. We all know the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on digital learning technology, which further cemented remote meetings and virtual education technology as hallmarks of collegiate education. In the words of Eire, we now reside in the “AI Apocalypse,” where nothing can be assumed to be original anymore. Artificial intelligence chatbots can write a paper, create a practice exam, take notes and generate resumes. It takes grit — and time — to ignore its siren song. Technology only continues to change more and more rapidly: The jump from the proliferation of online assignments to the introduction of AI took only a fraction of time compared to the transition from the typewriter to the computer. It’s hard not to wonder what’s next and how quickly it will change education — and our attention spans. This year’s graduating seniors watched educational technology from the pandemic era become the status quo and AI develop from irrelevant to ubiquitous, but remaining students will witness how it continues to shape Yale over the years to come. Will we scroll through the semester or learn to recover our focus? Personally, I’m hoping for the latter.

title: Looking up and slowing down
author: Michael Willen
text: Looking up I have a knack for noticing shoes. They hold space in my mind as a representation and understanding of my friends. I see how they compliment or clash with their personalities and how their choices change with the seasons. I notice when they switch it up, and I know instinctively when they’re debuting a new pair. A scarily good memory is partly to blame. But, in full, it’s a defense mechanism. Instead of focusing on a face, I would find some background fixation: like a baby’s game of peek-a-boo, looking down meant that no one could study me too closely or perceive too much. I spent a lot of my time at Yale afraid of that perception. The school can feel nauseatingly small sometimes: No one is more than one degree of connection away, and if you haven’t met someone, you’ve heard of them. We talk and compare so incessantly. Opinions spread like wildfire, which doesn’t inspire confidence for a people pleaser. In dining halls, I’d stress when conversations got too loud or clam up when neighboring groups got too quiet. Walking through crowded spaces had me on high alert. Overthinking my own racial identity often kept me away from events at the Afro-American Cultural Center. In general, I tried to let any moment of possible judgment pass as quickly as possible. We’re our own harshest critics, in this way. We notice every quirk in a photo and contemplate our actions until every facet has been scrutinized and regretted. Beyond that selfish veil, everyone is living far too much of their own lives to spend more than the briefest moments on you. Across my four bright college years, I’ve come to realize that more and more. And looking back, the moments I’ve enjoyed the most are the moments when I stopped caring. So own the space you take up and stay confident in your ability to make mistakes. Spill your chai latte everywhere in the Elm? Clean it up with a laugh and a smile. Be ready to fall on your ice skates and don’t care who might see — it’ll make you a better skater. Mess up a dance move on stage, or many — shoutout to Danceworks — and no one will notice. Accept that an interview or a date might go badly — the bad ones are the only way you’ll get practice for the good ones. Don’t look back in regret because you’ve let your fear of failure or embarrassment keep you from living Yale to the fullest. If things go wrong? Bounce back. Care about what people think to the extent that it helps you be a better person, but not so much that you live solely for that perception. I still notice shoes. But now I notice faces, too — the eyes, smiles and everything behind them. I let people truly see me, and in doing so I start to truly see them. Slowing down I’m a fast walker. Across two New York summers, I perfected the New Yorker stride that would get me from point A to point B as efficiently as possible without ever showing effort. It’s great when I’m making the crosstown trek from Grand Central Terminal to Penn Station on my way home to Philly or when I’m running embarrassingly late to a class. At Yale, our brains seem trained to work like that, too. We’re a school full of overachievers, overcommitters and overextenders. It’s what got us here in the first place, and here, it only gets worse: Seemingly infinite clubs, classes and events provide never-ending options to fill space in our lives — worsened by an embedded peer pressure to do so. So fill space I did. Each semester, I’d populate my life with every sign-up and every yes. But we have limits. Every new activity removed hours of sleep and added late nights spent in the library. Every new commitment meant less and less blank space in my Google Calendar. I took too long to realize the importance of that blank space. In finding more time for scheduled things, I found less time for myself. I rue the times I said no to friends’ side quests and the times I missed nights of hourslong chatter in our suite’s common room. I spent too many moments with some part of my attention stuck on the next task or deadline instead of what was happening directly in front of my eyes. Always on the lookout for the next thing that needed to be done, I never gave my brain the benefit of a pause. Sometimes, the velocity of keeping up caused me to speed past people. If some adjacent worry took over, I’d accidentally walk faster and leave a friend behind, or I’d space out on important parts of a deep conversation over dinner. I’d forget names or miss important moments. With graduation approaching, I look forward to finally getting a bit of that pause. But taking on so much isn’t in Yale’s graduation requirements. I wish I’d reflected sooner on the intentionality of my commitments, and I wish I’d given myself the grace to question what’s worth it and to drop what wasn’t. Don’t forget the value of slowing down. Have blank space in your schedule — it’s more important than whatever might take its place. It’s okay to say no when you know something is too much, and it’s okay to do less than what it seems like everyone else is doing. It’s okay to take a little bit longer to get from point A to point B. And don’t leave your friends or yourself behind on the way there.

title: Light and truth
author: Madison Butchko
text: On Ivy Day, I put on my lucky purple jacket, a soft lilac almost identical to my bedroom walls, which I had stared at for the 17 years of my life in Michigan. All day, I reassured myself that whatever verdict graced my laptop, I would be okay — though my body knew I was lying. Sitting at my desk, I knotted my fingers together. Before opening my Yale decision, I whispered a series of small prayers. I clicked, and the screen went black briefly before the bulldog appeared, singing, “Bow wow wow.” Tears streamed down my cheeks before I could process a single thought. My hand covered my mouth, muffling a series of “Oh my God, oh my God,” as I sobbed at my desk before running to tell my parents. For a long time, Yale existed in my mind as a lighthouse: distant, bright and almost impossible to reach. I believed education could move me toward a larger life, so I pushed harder into the current — I woke up early, stayed late, studied, worked jobs, joined clubs and sports and kept going, even when no direct sign of progress encouraged me. Somewhere ahead was a fixed point of light. I didn’t know if I would reach it, but it gave me direction when the water was dark and I could not feel the bottom. The following August, the light became a destination. Arriving at the entrance of Jonathan Edwards College with my parents and walking through the doors of Sterling Memorial Library for the first time, I thought I had reached my dream. The cathedral of books enchanted me: the vaulted ceilings, the stone archways, the light falling through tall windows in pale columns. I took photographs of the carved faces in the walls, the floral designs, the small details I wanted to savor in memory. For a long time, I wanted Yale to remain that place in my mind, that place I had dreamed for: all the libraries and courtyards, late nights in the buttery and bright laboratory windows, the stone-bound beauty. I wish I could write about those things as if they belonged to one life — without accepting any connection to his abuse, the court cases, my years of silence and suffering and the irreparable damage I will never have enough words to encapsulate. I still do not know how to separate my experience of Yale from the insurmountable pain I endured here. Lives do not divide themselves that neatly. When I think about graduation, I think about the people around me crying because they will miss this place. I am crying for what I missed — for the version of college I imagined before I knew how much these years would hold. There is no ceremony for that kind of shattering loss. At Yale, achievement is easily recognizable. It has titles, fellowships, publications, neat lines on a resume. Emotional growth doesn’t lend itself to such memorabilia. In high school, my “best” looked obvious: discipline, early mornings, good grades, awards, medals, things other people could see and measure. In college, my “best” became harder to specify. Sometimes, it looked like simply getting out of bed. Sometimes, it looked like answering one email, eating something without feeling sick immediately after, doing far less than the old version of me would have done and trying not to berate myself for it. I don’t want to call what I have been through strengthening. I never wanted to be stronger in this way. Surviving does not mean you are grateful for its so-called lessons, and endurance is not the same as growth. Healing has not felt like rising from the ashes, not like a fire catching again — dramatic and bright. Most of the time, I’ve felt like the embers after a fire: small, hot, exhausted, glowing in places but no longer able to burn the way they once did, with my soul broken into pieces too small to gather once more. I did not choose to keep fighting. I felt caught between my will and my body, between the part of me that wanted to stop and the body that kept moving anyway. Because bodies do that. They fall asleep. They wake again. The sun sets. The sun rises. Seconds pass into minutes, hours, and days, and somehow I remained imprisoned inside time, even when I felt left behind by it. Life moves forward with both cruelty and mercy. The cruelty is that everything continues while you are the one who carries the damage, even though you were not the one who caused it. The mercy is that time carries you too, even when you cannot carry yourself, moving you into another morning, another chance to keep living before you feel ready. Even the small things bring comfort — waking up a little earlier, sleeping without nightmares, meeting friends again after months of absence, walking across campus despite feeling unsafe, writing my truth despite the fear. Healing does not bring peace unilaterally. My friendships, my writing and the small ordinary things that accumulate into a life do not erase the years I’ve lost. They remind me that the harm I suffered can belong to my life without becoming the entirety of it. Healing has become the slow growth of life around the wound. A tree does not heal its wounds. Its trunk thickens around them, laying down new rings over what cannot be undone, with the wound preserved at the center of everything that follows. What happened to me is fixed at the center of these years in the same way: unchangeable, present in everything I have become — the core around which the rest of my life has had to grow. People carry far more than they show. We move beside people whose lives we may never fully understand. Pain has blanketed the world with loneliness, but it has also made me more tender toward the hidden lives of others. If there is anything I will carry beyond Yale, it is that tenderness: a deeper kindness, a greater compassion, a soft heart that reaches toward anyone who has been hurting. I mourn the girl who was seventeen, sitting at a desk in Michigan in a lilac bedroom, crying from pure joy because she was about to attend Yale. She believed the world was beautiful, that all people were good, that her dreams were infinite. I wish I could hold her tightly. I wish I could have protected her. She will go through things she cannot yet imagine, not cleanly, not without cost, but through. I think of “Lux et Veritas” and understand it differently. I came to Yale believing light was a destination, a reward for all that swimming toward the lighthouse. But light is what you learn to see after your eyes have adjusted to the dark. Truth is not a given, either. I used to think truth would be recognized simply because it was real — because it is what happened. I learned that truth can be denied, buried, silenced and made costly to speak. For the past four years, I prayed so desperately to return to my 17-year-old self, to the innocence and possibility she carried in her heart. I cannot. Instead, I protect what remains of her in me: a hope that has changed shape, the way light does, bending around whatever stands in its way and continuing on.

title: The ultimate Yucket list: 100 things to do before you graduate
author: Seth Goldin
text: The list that follows is semi-ordered: 1. Trauma-dumped in a pre-orientation “hometown” 2. Grabbed coffee at the Jitterbus 3. Read “ The Opposite of Loneliness ” 4. Snuck into your friend’s duty night 5. Strolled through the Yale University Art Gallery 6. Explored the Peabody Museum 7. Toured the Yale Center for British Art 8. Attended a recital at the School of Music 9. Explored the “Brain Museum” at the Cushing Center 10. Created a masterpiece in one of the college art studios 11. Peered out of the Observatory telescope 12. Requested texts from the Sterling or Beinecke archives 13. Hissed at a Yale Political Union debate 14. Caught a Dramat theater performance 15. Attended — or sang in — an a capella concert 16. Supported a friend at — or performed in — a dance show 17. Wore a last-minute costume to the Yale Symphony Orchestra Halloween concert 18. Published in — or been quoted in — the YDN 19. Cheered at any Yale sporting event besides The Game 20. Played for an intramural team 21. Visited every residential college library 22. Eaten in every dining hall — yes, that includes Pierson 23. Snacked at a buttery other than your college’s 24. Played ping-pong in a buttery 25. Cooked in a student kitchen 26. Grabbed a bite from a food truck behind Murray 27. Visited the Yale Farm or Wooster Square Farmers’ Market 28. Been to Pepe’s 29. Been to Sally's 30. Been to Modern 31. Argue about which of the above is the best pizza spot 32. Been to Patricia’s 33. Ordered a late-night Wenzel at Alpha Delta 34. Ordered an omelette at JE — if you know, you know 35. Gone to an event just for the free food 36. Said you’ll grab a meal with someone 37. Actually grabbed a meal with someone 38. Used GCal to schedule a meal with someone 39. Answered a tourist’s question 40. Yelled “happy birthday!” at a tour guide 41. Grabbed burgers at the Well 42. Said you “go to school in Connecticut” 43. Pet Handsome Dan 44. Taken the Yuttle 45. Stayed up past midnight talking about religion 46. Hiked East Rock at sunrise or sunset 47. Hiked West Rock 48. Ice skated at “The Whale” 49. Biked the Farmington Canal Heritage Trail 50. Worked out at Payne Whitney 51. Blown giant bubbles on Cross Campus 52. Napped in the Good Life Center 53. Run in the annual Faxon Law New Haven Road Race 54. Gone on a long walk to the Yale Divinity School at night 55. Had a deep, meaningful conversation in Murray’s secret courtyard 56. Retreated with a club 57. Visited a town in Connecticut other than New Haven 58. Rode the Metro North to New York City 59. Visited the Yale Club in New York City 60. Sledded down Science Hill 61. Studied at the Lillian Goldman Law Library 62. Picked a class because of a crush 63. Pulled an all-nighter 64. Fallen asleep in a class 65. Slept through a class 66. Credit/D/Failed a class 67. Cried in front of your dean 68. Requested a dean’s extension 69. Studied in the stacks 70. Changed the aux at Timothy Dwight’s dining hall 71. Split up a suite 72. Slept in a Bass cubicle 73. Eaten at Pasta E Basta 74. Attended a suite party 75. Did everything except drink tea at a college tea 76. Danced in the God Quad 77. Attended first-year Holiday “Dinner” 78. Tailgated at the Yale-Harvard game 79. Crashed the first-year snowball fight 80. Waited in line at the Pierson Inferno 81. Mingled at Senior Masquerade 82. Been to Woads, Soads, Hallowoads, Mistletoads or Valenwoads — bonus: “ Woads Scholar ” 83. Meditated with the Buddhist group at Harkness Tower 84. Heard the Whiffenpoofs sing at Mory’s 85. Sipped from a Mory’s cup 86. Caught the “Yague” 87. Been to GHeav after 2 a.m. 88. “The Tang” or “Caps” 89. Made fun of a friend at a Mellon forum 90. “All Star”-ed Feb Club 91. Polar plunged at Lighthouse Point 92. Attended “Battle of the Bands” 93. Dartied at Spring Fling 94. Picnicked on Cross Campus 95. Done the “Yorkside Challenge” 96. Stumbled upon the third Good Life Center 97. Infiltrated — or joined — a secret society 98. Witnessed — or streaked in — the Bass naked run 99. Attended a party hosted by the Pundits 100. Graduated...

title: The Quiet Architect at Yale
author: Jonas Loesel
text: Last August, Deborah Berke welcomed her newest students to the Yale School of Architecture. Standing atop Rudolph Hall, Yale’s Art and Architecture Building, the dean told them about “seven rules for life”—a creed that belonged not to the dean but to the school’s billionaire donor, Edward Bass ’68. “Ed Bass is truly an extraordinary man,” she recalled telling the new cohort. “It's an honor to have a position that's named after him, and I want to tell you his rules for life.” Nine months prior, a small group led by Bass’s wife, Sasha, had made one of the largest donations in the school’s history to endow Berke’s deanship in honor of Bass, now called the Edward P. Bass Dean at the Yale School of Architecture. Berke told me that she learned these rules over the years of knowing Bass, who is 80 years old, and now keeps them on a note taped to the bottom-left corner of her computer. The note reads: Work hard. Be nice. Have fun. Be humble. Be generous. Be grateful. Everyone deserves your respect. According to former Yale president Richard Levin, Bass has done “probably more philanthropy than any living Yale donor.” Although exact numbers are not public, Bass has quietly transformed the school since the 1990s with gifts totaling at least $300 million. From 2001 to 2013, he served on the Yale Corporation, in addition to a number of other university councils that he’s still on today. His architectural vision, university governance, and philanthropic contributions have significantly shaped Yale’s Science Hill, two new residential colleges, and the transformation of the Yale Peabody Museum. But most students have never heard of him. Bass hasn’t named a major building or program after himself (Bass Library was named after his younger brother, Robert), and he has cut a far slimmer public profile than many of Yale’s megadonors, including his peers in the nine figures. Stephen Schwarzman ’69, to name one, is the namesake of Yale’s Schwarzman Center, and Charles B. Johnson ’54 funded Yale’s field hockey stadium, Johnson Field, and the Brady-Johnson Program in Grand Strategy. Bass did not respond to interview requests made by email, U.S. mail, phone calls, or through the chief of security of one of his companies, Sundance Square. His quiet, “behind-the-scenes” work at Yale begs the question: Why does Ed Bass care about Yale? And how has he shaped the university in the last three decades? Ed Bass sits on a pony with Sid Richardson at the left and Perry Bass at the right. (1947) Photo from Fort Worth Star-Telegram archives. Ed Bass was born in 1945, the second son of Perry Bass ’37, a Fort Worth wildcatter who had worked for his uncle, the legendary oil tycoon Sid Richardson. Ed enrolled at Yale in the fall of 1963 — his mother told the New York Times Magazine in 1991 that his father “so enjoyed his Yale education that he said [the sons] could go to Yale—or they could go to work." When Richardson died in 1959, Bass inherited $2.8 million. Bass’s time at Yale coincided with a major shift in Yale’s demographics. Starting in the mid-60s, Yale president Kingman Brewster, Jr. overhauled the school’s admissions, recruiting “students from a much broader part of the United States,” as opposed to its traditional base of New England private schools, according to longtime Yale administrator Sam Chauncey ’57. “The face of Yale is changing really, very dramatically in the four years [Ed] was here.” Bass’s class of 1967 was the first where private school students weren’t the majority, but their influence still pervaded the school when Bass arrived. Four Yalies who overlapped with Bass recalled the divide on campus between public and private school students, who were then called “preppies.” Bass had come to Yale as a millionaire “preppie” from one of Yale’s feeders, Phillips Andover Academy. But classmates don’t remember him keeping with the Andover crowd. Joe Cohen ’67, a fellow classmate in his residential college Saybrook, recalled that Bass did not fit the prep-school stereotype. “There were many preppies that were very distinctive as preppies and he was not one of them. You know, even his dress, I don't remember him as being a sort of J-Press type guy,” referring to the upscale clothing store typically associated with prep-school students at the time. “I wasn't aware of him being a millionaire. He was very discreet and also open to all students.” Although Bass entered with the class of 1967, he deferred graduation until 1968, according to an entry in the Yale Class of 1967 50th Reunion Class Book. In his senior year, he joined a secret society—so secret, apparently, that fellow member Jack Emmons ’68 wouldn’t tell me its name—primarily composed of engineering and administrative science majors, like Emmons and Bass. “It wasn't quite like Skull and Bones or that sort of thing,” Emmons said. “It was much lower key.” Immediately after graduation, Bass served in the US Coast Guard for two years before returning to Yale for his master’s degree in architecture in 1970. He never finished that degree, dropping out because, according to his entry in his 50th Yale reunion class book, he “embarked on a summer building project from which I never returned.” Ed Bass, left, from a still in the documentary Spaceship Earth, about the members of Synergia Ranch. The summer building project was a house in Cedar Crest, New Mexico, according to Mark Nelson, a former friend who met Bass while doing landscaping for the project. At the time, Nelson lived at Synergia Ranch, a countercultural commune near Santa Fe inspired by the ecological philosopher Buckminster Fuller. Bass “got very turned on about the vision” of Synergia Ranch, said Nelson, and moved there in 1973. At Synergia, Bass worked the ranch’s land, studied its ecology, and toured with the ranch’s experimental theater troupe, the Theater of All Possibilities , in the 1970s and 1980s. In 1973, Bass also funded the Institute of Ecotechnics, a charity which supported Synergia’s ecologically-minded construction projects. Nelson ran the charity until 2024. During the 70s and 80s, Bass and his three brothers all invested their inheritance into billion dollar fortunes, although Bass’s three brothers spent more time investing on Wall Street than he would. Cohen, who knew of the Bass family while on Wall Street in the 1970s, said of their business that Ed Bass was on the “periphery,” and did not play a role in the “core Bass family involvement.” Levin agreed, saying that his brother Sid Bass ’65 “was driving the train on the investments.” Sid Bass declined to comment for this article. A 1984 cover of Newsweek portrayed Ed Bass, second from right, in the background of his family’s investments. In 1991, Bass’s father told the New York Times Magazine that at a young age, Ed “told his mother, ‘I never want to be part of the family business. I want to do something by myself.’” Although spending the intervening decades ranching and funding the members of Synergia, he kept his $2.8 million inheritance with the family’s investment company, Bass Brothers Enterprises, where it grew to more than a billion dollars by the 1980s. In 2024, Forbes ranked the Bass family as America’s 36th richest family, and Ed Bass the third-richest Bass brother. “In 1991,” Bass wrote in his class book, “I became reengaged with Yale after not setting foot on campus for 20 years.” The year prior, in May of 1990, Bass had already donated $20 million to Yale, at the time the single largest gift the university had received since 1966. He first returned to Yale’s orbit at the request of his father, Perry Bass, according to Levin. During Yale’s capital campaign in the ‘90s, Perry Bass gave $20 million to Yale to fund the Perry R. Bass Center for Molecular and Structural Biology, and “agreed to try to get his sons to each come up with $20 million,” Levin said. Ed stepped up first, and his 1990 donation created the Yale Institute for Biospheric Studies, which supports environmental science research. His donation was announced one year before his father’s and months before similar $20 million dollar donations from his brothers Sid and Lee. Ed caught the “Yale bug” after this donation, according to Levin. “Over time, it’s led to just one thing after another. Yale became a huge priority for him.” Even at Yale, finding a repeat megadonor is rare. Most of Yale’s largest benefactors have given only one major gift to the university. Since 2000, six donors have committed more than $100 million to Yale. Only two of those have given two major gifts: Ed Bass and Charles B. Johnson, who donated $250 million toward the construction of Yale’s new residential colleges, Benjamin Franklin and Pauli Murray. And even recruiting these donors for one gift isn’t easy. Yale’s previous Vice President for Development and Alumni Affairs Terry Holcombe told the Yale Daily News in 2018 that, for each nine-figure donor, “there are four other potential donors who—despite the University’s best efforts to sell its projects and mission—never give a gift of the same magnitude.” How does Yale infect its wealthiest alumni with the “Yale Bug?” For most Yale donors, the Yale Office of Development—a nearly 300-person department headquartered at the Connecticut Financial Center—is the main point of contact for their philanthropy. Gift officers research potential donors, identifying interests and potential overlaps with Yale’s needs, before soliciting donations. Yale’s senior leadership, especially the provost and president, have a limited role with these smaller donors, who numbered more than 30,000 in Yale’s 2024-25 fiscal year. Donors “with the potential to give $100,000 or more” are often assigned individual gift officers who act as a direct point of contact to solicit donations, according to Joan O’Neill, Yale’s Vice President for Alumni Affairs and Development. David Richards ’67, who donated his $1.4 million Rudyard Kipling collection to Yale, confirmed that he has a development officer assigned to him, as did Len Baker ’64, a former trustee and major donor to Yale. In Bass’s early days giving to Yale, the Office of Development was his primary contact at the university, according to Levin. But throughout the 1990s, and especially after he joined the Yale Corporation in 2001, he began to form closer friendships with two university leaders: Levin and Alison Richard, the former director of the Yale Peabody Museum who served as university provost from 1994 until 2002. Richard declined an interview. For larger donations, Yale’s president does much more personal fundraising, according to Levin, Yale administrator Chauncey, and two major donors. In addition to the “backup work” that the Office of Development does, such as donor research and compiling donor profiles, Yale’s presidents will frequently visit donors at their homes, keeping them personally engaged in their philanthropy. Levin estimated that, during his presidency, he visited Bass twice a year at his home in Fort Worth, in addition to occasional phone calls. President-emeritus Peter Salovey, who succeeded Levin in 2013, wrote in an email that he counted Bass and his wife as “personal friends,” and said that the Basses had stayed at his home during visits to Yale. Last year, Yale president Maurie McInnis, Salovey, and Levin all attended Ed’s 79th birthday party, where his wife Sasha announced the endowed deanship in his name. Richard Levin, Maurie McInnis, Ed Bass, and Peter Salovey, photographed at Bass’s 79th birthday party. Photograph by Tony Fiorini. As Bass continued donating around Yale—including smaller renovations at the Peabody in the 1990s, an estimated $25 million donation to the Class of 1954 Environmental Science Building in 2001, and $60 million toward Science Hill in 2006—Yale returned the favor, appointing him to councils and committees around the university. David Richards, Bass’s classmate and a major Yale donor, explained that it was common for Yale to name big donors to councils or committees related to their philanthropy. “If you're giving at that level, the big, wet kiss back is, ‘All right, you help us make some of the decisions.’” After donating his Kipling collection, Richards said that Yale made him president of the Yale Library Associates, a donor group for the university’s libraries. However, Ed’s service on university committees has gone far beyond most donors, serving on more committees or councils than any of his peers giving nine-figure donations. During his time at Yale, Bass has chaired committees at the School of Forestry & Environmental Studies, the Yale Institute for Biospheric Studies, the Peabody Museum, the Yale Tomorrow capital campaign, the Yale Corporation’s committees on Development and Buildings & Grounds, as well as serving on the School of Architecture’s Dean’s Council and the University Council, an advisory board to the president. He was named as one of the honorary co-chairs of Yale’s current capital campaign, “For Humanity,” in 2021. Describing his service to the university in his 50th reunion class book, published in 2017, Bass wrote that his “involvement progressed to immersion” since the 1990s. “I have served on a score of committees and councils, chairing several and founding a couple...and still serve today on seven committees/councils. I’d say I simply got very lucky.” Len Baker ’64, Charles B. Johnson ’54, and Ed Bass ’68 participate in the 2017 ribbon-cutting ceremony for Yale’s new residential colleges. Photo by Tony Fiorini. By late September of 2009, Yale was in serious trouble. Caught in the crosshairs of the 2008 financial crisis, Yale’s endowment had lost nearly 25% of its value in just one year, the first time it had posted a negative return in more than two decades. Just a year earlier, the university had announced its plans for two new residential colleges, and had earmarked endowment funds to begin the project, according to Len Baker ’64, the trustee who chaired the Corporation’s finance committee at the time. But when Yale’s endowment lost $5.6 billion in one year, those earmarked funds needed to support university operations, leaving the new colleges in limbo. Realizing this, Bass and another trustee personally donated a total of $20 million “to keep the architectural plan development...alive,” according to Baker. Three-quarters of that $20 million was Bass’s. “He kept that alive, and but for that, I think that the thread would have been lost and we would not have new residential colleges,” Baker said. The episode captures Ed Bass’s tendencies as a donor, characterized by those who worked with him as fueled not by his individual interests, but by his enthusiasm for Yale itself. Eight people who’ve worked with Bass told me that rather than throwing his weight around, Bass’s style is to quietly support the institutions he works with. Bass is known to be generally reserved—Levin called him “not a social animal”—and he rarely puts his name on philanthropic projects. In 2017, when he donated $10 million to the Yale Science Building, he requested that its new, 500-seat lecture hall be named after O.C. Marsh, a paleontologist whose fossil collections are now much of the Peabody Museum’s collection. In the case of Kroon Hall, Levin explained that although Richard Kroon ’64 and Bass donated around the same amount, “Rick Kroon got his name on, because Ed didn’t want his name.” The same thing happened with the Class of 1954 Environmental Science Center, where Ed Bass and members of the Yale class of 1954 donated gifts of “about the same size,” Levin said. At the School of Architecture, where Ed Bass has funded a host of programs, including free summer seminars around the world, two professors guessed that most students would have no idea who Bass is. The professors, Bimal Mentis and Joyce Hsiang, had taught the school’s five week summer workshop in Rome, which Bass named after former dean Robert A.M. Stern. Bass’s largest single gift was $160 million toward the renovation of the Yale Peabody Museum, which was the “lion’s share” of the project’s cost, according to David Skelly, the director of the museum. Skelly said that “if you see [Bass’s] name on anything, he almost certainly had to be talked into it.” He cited Bass Court, a courtyard between the Peabody and Kline Geology Laboratory as an example. “He definitely had to be talked into that.” Bass seems to focus on institutional priorities. Levin recalled how he teased Bass about his wish list of projects at Yale. “All of them were, you know, institutional. None of them were like, ‘Go do this thing for me because it’s something I want,’” Levin said. Baker agreed, saying that Bass “did not assert strong views about the university’s educational priorities, about its financial priorities, [or] about its political or ideological views. He just was ready to, ‘tell me what you want and I’ll try to make it happen.’” Yet, when Bass does express an opinion, university leaders listen. On questions of architecture, Yale administrators told me they relied on his judgement more than many donors.
