At the School of Architecture, Berke said that when Bass offers his opinion, “you listen carefully because it is knowledgeable and insightful.” Skelly, the Yale Peabody Museum director, echoed this, telling me that he’s learned that “generally, it's not a good idea to ignore [Bass’s] thinking. Because he's usually right.” Skelly recalled one meeting for the construction of the Yale Science Building, where plans for a new outdoor stairway were being discussed. Bass suggested reorienting the stairway away toward the intersection of Peabody Museum, Kline Geology Lab, and the Class of '54 Environmental Science Center. Skelly said that “the VP of Facilities saw that, and he goes, ‘My God, you’re absolutely right.’” During the Yale Corporation’s discussions on Yale’s new residential colleges, Benjamin Franklin and Pauli Murray, Bass disagreed with former trustee and architect Maya Lin on the colleges’ design, according to Baker, a fellow trustee. Bass and Lin both served on the Buildings & Grounds committee, and Baker said that Lin “didn’t like the residential colleges all that much because she would have had a very different, more modern design.” The committee ultimately chose a Gothic style for the new colleges, designed by Robert A.M. Stern, whom Bass had named the architecture school’s summer workshop after. Lin could not be reached for comment. In a 2018 interview with the New York Times , Bass said “I see institutions as having the power to transmit and perpetuate a set of fundamental values, and to do so generation to generation.” He told the Times that Yale’s long history also encourages him: “It’s been more than 300 years, so I have some faith.” Although his fundamental values may be unclear, many of Bass’s donations to Yale have funded environmental projects, including at the Peabody, the Center for Biospheric Studies, and on Science Hill. But Levin said he wasn’t an activist. “He’s less of an environmentalist than you might think. He’s just much more interested in the science.” Outside of Yale, Bass has also donated $30 million to the University of Arizona, which manages Bass’s Biosphere 2, a massive terrarium made famous for eclectic experiments with members of Synergia Ranch in the 1990s. Biosphere 2 is located 30 miles from the university’s campus. The current director of Biosphere 2, Joaquin Ruiz, told me that Bass invests in universities like the University of Arizona and Yale because “he really believes in their ability to operate and drive forward progress in the things he cares about.” Bass’s particular architectural vision is hard to pin down. He told the New York Times in 1991 that two of his major influences were the Synergia Ranchers and Buckminster Fuller, the intellectual forefather of the ranch and a prominent architect of geodesic domes, but he later fell out with many members of Synergia Ranch over disagreements with Biosphere 2. Architecture dean Berke said she couldn’t align him with a particular architectural movement. “Ed cares passionately about good design...Good design is about...the architect having a vision of how the building hits the ground, how it hits the sky, how it connects to its neighbors and its context, how it talks about history, even as it looks forward,” Berke said. When asked about his favorite building in a 2007 interview for the Yale Alumni Magazine, Bass also couldn’t answer. “There’s so many buildings that I appreciate and admire that maybe I don’t like that much. And others that I love but I see all their faults. I couldn't anymore do that than say who is my favorite family member,” he said. When asked in October if Bass had any big donations left, both Levin and School of Architecture dean Berke declined to offer any specifics, but hinted that Bass was not done giving. This April, the News confirmed his next big gift, which has not been previously reported and Yale has not yet publicly announced. This May, the Yale School of Architecture will tell its alumni about the Bass Challenge, a matching gift to the school’s financial aid budget which will allow its students to graduate debt-free, according to Berke. If the school manages to raise $20 million from its alumni for this challenge, Ed Bass and his wife Sasha will donate the remaining $40 million needed to eliminate loans from students’ financial aid packages. Berke said the Bass gift will be the largest that the school has ever received.

title: Under the Lilies
author: Cynthia Medina
text: This piece won 1st place in the fiction category of the 2026 Wallace Prize. The first year, my daughter tried to pick the lilies. She was four and furious with her father for not letting her take anything home from me. She stood on the damp edge of my grave in white shoes already going gray at the toes, tugging at his hand with both of hers as if force alone might loosen him from the silence he had brought there. The morning was bright in that merciless way spring can be, all soft air and wet earth and the smell of things returning. I had been dead eleven months. The lilies were fresh, thick-throated, and opening anyway. “Why can’t we take them?” she asked. Her father looked down at her, then back at the flowers, as if one of us might give him the answer. He had shaved too quickly that morning. There was still a pale line of cream near his jaw. I noticed things like that now. The dead do not leave all at once. First the body goes, then the voice, then the small untidy evidence of living. What remains is stranger: a man with my name still folded inside him like a note he cannot throw away, a little girl who does not remember my face whole and misses me all the same. “They’re for Mom,” he said. She frowned. “But she’s not using them.” He almost smiled. I watched it arrive and fail. A breeze moved through the cemetery and the lilies bent together. I had hated lilies when I was alive. They smelled like flowers for rooms where no one got better. Now he brought lilies because people had brought lilies on the day they lowered me into the ground, and grief is lazy with its symbols. My daughter crouched by the stone and pressed one finger into the mud. One button on her cardigan sat in the wrong hole, and the ribbon at the end of her braid had come half-loose on the walk from the car. He had learned to braid after I died. Badly at first, with the concentration of a man trying to build a bridge out of string. “Can Mom see these?” she asked, tapping the stem of the nearest lily. Her father was quiet. Children notice silence faster than adults do. She lifted her face to him, impatient already, her brow pinched in a way so familiar it seemed for a moment that I had been given back something bodily and impossible. “Daddy?” “Yes,” he said finally, but the word came out rough. “Yes, baby. I think she can.” She leaned closer to the stone. For a long moment she said nothing. Then, very softly, as if she was not sure what volume the dead required, she whispered, “Hi, Mom.” He no longer sang in the kitchen, not even by accident. But he had kept the rituals. He still cut the crusts from her sandwiches because I used to. He still warmed her towel in the dryer after baths. He still kissed the top of her head every time she passed him in the hallway, as though he were blessing something I had left behind. My daughter touched the carved letters of my name with one damp finger. “Was she pretty?” Something inside him seemed to stop. “Yes,” he said. “Very.” “What did she look like?” He knelt in the wet grass so they were eye level. “She had your nose,” he said. This was false, but grief makes revisionists of us. He was trying to give her something. “And your hair,” he added. That was truer. “She laughed loud,” he said. “Louder than anybody.” My daughter smiled. “Like me?” His face changed then. “Yes,” he said. “Like you.” Then she asked, “Do you think she would know me?” The world did not stop, but it ought to have. The breeze went on moving. The lilies went on nodding. Her father lowered his eyes. He pressed his thumb once against the inside of her wrist, where the pulse beats thin and fast. “You are the part of her that stayed,” he said. She did not fully understand, but children know when something matters by the way adults go still around it. She stepped forward and put both arms around his neck, nearly knocking him sideways in the mud. He laughed then, but only once, and held her with such force that for a moment I felt the old animal panic of love, that to care for anyone is to stand forever at the edge of losing them. I could not touch them. This remained the indignity of death. To see and not be seen was one thing. To love and not be able to lay a hand on what one loved was another. Before they left, she picked up a fallen petal and held it in her palm. The knowledge came then, thin and sharp: I had already begun to vanish from the child I loved most, and she would survive me by doing exactly that. The petal stayed in her hand all the way home. Before I died, before lilies and wet earth and my name cut into stone, there was a Tuesday in June when I burned the grilled cheese. Nothing about the day announced itself as worth keeping. Its importance did not arrive in costume. It came in half-sliced fruit, damp dish towels, the sound of someone you love opening the front door and calling your name from the other room. Our daughter was still small enough then to believe every kitchen should belong to her. She sat on the counter in nothing but a diaper and one yellow sock, drumming her heels against the cabinet while I moved between stove and sink, pushing a pan too hot with the side of my hip because both my hands were busy. There were strawberries in a colander. There was a cartoon voice talking nonsense from the next room. There was a glass of water on the windowsill I had forgotten to finish, collecting a crescent of afternoon light. “One day,” he said from the doorway, “I’m going to count them.” “You’ll lose track before the hallway,” I said. He leaned there watching us as if he still could not believe the life was his. Our daughter saw him and shrieked, then reached both arms out with such confidence it did not occur to her he might fail to catch her. He crossed the kitchen in three strides and took her off the counter with a grunt, pressing his mouth to her neck until she kicked and squealed and slapped his cheek with her sticky hand. “You smell like outside,” I said. “You smell like smoke,” he answered. I turned too late. The sandwich had gone dark on one side, black at the corners. “Oh, damn it.” He laughed. “That’s the third one this month.” “It is not.” “It is, and I have witnesses.” Our daughter, traitor that she was, clapped. “Give it here,” he said, reaching for the spatula. “No. You always press too hard.” “It’s a grilled cheese, not open-heart surgery.” “That is exactly why you shouldn’t manhandle it.” He smiled at that, the slow one that began on only one side of his mouth. I loved him most, maybe, in moments like that, not when he was trying, but when affection escaped him by accident. Our daughter had found the wooden spoon and was banging it against his shoulder with all the solemn violence of the very young. He caught it from her and handed her a strawberry instead. She bit into it, then frowned down at the red juice on her wrist. “Sticky,” she said. “That’s the price,” I told her. “For what?” “For wanting something sweet.” The radio by the fridge was murmuring some song I no longer remember, only that it was soft and that my husband knew the words badly and sang anyway. He said, “I have a question.” “That’s never a good opening.” “Would you still love me if I got a boat?” “No.” He looked wounded. “That was immediate.” “A boat is how a man announces he means to become impossible.” “Plenty of reasonable men have boats.” “No,” I said, flipping the sandwich, “they have boating personalities, which is different.” Our daughter laughed because we did, though she understood none of it. He kissed the top of her head. “What if it was a small boat?” “That’s worse.” This time he laughed hard enough to bend forward. Our daughter joined him, delighted, and for a moment the kitchen was full of us. “Mommy,” our daughter said, holding up her sticky fingers. “Wet.” I took the dish towel from my shoulder and wiped her hand clean, each finger one by one. She leaned into me without thinking, warm and trusting and heavy with lunch and afternoon. Later that afternoon she fell asleep on the couch with one sock still on and the other lost somewhere beneath her. I sat beside her and smoothed the damp hair from her forehead. My husband stood in the doorway holding the small abandoned sock between two fingers like evidence from a crime. “Do you think she’ll always be this sweet?” he asked. “No,” I said. “I think she’ll become a person.” “That sounds ominous.” “It is. For us.” He came to stand behind me, resting one hand on the back of my neck. That touch, casual and exact, belonged only to us. He looked down at our daughter sleeping, then at me. “What?” I asked. “Nothing.” “That’s never true.” He smiled. “I was just thinking how lucky I am.” I reached back and took his wrist, kissed the inside of it without looking. “You should be,” I said. He laughed softly through his nose, and for a moment no one moved. Our daughter breathed between us in the long, even rhythm of sleep. If I had known how little of her I would get, I would have lain down beside her. I would have breathed with her until she woke. I would have memorized the exact weight of her against the couch cushion, the soft crease near her mouth, the smell of jam and milk and sun-warmed skin at the back of her neck. I would have turned and looked at my husband until he was embarrassed by it. Instead I rose to find the other sock. I did not understand, while I was alive, how quickly a life could become administrative. There are grand words people use afterward, the ones that stand upright in the mouths of doctors and relatives and priests. Complication. Emergency. Loss. Tragedy. They are not false. They are simply too clean for what happens inside a room when a body begins to fail. The truth is smaller. A nurse moving fast without running. A cup of water left sweating on a tray beside a bed no one is looking at. A husband nodding as if he understands. A pair of socks folded in the hospital bag because someone believed there would still be time for socks. Our daughter was three when I died. That is old enough to have loved me. Not old enough to keep me. She had a cold that week, the harmless kind, all damp cough and hot cheeks and the unreasonable misery of toddlers. On the third morning, when her fever broke, my husband brought her to the hospital in leggings and a sweater with one cuff chewed at the wrist. She came in with my mother’s hand in hers and a stuffed rabbit by one ear, dragging the other ear over the floor. I remember very little in order. The room was too bright. My husband’s face looked unlike itself. “Mommy,” she said, with the terrible ease of someone certain she belongs to you. She climbed carefully onto the bed when he lifted her, one knee, then the other, then both hands pressing into the sheet by my hip to steady herself. “Hi, bug,” I said. My voice sounded wrong to me, as if it were coming from the bottom of a well. She looked at the tape on the back of my hand, the clear line running from it to the bag on the pole. “What’s that?” “Hospital spaghetti,” I said. My husband laughed once, unwillingly. She accepted this answer with the dignity of the barely persuaded. Then she leaned down and put her cheek against my chest. For one second, one merciful, unbearable second, the room righted itself around that weight. Our daughter lifted her head and looked at me closely. “You look weird,” she said. I smiled. “That’s rude.” “You do.” “She’s right,” my husband said softly. I turned my eyes toward him. “Don’t start.” He looked at the floor, smiling despite himself. I think now that this was our last ordinary exchange. “Can you come home now?” she asked. I looked at my husband first. “Not yet,” I said. “When?” “Soon.” It was a lie. There are moments when hope and dishonesty become impossible to separate. She accepted it because she was three. She put the stuffed rabbit on my stomach and patted it there as if leaving me company. My husband came to the bedside then and brushed her hair back from her face. His hand shook once before going still. “You should let Mommy rest,” he said. “No.” “Just for a little.” “No.” He looked at me. Even now, dead as I am, I do not know whether the greater anguish was leaving them or watching him begin to understand that I was. My daughter laid her hand over mine, very carefully, because she had already learned from the room that something about me was breakable. “Mommy?” she said. “Yes?” “When I’m big, will I be big like you?” The question entered me with such force I thought for a moment I had been given back a whole living body just so it could split again. “Yes,” I said. Then the room changed. An urgency in other people’s bodies. A nurse saying my name in a voice I immediately hated. My mother taking our daughter off the bed so quickly she began to cry. “No,” she said, twisting in my mother’s arms. “No, no, no.” “It’s okay,” someone said. Nothing makes me angrier than that phrase. My husband had moved to my side. He was saying something to me, but by then the room had become a place I was already leaving, and his words reached me only in pieces. My name. Stay with me. Look at me. The sort of sentences spoken by people who still think love is leverage. I tried. Then I was above it. Not in the theatrical sense. No bright tunnel, no revelation. Just an angle newly wrong. The room below me, my own body in it, too still now to be mistaken for sleeping. My mother bent almost double around our daughter, who was screaming with all the righteous betrayal of the very young. My husband with one hand braced on the bed rail, the other still reaching toward me as if late were only another form of distance. The dead do not become wise. We become helpless first. Helplessness, I learned, is only rage with nowhere to go. I saw him understand. Then he made a sound I have thought of since as grief’s first true language. Afterward came the administration of loss. Papers. A signature. A woman with a careful voice asking questions no one should have to answer under fluorescent lighting. My husband carrying my bag though there was no reason to carry it anymore. Inside were the folded socks, my toothbrush, a packet of gum, the blue cardigan I had meant to wear home. My daughter asked for me that night just once. Later he stood in the doorway of her room while she kicked free of the blanket and asked, already half asleep, “Where’s Mommy?” He opened his mouth. Nothing came out. She waited, eyelids heavy. “In the hospital?” she guessed. There are losses so complete the body makes a smaller story just to live through them. “Yes,” he said. She nodded. Accepted it. Rolled onto her side with the rabbit against her throat. Then he went into our bathroom and learned, all at once, the full stupidity of objects. My toothbrush by the sink. My damp towel still hooked behind the door. A strand of my hair caught in the teeth of the comb. He sat on the closed toilet lid with my hairbrush in his lap and cried into one of my sweaters until he could not breathe through his nose. The next morning our daughter said, with the serene cruelty of children, “My braid is wrong.” It wasn’t even a braid yet, only two uneven parts and one side clipped back badly. He had tried before daycare and failed. He came around the table, knelt behind her chair, and began again. His fingers moved clumsily through her hair. Too tight, then too loose. She winced. “You’re pulling.” “I know.” “You’re doing it wrong.” “I know.” His voice was gentle, but beneath it I heard the new thing grief had made in him, a rawness always one inch below the skin. Then she lifted one hand and laid it on his wrist. “Okay,” she said. He stopped. Then he finished the braid, badly. One side lower than the other. The part wandering. When he was done, he kissed the top of her head. She let him. That is how the house continued without me. Not nobly. Not beautifully. In cereal and loose clips and lies told at bedtime because the truth was too large to hand a child all at once. In the humiliating little expertise survival demands. But even then, in those first raw days, something of me had already gone forward. In my daughter’s damp head bent over cereal. In my husband’s ruined braid. In the hand she laid on his wrist as if she understood, dimly, that one of them would have to learn how to stay. By the seventh spring, my daughter no longer needed help getting out of the car. She was ten then, all elbows and sharp opinions and the first early signs of privacy, that new and terrible country children enter when they begin to understand that part of their life belongs to them alone. She opened the back door before her father could come around to her side, shouldered her own bag, and stood for a moment in the parking lot squinting into the light with one hand lifted to her brow. But the dead become students of recurrence. That was mine, the squint. Just the motion, brief and unconscious, as if sunlight were an inconvenience she would not grant the dignity of complaint. Her father saw it too. I could tell by the way he stopped with the keys still in his hand. She looked back at him. “What?” “Nothing.” “That means something.” He smiled a little. “You did that like her.” She frowned, then repeated it deliberately, more exaggerated this time, and laughed when he laughed. “Well,” she said, “I guess she had one good idea.” I loved her then with such violence that, if love were a force capable of rearranging matter, the whole cemetery would have split open. The lilies had come again. This year the lilies were taller, their scent thick and overripe. Flowers for rooms where no one gets better. He had told her that story, I knew, because as they walked up the path she said, “I still think that’s dramatic.” “She was dramatic.” “No,” my daughter said. “You’re dramatic when you talk about her. She sounds funny.” He glanced down at her. “She was funny.” “Not the way you say it.” She stepped around a patch of mud, careful without appearing to be. “You always talk about her like she was made of candlelight. But every time you tell a real story, she sounds kind of mean.” He barked out a laugh before he could help it. “I was not mean,” I said, offended in principle. “You know,” she continued, warming to the subject, “the parking thing, and the flowers, and how she said your boat idea was stupid.” His face opened then into something I had not seen on him in years, not only amusement, but relief. “She didn’t say stupid,” he said. “She implied it.” “She did imply it.” At the grave, she did not sit immediately the way she had in earlier years. She stood reading the stone, though by then she knew every word of it. Her father spread the blanket before she could comment. It was the blue one. Faded now at the fold lines. One corner torn and restitched by hand. He had remembered. Then she reached into her bag and took out a book. When she was little, she had wanted toys at the grave, snacks, a ribbon. Now she brought a book. Thick, secondhand, the spine already cracked white in two places. She set it in her lap without opening it. Her father noticed. “What’s that?” She shrugged. “Just something I’m reading.” “Let me see.” “No.” After a minute she sighed and handed it over. He looked at the cover. Then at her. “You brought your mother Anne of Green Gables?” She gave him a look of immediate annoyance. “It’s not your mother Anne of Green Gables. It’s a library book.” “You hated that book when I read it to you.” “I was six.” “And now?” She looked away, out toward the path. “Now I think she would’ve liked Anne.” The sentence entered the world so lightly neither of them seemed, at first, to understand what it had done to me. “What makes you think that?” he asked. She shrugged again, but her voice had gone quieter. “She sounds like someone Mom would’ve liked.” He handed the book back carefully. “She would have,” he said. “I know,” my daughter said. She opened the book then, turning to a page already marked with a receipt, and rested her palm over the paper for a moment without reading. One thumbnail was bitten raw at the corner, exactly as mine used to be. I had not expected inheritance to be so small, or so exact. Just a bitten thumbnail. A dry joke. A girl who picked the books I would have picked without knowing why. After a while she said, still looking at the page, “Sometimes I do something and it feels like I’m remembering for no reason.” Her father did not answer immediately. “What do you mean?” She turned a page she had not read. “Like when I got mad at that florist because the flowers smelled too sweet. Or when Mrs. Bell said I laugh with my whole shoulders. Or when I know what kind of story I’ll like before I read it.” She stopped. “It feels like missing someone and finding them at the same time.” Her father looked out over the cemetery instead of at her. “That sounds right,” he said. She closed the book in her lap. “Sometimes I try to think of her face,” she said. “And I only get the picture by the stairs.” He was quiet. “If I forget her face,” she went on, “is that how she disappears?” He bent his head. “No.” She waited. “No,” he said again, more steadily. “That only means you were too young to keep what should have been yours.” Something in me broke open at that. She looked at him then, her face small and fierce. “Why do I miss her if I don’t remember her?” He closed his eyes. “Because love gets to us before memory does,” he said. She stared at him. Then, with the ruthless clarity children sometimes have, she asked, “Do you miss her all the time?” He smiled in a way that was not a smile. “No,” he said. “Not all the time.” She waited. “But every day.” She placed the book beside her on the blanket and drew one knee up, wrapping both arms around it. “I used to be scared I’d forget everything,” she said. He waited. “And now?” She shrugged. “Now I think maybe remembering everything isn’t the point.” He turned to her then. “What is?” She took longer to answer this than any question before. “Maybe,” she said, “it’s that some of it stays anyway.” He looked at her with the stunned, almost frightened expression of a person who has just watched a child step over some invisible threshold. Then he did something he had not done there in years. He cried. Not violently. Not all at once. Just a sudden ungoverned breaking at the face. He covered his mouth with one hand and turned away, embarrassed even alone with her. She stared. Then, in one movement so familiar I felt it like weather through bone, she shifted closer and put her hand on the back of his neck. Casual. Exact. My touch. He bent forward, laughing once through the tears because the alternative was worse, and she left her hand there, steady and unafraid. “It’s okay,” she said. Nothing, in all the years since my death, had undone me more than those words. He drew a long breath and nodded. “I know,” he said, though of course he didn’t. A breeze moved through the cemetery, and one of the lilies, too open now, gave way. A white petal loosened, then another, then another, falling soundlessly into the grass. When my daughter noticed, she stood. For one moment I thought she would leave it there. But instead she bent over the bouquet. With the grave care of someone tending a wound that cannot be closed, she lifted one broken stem and tucked it gently back among the others, where it would not hold for long but held, for the moment, well enough. Then she looked at my name. Not at the dates. Not at the inscription. Just my name, plain in stone. And she smiled, not sadly, not brightly, just with the quiet recognition one gives something once lost and now found in altered form. When they left, she took the book. I watched them walk back through the gate, not touching now, but moving with the same brief hesitation at the curb. Nothing the earth loves does it keep forever. I had wanted more than this. More years. More mornings. We had not asked for miracles. Only the usual things. To grow older in the same house. To watch our daughter become someone we might not entirely understand. To complain about our knees and forget where we parked. To sit beside each other in the long, unremarkable light of late afternoons. Maybe grandchildren. Maybe separate chairs and the same silence. The kind of future people call ordinary because they have not yet had it taken. Instead I remained this way, in gestures and weather and the borrowed architecture of those who had loved me. Not living. Not gone. Only carried.

title: A Phantom Lineage
author: Yevgeniya Regent
text: This piece won 1st place in the nonfiction category of the 2026 Wallace Prize. We say in Ukraine that children usually grow up with one of two problems: not having a father, or having one. After an amputation, the brain often refuses to acknowledge the loss, creating the sensation that the hand is still present—a condition known as Phantom Limb Syndrome. Though the limb is gone, the brain’s sensory and motor maps remain active, generating sensations of movement, pressure, or pain in the missing part. Some patients describe a phantom fist they can’t unclench, nails digging into a palm that no longer exists, with pain as vivid as if the hand were still there. The brain retains this cortical map for years, sometimes decades, reaching for what’s gone, trying to soothe a part of the self that can no longer respond. *** Dear Dad, there’s something sickening about how we are now. Sometimes I close my eyes and step into your body, walking through my childhood apartment—your home—alone. A place built for four now holding one. Twenty years spent creating a life with two daughters and a wife—then everyone scattered in different directions. My sister hasn’t seen you since she was eight. Her memory of you is evaporating—soon she won’t remember what it was like to have a father at all. Sometimes I wonder if I study the human mind just to understand how forgetting works, or why something still bleeds when it comes to you. You haven’t changed anything in the apartment. My bed is still covered with the blue blanket you spread after I left. You escape the house only at five in the morning or after dark, slipping through the streets so no one can hand you a draft notice. I picture you there, pacing between the same walls, the same chairs, the same silence. And sometimes I cry about you. I don’t cry about other things anymore. Last time we talked, I was in the Czech Republic. With friends you’ve never met, I walked through Lidice—a village erased from the map by the Nazis. So I wouldn’t cry, I told you about the cameras I’d been using, the film work I was doing. You gave me my first camera. You taught me how to see the world through a viewfinder. You always seemed so far ahead of me in your hobby. Standing there, I realized I know more than you now. *** Patronymics—names built from a father’s name—run through ancient Indo-European cultures. Early Slavic societies identified people as “X, son/daughter of Y” centuries before surnames existed. Under the Russian Empire, patronymics became mandatory in church records, censuses, and legal documents. The Soviet Union intensified this insistence: every passport, birth certificate, school file, and military form required a patronymic. When a father was unknown, officials fabricated one, from a name the mother picked out or a bureaucratic placeholder, so the structure itself remained intact. Independent Ukraine retained the tripartite formula: given name, patronymic, surname. *** Dear Dad, I flinch when someone asks if you’re safe. I never know what to say. Why can’t you call? It feels childish to think you don’t care, but I can’t convince myself you do. I’m turning twenty. You last knew me as a fifteen-year-old girl—still soft around the edges, still yours in ways I’m not anymore. I always loved your name. It was the only reason my stubborn, feminist self accepted being called Yevgeniya Vitaliivna. Your name felt like a thread tying us together. Now no one calls me that in New England. *** During the upheavals of the 1920s and 1930s, girls were the first to be sent away. After the 1921–23 famine and civil war, the Soviet state counted between 4 and 7.5 million homeless children. By 1934, after collectivization and the Holodomor, officials registered over 600,000 new juvenile vagrants and famine orphans in a single year, many with living parents. Families could not feed their daughters, so they sent them to shelters, to distant relatives, to cities where the state might keep them alive. Between 1928 and 1938, enrollment of girls in urban technikums tripled. By the late 1930s, girls made up more than 40% of vocational students—thousands lifted from villages and deposited in cities with suitcases, state papers, and no way home. *** My great-grandmother, Anna Yefimovna, was born in 1921, in a village where the house always felt too small for the number of children inside it. She grew up working the fields as collectivization rearranged the country around her. Then came the Holodomor, Stalin’s engineered famine, and her father, Yefim, sold their house for survival. He took a railway job for rations, a line between hunger and death, disappearing for days at a time. At fourteen, Anna was sent to Kharkiv to train as a nurse, an eighteen-hour journey away from everything familiar. She ran away again and again. Each time she returned, her family fed her, let her rest, and in the morning, Yefim woke her gently, walked her to the station, and put her back on the train. She survived the war as a front-line surgical nurse. He fought as a soldier. When the war was finally over, she moved to Kyiv and left her entire family behind. My grandmother, Svetlana Leonidovna, was born in 1945, the daughter of a Red Army general named Leonid. Peace had barely arrived when a wealthy woman in Belarus decided she wanted Leonid for herself. She threatened to have my great-grandmother killed. Not long after, he was reassigned, conveniently, to Belarus. My grandmother met him only once as an adult. The woman greeted her with a strange remorse: God punished me for taking away your father, she whispered. Her own child had severe autism. My mother, Yuliya Vladislavovna, arrived in 1977. She grew up without the softness childhood should have had. She never met her own father—Vladislav, a mathematician, a scientist. She believes her intelligence and analytic mind come from him. When she turned eighteen, they talked on the phone once. She said it felt like talking to a stranger whose voice held no place for her. Half your genes do not make a father. *** Studies in developmental psychobiology suggest attachment isn’t only learned through behavior; it’s also carried through physiology. A parent’s stress biology shapes the intrauterine environment long before birth, altering fetal HPA-axis calibration and producing infants with elevated baseline cortisol and faster sympathetic activation. These physiological settings become the scaffolding for later attachment: bodies tuned for danger often form anxious bonds, while bodies accustomed to passionate scarcity lean toward avoidance. Epigenetic research adds another layer. Chronic stress in one generation modifies DNA methylation in genes regulating stress response and social bonding, and these marks can persist in offspring. A child may therefore inherit not just a parent’s temperament but their vigilance, their difficulty soothing, even their reflex to withdraw. *** Ukrainian fatherhood was shaped by geopolitics long before it was ever allowed to be a choice. From Kyivan Rus onward, this land was crossed, claimed, emptied, and refilled by successive powers: Kyivan Rus campaigns, Mongol invasions, centuries of Polish–Lithuanian rule, Ottoman raids, serfdom, imperial rule under the Habsburgs and Romanovs, Tsarist conscription, two World Wars, Stalinist collectivization, genocide and famine, camps and mass deportations, Nazi forced labor (Ostarbeiter), economic collapse, now full-scale russian invasion. Generation after generation, men left, while women stayed to ensure survival, gradually solidifying as the durable center of the household. Men, by contrast, learned to be temporary. In a structure built to endure absence, attachment is dangerous. Outside the home, masculinity comes with instructions: work, suffer, sacrifice, endure. Inside, there is no inherited language for intimacy or care. Alcohol steps in as a solvent: a cheap amnesia that dulls the shame of powerlessness in a society that insists men must hold power somewhere. What looks like personal failure—a father who withdraws, drinks, vanishes—is often the residue of historical training that taught Ukrainian families to function without a man. ​​*** My mom once told me that growing up, she never wished for a good husband; she wished for a good father for her children. My father wanted to be that. I’d grown up hearing that fathers either vanish or rot in place. I believed he was an exception. He played football with me in the yard, sledded through winters that seemed to last longer than the calendar allowed. He waited outside my dance lessons in the car, letting me play games on his Nokia phone when I cried. We snorkeled for crabs in Crimea. We played chess, and somehow I always won. One day, I begged him to buy a baguette—an unprecedented request in our health-obsessed family. When we got home and realized we’d left it behind at the store, I broke down—full, shameless tears. So he sliced ordinary bread, fried it with garlic and salt, set it in front of me with a small flourish, and declared it Father’s baguette. He never told me what I could or couldn’t be, never forced me to act like a girl, never belittled or shamed me. When I grew too loud, too restless, he pressed his precious camera into my hands. Go photograph something . He helped me build my sails and stood behind me, blowing to create wind. My sister was born, and he did everything: diapers, long stroller walks, his fat-free borscht, and entire afternoons crawling with her around our home. In turn, school consumed me. I aligned myself with my mother’s academic gravity and let my father drift to the edges of the apartment. I practiced independence, telling him I didn’t need help: I could walk to school alone; I could take care of my sister; I could manage without him. He began disappearing on long trips to the grocery store. When he came back, he complained that my mother was too commanding; that she had pushed his friends out of his life; that she had forced him to resign from the army; that she was turning me against him; that we didn’t appreciate him; that my sister was growing up watching a weak male figure. Then came the flask in his pocket. The cognac bottles that migrated across the counter. The arguments. The plates breaking. When my mother and I agreed I should apply to U.S. schools, he didn’t protest; he retreated to his corner of the apartment and disappeared. I was relieved. With him gone, the air felt calmer, the fighting thinner, and the days around them felt easier to survive. After I was accepted, I told them I wouldn’t leave unless they tried to patch their marriage together—at least for my sister. Over winter break, I returned home. Everything felt slightly misaligned, as if the apartment had shifted without me. Now, I spoke a language they didn’t understand, in a country they had never been to; I had already begun living in a world they could not even picture. He took me to the airport at 1 a.m. We played Folklore —the CD I’d forced him to burn. He drove slowly so I could sleep. At the terminal, he hugged me too tightly, then released me. I watched him dissolve into the crowd. A month later, russia launched the full-scale invasion. I haven’t seen him since. These days, my mother and I sit in a kitchen in Germany and talk too much. Martial law keeps my father from leaving Ukraine; he turns off his phone and disappears for weeks at a time, ignoring us. She yells at him—her husband now mostly in name—across weak connections. Text me every morning, so I know you’re alive . She asks if he’s been bombed, if he has electricity. He periodically replies with one word: norm . Between classes and my constant rush of college life, my mom texts me, How are you? My thumbs type automatically: norm. *** Roughly 20.05 million Soviet males were killed in World War II; around 8-11 million were soldiers, almost all of child-raising age. This left 7 to 9 million children fatherless. Post-war instability created a second category: fathers who were alive but absent. In 1945, 24.4% of Soviet births were extramarital. Between 1945 and 1968, over 15 million children received birth certificates with a dash where the father’s name should be. By 1957, 3.3 million single mothers were receiving state support. The Soviet Union produced more than 20 million fatherless children after the war. Roughly 35 to 40% of children in independent Ukraine—well over two million—are growing up without a father present. Since 2014, and especially after the full-scale invasion in 2022, tens of thousands have lost fathers to combat, while hundreds of thousands more live in prolonged separation, displaced abroad with their mothers while fathers remain in Ukraine under martial law. *** Recently, my Ukrainian friends and I sat on a beach, joking about everything we shouldn’t—the war, corruption, politicians, the strange fact of having survived at all. Then someone said father , and the music went quiet. We are students at Yale—the bright, guiding stars of Ukraine, pulled from different regions, all supposedly destined for success. Mine calls me a slut. Mine left my mother pregnant with my brother. We hide my location from mine—he doesn’t know I go to Yale. We waded into the water and cried. All of us carry the same inheritance. All of them hit us, or vanished, or both. All of them are no longer spoken to. *** In the absence of sensory input, the brain does not fall silent; it improvises. Motor and somatosensory regions keep firing toward an empty space, generating pressure, movement, and ache. The system keeps sending messages with no target to receive them—a closed loop with no closure. Biology rarely accepts loss. The brain would rather be wrong than empty: it will invent sensation, even pain, rather than admit that what it loved is gone. *** Dear Dad, I block you out of my mind to survive. I don’t know if we could ever exist in a space together again. But I see you in me. The broad shoulders. The brown eyes. The snub nose. The stubbornness. The avoidance. The impulse to run. The talent for leaving first. I won’t call you. You won’t call me. We’re both built this way. Some absences the mind never rewrites.

title: San Sebastián
author: Ingrid Rodriguez Vila
text: This piece won 2nd place in the fiction category of the 2026 Wallace Prize. I made Lino stand against a transit sign at the corner where the Cristo and San Sebastián streets met. The sharp, rusty pole, he made sure to complain, made for an extremely uncomfortable resting place. The mosaic embedded in the yellow building behind him hung over his head like a Pentecostal flame: a young man, traced over a small rectangle of creamy tile in the watery brushstrokes characteristic of Old San Juan street mosaics, pinned to an equally-watery tree by two faint arrows sticking out of his chest. A missing tile near the saint’s feet gave the impression of a perfectly square puddle of yellow sunlight. Though he oversaw one of the most heavily transited bottlenecks in the colonial city, funneling people from the Plaza Colón to sift into the many colorful bars further up the street, no one had bothered to touch San Sebastián up for his feast day. The air was flush with morning, and a crowd was just beginning to coalesce on the street below. Locals and tourists alike stumbled around Lino like bumblebees drunk on nectar. From ramshackle wooden stands, vendors hawked products ranging from rum-vodka-tequila cocktails in bright tropical colors to little wooden saints, contorted in various types of distress—skinned, skewered, riddled with arrows. San Sebastián was powerless to chase any merchants from his temple. Perhaps he didn’t care to. Despite the pain, he kept his eyes trained stubbornly upward, his expression defiantly bored. Lino also looked bored as he leaned against the metal pole, balancing a yard-long plastic tube of sangria in his right hand and a giant fried bacalaito in his left. Subtle tan lines circled his bare arms. A tiny silver hoop was embedded in his right ear. Preteen acne had left his cheeks peppered with pushpin craters. In short, my twin brother was an archetypal eighteen-year-old boy from Río Piedras. Only his newest wounds, fresher than his canonized counterpart’s, differentiated him from the local youths who gawked as they tipsily rounded the corner. Two arrows stuck out of wet, fleshy entrance lesions on his chest—globs of flour-infused Vaseline, colored red. Another arrow, cleaved in his upper left thigh, dripped red food coloring down his beige shorts and onto the bright blue cobblestone below. The bacalaito, purchased to appease Lino’s hunger, and the sangria, purchased to appease his ludicrous conviction that one could only wash a bacalaito down with sangria, had proven a fortuitous addition to a gorgeous frame. Through my camera’s viewfinder, I saw a martyr, as stubbornly modern as he was hopelessly antiquated, who proved little more than an oddity to the crowd that had made the anniversary of his violent death the biggest party of the Christmas season. It was a nearly flawless image. “Stop drinking the sangria!” I shouted at Lino from across the street, peeking over the viewfinder. “You’re already halfway through it!” Lino, who had been absentmindedly sipping his blood-red drink, stuck his middle finger out at me while keeping an awkward grip on the cup. ——— The first vision had come in late December—the day after Martín and I broke up. While making molasses cookies, I accidentally tapped my knee to the closing oven door, and immediately felt the brush of petal-textured fingers over what should have reddened into an angry splotch. The burning, the throbbing, the pus-filled bubbles, they never came. Instead, I felt clean, as if the skin had been peeled off my knee, dipped in acetone, and stretched back over the joint. It was the closest my body had ever come to feeling like a mind, I realized later, peeling soft brown bodies off the baking sheet and crushing them between my teeth. The rest of my Christmas vacation was marked by similarly sporadic brushes. In the bathroom (I nicked my shin while shaving), at the supermarket (I dropped a can of lentils on my little toe), in the backseat of my mother’s car (antecedent unclear). Four days before San Sebastián’s feast, I lay in bed with Martín’s old hoodie pulled up to my eyes, door closed, shutters tight. Hunger tapped against my sinuses, squeezing migraines against my sockets, as I pressed play on the third season of Fatmagul. Through the plywood wall behind my headboard, I could hear Lino on a group call with his tiresome friends, hammering out logistics for the SanSe weekend. As they squawked over each other—about rum and sneaky links and the possibility that Bad Bunny might show up—he sliced through stagnant air like a crisp January breeze. San Sebastián, I mean. The Roman-centurion-turned-Catholic-martyr I remembered from elementary school catechism. Like a figure hazily recalled from a dream, he came upon me as pure contingency: no face, no body, no discernible features at all, only sorrow and split flesh and arrow-wound. Dream logic suggested that I was looking at a near-naked man, though there was an undeniably feminine air to him as he stood slumped against a metal pole, nursing his wounds among an equally-faceless crowd of festival-goers. His body seemed to have been ruined far beyond what the thin streams of blood running from the holes in his chest would suggest. I attempted to reach into the image, feeling my fingers, my hand, my forearm, scrubbed clean as they crossed the dream threshold. I pulled behind me a thick rope of self meant to bind him to me. But, before I managed to tie it around his wrist, he disappeared, and I was left to swaddle my clean arm in my cotton bedsheets, afraid of the slightest contamination. I had not been properly religious in years. But I was certain that the apparition—whatever it was—had decided to cloak itself in distinctly Catholic garb for my sake. An attempt at communication. I pulled my old Lives of the Saints picture book from a box in the garage and flipped through its glossy, dog-eared pages. After a lifetime of addressing shaky Padrenuestros to the brown water stains on my ceiling, I had forgotten how many ways there were to attempt contact with the divine. Catherine of Siena had fasted to the point of near-starvation. Rita of Cascia had tended to a dead vine until it bore fruit. The children of Fátima had tied ropes around their waists before taking their sheep to graze in the Portuguese mountains, dedicating the subsequent rope burns to the souls in Purgatory. The prospect of taking my place among the elect, prickled with hunger, dirt embedded in my nailbeds, stomach skin slashed with puffy red bands, struck me as equally ridiculous and electrifying. I read on. In the mountains of Tepeyac, the Lady of Guadalupe enlisted Juan Diego to erect a shrine in her likeness through a meaty, urgent vision. My fellow Latin American witness gave me an idea. Perhaps, just in time for his feast day, I could attempt to immortalize San Sebastián’s essence in pixels. If all else failed, I would walk away with a new project for my photography portfolio. In order to fulfill my vision, however, I would need a model. My choice came down to half-convenience, half-divine inspiration. “I’m trying to capture some of the absurdity inherent to the framework of Catholic martyrology,” I pitched to Lino over dinner that night. “And what would be more absurd than having you, of all people, play a saint?” “Jesus, Cassia,” he replied through a mouthful of rice and kidney beans. “You need to get laid before you end up in a convent.” Of course, I never expected to stir Lino on the strength of my creative vision alone. No one—not even Sister Teresa, who had so inimitably dramatized the tragic disembowelment of little Santa María Goretti for our third grade catechism class—had ever managed to render Catholic imagery compelling to him. I, on the other hand, hadn’t even allowed Sister Teresa to finish her story before poking my head under my little desk, scrambling to make sure my intestines weren’t spilling out from under my school jumper. While I spent many a recess period playing missionaries with my friends, performing sleight-of-hand miracles to convince the preschool students of the one true God, Lino had kept to Pokémon cards and pick-up basketball. Lino bore no cross, a circumstance that rendered him fundamentally unable to understand anything like visions, heartbreak, or art. Eventually, however, we came to an agreement: Lino would pose for me only if I agreed to stay in Old San Juan into the night and drive him and his friends—who had been unable to decide upon a designated driver—home. ——— I had done my best to take advantage of the gentle morning light, making Lino scamper into every caved-out Catholic carcass I could find in the colonial city. I made him commune with the creepy plastic saints I found huddled in the San Juan Cathedral’s musty alcoves. I positioned him against the checkerboard floors and teal-armed chandeliers in the gorgeously gaudy lobby of El Convento, the seventeenth-century Spanish Carmelite convent turned four-star luxury boutique hotel. Lino had melted surprisingly well into his role, taking every opportunity to highlight the extent of his martyrdom: complaining about the sweat and mosquito bites accumulating near his wounds, hiding behind potted palms whenever he thought he saw a friend’s fuzzy outline in the distance. I felt refreshed, as if stagnant blood had been flushed from my circulatory system and replaced with something full-bodied and sweet. Lino was finally done drinking, and let his burdened hands hang by his sides once more. I worked on re-centering him among the fresh cast of characters that had populated the background. A young woman struggled to push a stroller over the bumpy cobblestone. A pair of middle-aged men in short-sleeved polo shirts stood in line for fried empanadillas, gesticulating with their hands as they argued. A little old woman browsed the wooden saints, squinting through pearl-strapped spectacles. She strayed closer and closer to the middle of the frame, nose pointed downward like a little dog sniffing out a blood trail, and finally came to rest right between Lino and I. I gave her a moment. Perhaps the image of my brother bleeding out by the side of the road had disoriented her. But a minute passed, and still she did not budge. In fact, the two of them seemed to be talking. Lino was shaking his head wildly, and I heard him utter the faint word “Vaseline.” I saw him grab the arrow nearest his heart by the shaft and begin to stick a finger in the gunk that I had stayed up all night perfecting. The old woman and I gasped in unison. She crossed herself hastily, her frail, manicured fingers only vaguely approaching the four points of the cross. I felt vaguely uncomfortable, watching the two of them through the viewfinder. Studying the old woman’s form, I noticed the characteristic line of an abdominal brace protruding just under her flowered blouse. Martín had worn a brace for a while, after his injury—a hernia, a tuft of organ pulled through his abdominal wall while he swung one weight plate too many over his head. Typical. When I called him the morning after Christmas, he had responded with a brusque grunt—I had interrupted his barbell squat reps, and some Lululemon-clad girl had taken the chance to park her pink Stanley cup on the Smith machine. Beyond the ceremonial “we need to talk about our relationship,” I had not rehearsed exactly what I would say to him, so, naturally, the interaction soon devolved into a screaming match. “I just don’t understand why I’m dating a fucking Pilgrim,” he shouted. “Everyone does it, I mean, look at your brother! Do you think he’s going to Hell?” “Maybe you should just fuck him, then!” I had screamed directly into the speaker before hanging up. I was excessively relieved when, after a minute or two, I saw the old woman’s form begin to stray out of frame, retreating back to the artisan’s stand. Her nose still pointed downward, but a tremor shook her every step. As I crossed the street, I noticed that Lino’s face had relaxed into a dull grin. The building behind him cast a yellow glow over his cheekbones. “Freaky shit,” he said as I approached, shaking his head gently, like he was waking up from a dream. I asked him what they had been talking about. “I think she thought I was a giant wood carving, at first. I said hi to her and she freaked out. I tried to tell her it was all fake, but...” He gestured to the now-smeared Vaseline on his chest. “Apparently, her only grandson died in Iraq a while ago, and San Sebastián was his baptismal saint. She’s been praying to him for years. Is he up there with you, was he in pain when he died, shit like that.” “What did you say to her?” “That I—you know, San Sebastián—didn’t actually die when he was shot with the arrows.” “That’s not even true,” I said, flicking my wrist up towards the mosaic. “San Sebastián is a martyr, remember?” “It is true,” he said matter-of-factly. “The next day, I went back to the Romans to tell them that my God had protected me. So, Diocletian bludgeoned me to death on the steps of the palace. Nasty. Blood everywhere. Brain spilling out of my ears...” “Why would you say that to her?” I interrupted, horrified. “Obviously I didn’t say all that to her. Just the thing about the arrows. You should have seen her after I told her that. Her eyes lit up, she stood up taller. She looked twenty years younger!” I tried to stall my creeping anguish by saying something about the questionable morality of misleading the elderly, but Lino was not listening to me. His head was turned towards the old woman who now rocked back and forth in prayer, facing the mosaic. The blood-spattered, blank-faced San Sebastián painted on the wall gazed up serenely. As if, puncture wounds aside, he had never been in any pain at all. I shifted my gaze back to Lino, only to realize that I did not recognize whoever it was that stood in front of me, licked by that strange yellow light that made his monstrously artificial wounds glisten. “How do you even know all that?” I asked, properly terrified. “About San Sebastián.” Lino shrugged the sun off his shoulders. “Catechism, I guess,” he said, waving his bacalaito like a giant deep-fried fan. The old woman looked down from the mosaic to see Lino take a giant bite of bacalaito and wash it down with a swig of sangria, like a third grader administering his own first communion. ——— I would have liked to study Lino further. But, by the time he had scrubbed off the Vaseline and pulled a t-shirt over his head, his cheeks had more or less dulled back to their usual bronze. Whatever glimpse of gold I caught thereafter was faint as an old bikini-strap tan line. The arrival of his friends—a rowdy gaggle of sweaty skater boys layering fake pearl necklaces over Thrasher t-shirts, prattling, pointing, pulling—rendered a difficult task impossible. As the sun made its way across the sky, stretching long shadows along San Sebastián street, I only managed to steal cursory glances at Lino: now singing along to a plena, now flirting with some girl, now becoming increasingly inebriated. By nighttime, I had more or less given up on verifying the miracle. We had wandered into one of the little bars on San Sebastián street, and a perturbing experience on the dance floor had pushed me back into an empty booth near the bathrooms. I gingerly placed my plastic cup of water on a little wooden table, taking note of its position among a dozen identical plastic cups, abandoned during different stages of consumption. I felt the steady blow of a metal fan tickle my right ear. I felt little splinters brush my bare thighs. I felt as if I were strapped to a dentist’s chair, revving with rabid desire to rip off my body as my jaw locked and metal clinked against teeth. Attempting a distraction, I took my camera out of its bag and, careful to avoid tapping it against any mysterious liquid, began clicking through my photos from the day. A close-up plastic Virgen de la Inmaculada, with storm-gray tears embedded like pearls in her cheeks. A stray cat’s tired gray face, nestled in Lino’s hand. The street corner. Lino stood with his offerings and a complex look on his face I could only describe as accidentally-holy. I examined him with curious jealousy. On one hand, Lino, with his horrifically faux stab wounds, clearly had no right to it. To any transfiguration, or even any flicker of spiritual clarity. On the other hand, I had the matter. San Sebastián, effortlessly at home among the festival crowd, so unbothered by his own impalement that he had gone back for more. I wondered whether this was a test, or a trick. I wondered whether my womanhood was the problem—Lino was, after all, my male-bodied analog. I wondered what I could possibly do about this unfortunate circumstance. “That one’s sick.” I whipped my head around to see Lino floating over my shoulder. “I look really good in it,” he added. “I mean, I look stupid, but I look pretty good.” He was crumpled over himself, sitting back on his heels, rocking gently. His breath stank of cheap beer. Dim phosphorescent light from a neon sign across the room made him glisten green. “Where are your friends?” I asked, annoyed. Lino shrugged. “I dunno. I kinda lost track of them.” He noticed the cup right next to me. “What are you drinking?” “Um, water? I’m your ride home, remember?” Lino looked disappointed. He slumped down next to me and took his vape out of his pocket. He didn’t bother offering it to me. “I just saw Marta. It put me in a bad mood.” Marta. Whoever that was. Despite the number of girls who had entered and exited his life—and I had endured the unimaginably disturbing, skin-crawling experience of hearing some of them through the plywood wall separating our bedrooms—I struggled to recall ever having seen Lino mourn the end of a relationship. I was moved by an irresistible compulsion to say something blunt and contentious. Perhaps being surrounded by drunk people had lowered my inhibitions through osmosis. Lino beat me to it. “What happened with the guy?” he asked. “What guy?” “The tall one with the glasses. The one you were dancing with earlier.” “Oh,” I said, cringing at the memory. Phantom fingers fastened themselves onto my shoulder with a squelch. A clammy, stilted swaying, dampened by sticky contact with strange bare arms. Little dirty crystals swimming around in my socks, sliding between my toes. Little dirty crystals sliding up and down our fingers as they intertwined. I hadn’t even realized that he wore glasses—it hadn’t occurred to me to look up at his face. “I wasn’t feeling him,” I said. Lino shrugged. “Just thought it might be nice for you. A rebound.” Martín and I dated for six months. Still, I managed to miss him only erratically, only in bursts. My first boyfriend—funny, good-looking, kind enough, obsessed with weight training, constantly attempting physical intimacy. For fear of offending him, I had endured six months of squirming at the feeling of his large hands grabbing my waist (I have a waist that can be grabbed), his musky breath brushing my nose (I have a nose that can be brushed), his—. “Are you even attracted to men?” Lino asked. “What?” I started, caught off guard. “What are you talking about?” “Nothing,” he said quickly, looking down at his feet. The seat pulsed with the bassline of some garbage reggaetón song, sending consistent massage gun vibrations up and down my thighs. Through the bar commotion, I could just hear the clank of the metal-wire fan as it whirred overhead. “I was just thinking about something Martín said to me once,” Lino continued. “Back when you guys were dating. About how you kissed him back like there were worms in his mouth, and it made him feel really insecure. He said you were either a closet lesbian, or you hated him.” “Why the fuck would he tell you that?” “I dunno,” Lino shrugged, defensive now. “Maybe cause I’m friendly. Maybe if you were friendlier, people would tell you things, too.” I remembered the old woman, hands clasped into a wrinkly spool, and felt hot. Lino exhaled a puff of sweet-smelling smoke, seemingly satisfied with his performance in this strange conversation. He reached across me for the water. And there it was. Bikini-strap tan line. Neon green, bright gold, neon green. He took an indulgent gulp, and immediately recoiled. “Fuck,” he said, sputtering. “This isn’t water.” “What do you mean?” Lino stuck his nose in the cup, squinted slightly, and took another tentative sip. “This is, like, straight liquor,” he concluded. “You must’ve grabbed the wrong cup,” I snapped, quickly flicking dewdrops from the inner corners of my eyes and making a show of checking my fingertips for smudged eyeliner. “Gross. That’s how people get mono, you know.” “There’s no mono in Puerto Rico,” he snapped back. “And it’s definitely yours. There’s a C on it.” Surely enough, a Sharpie semicircle wiggled along the cup’s ridges. I stared at it as if it had been etched in bile. Of course, the first letter of my name was far from unique. A Camila or a Carlos could have very easily abandoned a cup full of rum or vodka on this very table. But my mind turned to Lives of Saints , and to the fact that, though the attribution of one miracle makes one eligible for beatification, it takes two to be elevated to sainthood. Shortly after Lino and I received our first communion, I had dedicated all my eight-year-old ambitions to achieving just that. I had always assumed—I still assumed—that my natural resentment for my body, my stilted, rehearsed movements through an unbearably material world, predisposed me towards divinity. Or, at least, granted me access to a higher, more sophisticated plane of understanding, the domain of great artists and philosophers. But I had to admit there was something dazzling about Lino’s effortless, miraculous embodiment. Not Martín-like embodiment, not body-worship, but capricious, childlike naturality. Lino rested his weight on the wooden bench with full faith that something hard and tangible would be there to support him. He took another sip from the plastic cup, seemingly unaware of how the world stretched and stiffened around him. Safely bound, rather than held back by, the realm of matter. Like a great photographer, like an artist of pure composition. Without taking my eyes off of my brother, I fumbled in my lap for my camera, and switched on night mode. I lifted it up to my eyes, found my subject by the thin, glimmering line of his nose, and clicked. A flash of pure white light lit up the bar. Lino cursed, but I was no longer looking at him. I was looking at the screen, where, quick as a spasm, the unspeakable froze, before blinking back into darkness.

title: Silhouette E25: How to Tell a Story with Professors in the Yale English Department
author: Amiya Vashi
text: Host Amiya Vashi speaks to Yale English professors and students about what it means to tell a story through creative writing. This episode uncovers the way Yale preserves the tradition of storytelling through the popular class English 1020. As a literary essay class that centers on personal narrative, how does it strengthen students’ ability to write, communicate and engage? Guests: Jennifer Stock ’03, Jack Hanson GRD ’24, Kimberly Shirkhani, Miriam Syoum ’29, Courtney Tetteh-Martey ’29 Producers: Amiya Vashi ’29, Andre Fa’aoso ’27 Music: Blue Dot Sessions

title: Silhouette E24: On Seasonal Sadness with Psychiatrist Paul Desan
author: Esmeralda Vasquez-Fernandez
text: As Yale students hunkered down over the Fall and early Spring due to extreme cold and snow days, Desan delves into the science behind how weather changes and the winter darkness affects our moods. Desan also shares strategies for coping during these periods, and Vasquez-Fernandez speaks with students about how the weather has affected them and what they do to cope. Guests: Paul Desan MED ’93, Carissa Alcaraz ’29, Annasophia Castillo ’29, Gabriella Mwangi ’29, Sean Yu ’29 Producers: Esmeralda Vasquez-Fernandez ’28, Andre Fa’aoso ’27 Music: Blue Dot Sessions
